speeches.speech: Escalus.
lines: Escalus.

speeches.speech: My lord.
lines: My lord.

speeches.speech: Of government the properties to unfold, Would seem in me to affect speech and discourse; Since I am put to know that your own science Exceeds, in that, the lists of all advice My strength can give you: then no more remains, But that to your sufficiency [--] [--] as your Worth is able,] And let them work. The nature of our people, Our city's institutions, and the terms For common justice, you're as pregnant in As art and practise hath enriched any That we remember. There is our commission, From which we would not have you warp. Call hither, I say, bid come before us Angelo. [Exit an Attendant] What figure of us Think you he will bear? For you must know, we have with special soul Elected him our absence to supply, Lent him our terror, dress'd him with our love, And given his deputation all the organs Of our own power: what think you of it?
lines: Of government the properties to unfold,
Would seem in me to affect speech and discourse;
Since I am put to know that your own science
Exceeds, in that, the lists of all advice
My strength can give you: then no more remains,
But that to your sufficiency [--]
[--] as your Worth is able,]
And let them work. The nature of our people,
Our city's institutions, and the terms
For common justice, you're as pregnant in
As art and practise hath enriched any
That we remember. There is our commission,
From which we would not have you warp. Call hither,
I say, bid come before us Angelo.
[Exit an Attendant]
What figure of us Think you he will bear?
For you must know, we have with special soul
Elected him our absence to supply,
Lent him our terror, dress'd him with our love,
And given his deputation all the organs
Of our own power: what think you of it?

speeches.speech: If any in Vienna be of worth To undergo such ample grace and honour, It is Lord Angelo.
lines: If any in Vienna be of worth
To undergo such ample grace and honour,
It is Lord Angelo.

speeches.speech: Look where he comes.
lines: Look where he comes.

speeches.speech: Always obedient to your grace's will, I come to know your pleasure.
lines: Always obedient to your grace's will,
I come to know your pleasure.

speeches.speech: Angelo, There is a kind of character in thy life, That to the observer doth thy history Fully unfold. Thyself and thy belongings Are not thine own so proper as to waste Thyself upon thy virtues, they on thee. Heaven doth with us as we with torches do, Not light them for themselves; for if our virtues Did not go forth of us, 'twere all alike As if we had them not. Spirits are not finely touch'd But to fine issues, nor Nature never lends The smallest scruple of her excellence But, like a thrifty goddess, she determines Herself the glory of a creditor, Both thanks and use. But I do bend my speech To one that can my part in him advertise; Hold therefore, Angelo:-- In our remove be thou at full ourself; Mortality and mercy in Vienna Live in thy tongue and heart: old Escalus, Though first in question, is thy secondary. Take thy commission.
lines: Angelo,
There is a kind of character in thy life,
That to the observer doth thy history
Fully unfold. Thyself and thy belongings
Are not thine own so proper as to waste
Thyself upon thy virtues, they on thee.
Heaven doth with us as we with torches do,
Not light them for themselves; for if our virtues
Did not go forth of us, 'twere all alike
As if we had them not. Spirits are not finely touch'd
But to fine issues, nor Nature never lends
The smallest scruple of her excellence
But, like a thrifty goddess, she determines
Herself the glory of a creditor,
Both thanks and use. But I do bend my speech
To one that can my part in him advertise;
Hold therefore, Angelo:--
In our remove be thou at full ourself;
Mortality and mercy in Vienna
Live in thy tongue and heart: old Escalus,
Though first in question, is thy secondary.
Take thy commission.

speeches.speech: Now, good my lord, Let there be some more test made of my metal, Before so noble and so great a figure Be stamp'd upon it.
lines: Now, good my lord,
Let there be some more test made of my metal,
Before so noble and so great a figure
Be stamp'd upon it.

speeches.speech: No more evasion: We have with a leaven'd and prepared choice Proceeded to you; therefore take your honours. Our haste from hence is of so quick condition That it prefers itself and leaves unquestion'd Matters of needful value. We shall write to you, As time and our concernings shall importune, How it goes with us, and do look to know What doth befall you here. So, fare you well; To the hopeful execution do I leave you Of your commissions.
lines: No more evasion:
We have with a leaven'd and prepared choice
Proceeded to you; therefore take your honours.
Our haste from hence is of so quick condition
That it prefers itself and leaves unquestion'd
Matters of needful value. We shall write to you,
As time and our concernings shall importune,
How it goes with us, and do look to know
What doth befall you here. So, fare you well;
To the hopeful execution do I leave you
Of your commissions.

speeches.speech: Yet give leave, my lord, That we may bring you something on the way.
lines: Yet give leave, my lord,
That we may bring you something on the way.

speeches.speech: My haste may not admit it; Nor need you, on mine honour, have to do With any scruple; your scope is as mine own So to enforce or qualify the laws As to your soul seems good. Give me your hand: I'll privily away. I love the people, But do not like to stage me to their eyes: Through it do well, I do not relish well Their loud applause and Aves vehement; Nor do I think the man of safe discretion That does affect it. Once more, fare you well.
lines: My haste may not admit it;
Nor need you, on mine honour, have to do
With any scruple; your scope is as mine own
So to enforce or qualify the laws
As to your soul seems good. Give me your hand:
I'll privily away. I love the people,
But do not like to stage me to their eyes:
Through it do well, I do not relish well
Their loud applause and Aves vehement;
Nor do I think the man of safe discretion
That does affect it. Once more, fare you well.

speeches.speech: The heavens give safety to your purposes!
lines: The heavens give safety to your purposes!

speeches.speech: Lead forth and bring you back in happiness!
lines: Lead forth and bring you back in happiness!

speeches.speech: I thank you. Fare you well.
lines: I thank you. Fare you well.

speeches.speech: I shall desire you, sir, to give me leave To have free speech with you; and it concerns me To look into the bottom of my place: A power I have, but of what strength and nature I am not yet instructed.
lines: I shall desire you, sir, to give me leave
To have free speech with you; and it concerns me
To look into the bottom of my place:
A power I have, but of what strength and nature
I am not yet instructed.

speeches.speech: 'Tis so with me. Let us withdraw together, And we may soon our satisfaction have Touching that point.
lines: 'Tis so with me. Let us withdraw together,
And we may soon our satisfaction have
Touching that point.

speeches.speech: I'll wait upon your honour.
lines: I'll wait upon your honour.

speeches.speech: If the duke with the other dukes come not to composition with the King of Hungary, why then all the dukes fall upon the king.
lines: If the duke with the other dukes come not to
composition with the King of Hungary, why then all
the dukes fall upon the king.

speeches.speech: Heaven grant us its peace, but not the King of Hungary's!
lines: Heaven grant us its peace, but not the King of
Hungary's!

speeches.speech: Amen.
lines: Amen.

speeches.speech: Thou concludest like the sanctimonious pirate, that went to sea with the Ten Commandments, but scraped one out of the table.
lines: Thou concludest like the sanctimonious pirate, that
went to sea with the Ten Commandments, but scraped
one out of the table.

speeches.speech: 'Thou shalt not steal'?
lines: 'Thou shalt not steal'?

speeches.speech: Ay, that he razed.
lines: Ay, that he razed.

speeches.speech: Why, 'twas a commandment to command the captain and all the rest from their functions: they put forth to steal. There's not a soldier of us all, that, in the thanksgiving before meat, do relish the petition well that prays for peace.
lines: Why, 'twas a commandment to command the captain and
all the rest from their functions: they put forth
to steal. There's not a soldier of us all, that, in
the thanksgiving before meat, do relish the petition
well that prays for peace.

speeches.speech: I never heard any soldier dislike it.
lines: I never heard any soldier dislike it.

speeches.speech: I believe thee; for I think thou never wast where grace was said.
lines: I believe thee; for I think thou never wast where
grace was said.

speeches.speech: No? a dozen times at least.
lines: No? a dozen times at least.

speeches.speech: What, in metre?
lines: What, in metre?

speeches.speech: In any proportion or in any language.
lines: In any proportion or in any language.

speeches.speech: I think, or in any religion.
lines: I think, or in any religion.

speeches.speech: Ay, why not? Grace is grace, despite of all controversy: as, for example, thou thyself art a wicked villain, despite of all grace.
lines: Ay, why not? Grace is grace, despite of all
controversy: as, for example, thou thyself art a
wicked villain, despite of all grace.

speeches.speech: Well, there went but a pair of shears between us.
lines: Well, there went but a pair of shears between us.

speeches.speech: I grant; as there may between the lists and the velvet. Thou art the list.
lines: I grant; as there may between the lists and the
velvet. Thou art the list.

speeches.speech: And thou the velvet: thou art good velvet; thou'rt a three-piled piece, I warrant thee: I had as lief be a list of an English kersey as be piled, as thou art piled, for a French velvet. Do I speak feelingly now?
lines: And thou the velvet: thou art good velvet; thou'rt
a three-piled piece, I warrant thee: I had as lief
be a list of an English kersey as be piled, as thou
art piled, for a French velvet. Do I speak
feelingly now?

speeches.speech: I think thou dost; and, indeed, with most painful feeling of thy speech: I will, out of thine own confession, learn to begin thy health; but, whilst I live, forget to drink after thee.
lines: I think thou dost; and, indeed, with most painful
feeling of thy speech: I will, out of thine own
confession, learn to begin thy health; but, whilst I
live, forget to drink after thee.

speeches.speech: I think I have done myself wrong, have I not?
lines: I think I have done myself wrong, have I not?

speeches.speech: Yes, that thou hast, whether thou art tainted or free.
lines: Yes, that thou hast, whether thou art tainted or free.

speeches.speech: Behold, behold. where Madam Mitigation comes! I have purchased as many diseases under her roof as come to--
lines: Behold, behold. where Madam Mitigation comes! I
have purchased as many diseases under her roof as come to--

speeches.speech: To what, I pray?
lines: To what, I pray?

speeches.speech: Judge.
lines: Judge.

speeches.speech: To three thousand dolours a year.
lines: To three thousand dolours a year.

speeches.speech: Ay, and more.
lines: Ay, and more.

speeches.speech: A French crown more.
lines: A French crown more.

speeches.speech: Thou art always figuring diseases in me; but thou art full of error; I am sound.
lines: Thou art always figuring diseases in me; but thou
art full of error; I am sound.

speeches.speech: Nay, not as one would say, healthy; but so sound as things that are hollow: thy bones are hollow; impiety has made a feast of thee.
lines: Nay, not as one would say, healthy; but so sound as
things that are hollow: thy bones are hollow;
impiety has made a feast of thee.

speeches.speech: How now! which of your hips has the most profound sciatica?
lines: How now! which of your hips has the most profound sciatica?

speeches.speech: Well, well; there's one yonder arrested and carried to prison was worth five thousand of you all.
lines: Well, well; there's one yonder arrested and carried
to prison was worth five thousand of you all.

speeches.speech: Who's that, I pray thee?
lines: Who's that, I pray thee?

speeches.speech: Marry, sir, that's Claudio, Signior Claudio.
lines: Marry, sir, that's Claudio, Signior Claudio.

speeches.speech: Claudio to prison? 'tis not so.
lines: Claudio to prison? 'tis not so.

speeches.speech: Nay, but I know 'tis so: I saw him arrested, saw him carried away; and, which is more, within these three days his head to be chopped off.
lines: Nay, but I know 'tis so: I saw him arrested, saw
him carried away; and, which is more, within these
three days his head to be chopped off.

speeches.speech: But, after all this fooling, I would not have it so. Art thou sure of this?
lines: But, after all this fooling, I would not have it so.
Art thou sure of this?

speeches.speech: I am too sure of it: and it is for getting Madam Julietta with child.
lines: I am too sure of it: and it is for getting Madam
Julietta with child.

speeches.speech: Believe me, this may be: he promised to meet me two hours since, and he was ever precise in promise-keeping.
lines: Believe me, this may be: he promised to meet me two
hours since, and he was ever precise in
promise-keeping.

speeches.speech: Besides, you know, it draws something near to the speech we had to such a purpose.
lines: Besides, you know, it draws something near to the
speech we had to such a purpose.

speeches.speech: But, most of all, agreeing with the proclamation.
lines: But, most of all, agreeing with the proclamation.

speeches.speech: Away! let's go learn the truth of it.
lines: Away! let's go learn the truth of it.

speeches.speech: Thus, what with the war, what with the sweat, what with the gallows and what with poverty, I am custom-shrunk. [Enter POMPEY] How now! what's the news with you?
lines: Thus, what with the war, what with the sweat, what
with the gallows and what with poverty, I am
custom-shrunk.
[Enter POMPEY]
How now! what's the news with you?

speeches.speech: Yonder man is carried to prison.
lines: Yonder man is carried to prison.

speeches.speech: Well; what has he done?
lines: Well; what has he done?

speeches.speech: A woman.
lines: A woman.

speeches.speech: But what's his offence?
lines: But what's his offence?

speeches.speech: Groping for trouts in a peculiar river.
lines: Groping for trouts in a peculiar river.

speeches.speech: What, is there a maid with child by him?
lines: What, is there a maid with child by him?

speeches.speech: No, but there's a woman with maid by him. You have not heard of the proclamation, have you?
lines: No, but there's a woman with maid by him. You have
not heard of the proclamation, have you?

speeches.speech: What proclamation, man?
lines: What proclamation, man?

speeches.speech: All houses in the suburbs of Vienna must be plucked down.
lines: All houses in the suburbs of Vienna must be plucked down.

speeches.speech: And what shall become of those in the city?
lines: And what shall become of those in the city?

speeches.speech: They shall stand for seed: they had gone down too, but that a wise burgher put in for them.
lines: They shall stand for seed: they had gone down too,
but that a wise burgher put in for them.

speeches.speech: But shall all our houses of resort in the suburbs be pulled down?
lines: But shall all our houses of resort in the suburbs be
pulled down?

speeches.speech: To the ground, mistress.
lines: To the ground, mistress.

speeches.speech: Why, here's a change indeed in the commonwealth! What shall become of me?
lines: Why, here's a change indeed in the commonwealth!
What shall become of me?

speeches.speech: Come; fear you not: good counsellors lack no clients: though you change your place, you need not change your trade; I'll be your tapster still. Courage! there will be pity taken on you: you that have worn your eyes almost out in the service, you will be considered.
lines: Come; fear you not: good counsellors lack no
clients: though you change your place, you need not
change your trade; I'll be your tapster still.
Courage! there will be pity taken on you: you that
have worn your eyes almost out in the service, you
will be considered.

speeches.speech: What's to do here, Thomas tapster? let's withdraw.
lines: What's to do here, Thomas tapster? let's withdraw.

speeches.speech: Here comes Signior Claudio, led by the provost to prison; and there's Madam Juliet.
lines: Here comes Signior Claudio, led by the provost to
prison; and there's Madam Juliet.

speeches.speech: Fellow, why dost thou show me thus to the world? Bear me to prison, where I am committed.
lines: Fellow, why dost thou show me thus to the world?
Bear me to prison, where I am committed.

speeches.speech: I do it not in evil disposition, But from Lord Angelo by special charge.
lines: I do it not in evil disposition,
But from Lord Angelo by special charge.

speeches.speech: Thus can the demigod Authority Make us pay down for our offence by weight The words of heaven; on whom it will, it will; On whom it will not, so; yet still 'tis just.
lines: Thus can the demigod Authority
Make us pay down for our offence by weight
The words of heaven; on whom it will, it will;
On whom it will not, so; yet still 'tis just.

speeches.speech: Why, how now, Claudio! whence comes this restraint?
lines: Why, how now, Claudio! whence comes this restraint?

speeches.speech: From too much liberty, my Lucio, liberty: As surfeit is the father of much fast, So every scope by the immoderate use Turns to restraint. Our natures do pursue, Like rats that ravin down their proper bane, A thirsty evil; and when we drink we die.
lines: From too much liberty, my Lucio, liberty:
As surfeit is the father of much fast,
So every scope by the immoderate use
Turns to restraint. Our natures do pursue,
Like rats that ravin down their proper bane,
A thirsty evil; and when we drink we die.

speeches.speech: If could speak so wisely under an arrest, I would send for certain of my creditors: and yet, to say the truth, I had as lief have the foppery of freedom as the morality of imprisonment. What's thy offence, Claudio?
lines: If could speak so wisely under an arrest, I would
send for certain of my creditors: and yet, to say
the truth, I had as lief have the foppery of freedom
as the morality of imprisonment. What's thy
offence, Claudio?

speeches.speech: What but to speak of would offend again.
lines: What but to speak of would offend again.

speeches.speech: What, is't murder?
lines: What, is't murder?

speeches.speech: No.
lines: No.

speeches.speech: Lechery?
lines: Lechery?

speeches.speech: Call it so.
lines: Call it so.

speeches.speech: Away, sir! you must go.
lines: Away, sir! you must go.

speeches.speech: One word, good friend. Lucio, a word with you.
lines: One word, good friend. Lucio, a word with you.

speeches.speech: A hundred, if they'll do you any good. Is lechery so look'd after?
lines: A hundred, if they'll do you any good.
Is lechery so look'd after?

speeches.speech: Thus stands it with me: upon a true contract I got possession of Julietta's bed: You know the lady; she is fast my wife, Save that we do the denunciation lack Of outward order: this we came not to, Only for propagation of a dower Remaining in the coffer of her friends, From whom we thought it meet to hide our love Till time had made them for us. But it chances The stealth of our most mutual entertainment With character too gross is writ on Juliet.
lines: Thus stands it with me: upon a true contract
I got possession of Julietta's bed:
You know the lady; she is fast my wife,
Save that we do the denunciation lack
Of outward order: this we came not to,
Only for propagation of a dower
Remaining in the coffer of her friends,
From whom we thought it meet to hide our love
Till time had made them for us. But it chances
The stealth of our most mutual entertainment
With character too gross is writ on Juliet.

speeches.speech: With child, perhaps?
lines: With child, perhaps?

speeches.speech: Unhappily, even so. And the new deputy now for the duke-- Whether it be the fault and glimpse of newness, Or whether that the body public be A horse whereon the governor doth ride, Who, newly in the seat, that it may know He can command, lets it straight feel the spur; Whether the tyranny be in his place, Or in his emmence that fills it up, I stagger in:--but this new governor Awakes me all the enrolled penalties Which have, like unscour'd armour, hung by the wall So long that nineteen zodiacs have gone round And none of them been worn; and, for a name, Now puts the drowsy and neglected act Freshly on me: 'tis surely for a name.
lines: Unhappily, even so.
And the new deputy now for the duke--
Whether it be the fault and glimpse of newness,
Or whether that the body public be
A horse whereon the governor doth ride,
Who, newly in the seat, that it may know
He can command, lets it straight feel the spur;
Whether the tyranny be in his place,
Or in his emmence that fills it up,
I stagger in:--but this new governor
Awakes me all the enrolled penalties
Which have, like unscour'd armour, hung by the wall
So long that nineteen zodiacs have gone round
And none of them been worn; and, for a name,
Now puts the drowsy and neglected act
Freshly on me: 'tis surely for a name.

speeches.speech: I warrant it is: and thy head stands so tickle on thy shoulders that a milkmaid, if she be in love, may sigh it off. Send after the duke and appeal to him.
lines: I warrant it is: and thy head stands so tickle on
thy shoulders that a milkmaid, if she be in love,
may sigh it off. Send after the duke and appeal to
him.

speeches.speech: I have done so, but he's not to be found. I prithee, Lucio, do me this kind service: This day my sister should the cloister enter And there receive her approbation: Acquaint her with the danger of my state: Implore her, in my voice, that she make friends To the strict deputy; bid herself assay him: I have great hope in that; for in her youth There is a prone and speechless dialect, Such as move men; beside, she hath prosperous art When she will play with reason and discourse, And well she can persuade.
lines: I have done so, but he's not to be found.
I prithee, Lucio, do me this kind service:
This day my sister should the cloister enter
And there receive her approbation:
Acquaint her with the danger of my state:
Implore her, in my voice, that she make friends
To the strict deputy; bid herself assay him:
I have great hope in that; for in her youth
There is a prone and speechless dialect,
Such as move men; beside, she hath prosperous art
When she will play with reason and discourse,
And well she can persuade.

speeches.speech: I pray she may; as well for the encouragement of the like, which else would stand under grievous imposition, as for the enjoying of thy life, who I would be sorry should be thus foolishly lost at a game of tick-tack. I'll to her.
lines: I pray she may; as well for the encouragement of the
like, which else would stand under grievous
imposition, as for the enjoying of thy life, who I
would be sorry should be thus foolishly lost at a
game of tick-tack. I'll to her.

speeches.speech: I thank you, good friend Lucio.
lines: I thank you, good friend Lucio.

speeches.speech: Within two hours.
lines: Within two hours.

speeches.speech: Come, officer, away!
lines: Come, officer, away!

speeches.speech: No, holy father; throw away that thought; Believe not that the dribbling dart of love Can pierce a complete bosom. Why I desire thee To give me secret harbour, hath a purpose More grave and wrinkled than the aims and ends Of burning youth.
lines: No, holy father; throw away that thought;
Believe not that the dribbling dart of love
Can pierce a complete bosom. Why I desire thee
To give me secret harbour, hath a purpose
More grave and wrinkled than the aims and ends
Of burning youth.

speeches.speech: May your grace speak of it?
lines: May your grace speak of it?

speeches.speech: My holy sir, none better knows than you How I have ever loved the life removed And held in idle price to haunt assemblies Where youth, and cost, and witless bravery keeps. I have deliver'd to Lord Angelo, A man of stricture and firm abstinence, My absolute power and place here in Vienna, And he supposes me travell'd to Poland; For so I have strew'd it in the common ear, And so it is received. Now, pious sir, You will demand of me why I do this?
lines: My holy sir, none better knows than you
How I have ever loved the life removed
And held in idle price to haunt assemblies
Where youth, and cost, and witless bravery keeps.
I have deliver'd to Lord Angelo,
A man of stricture and firm abstinence,
My absolute power and place here in Vienna,
And he supposes me travell'd to Poland;
For so I have strew'd it in the common ear,
And so it is received. Now, pious sir,
You will demand of me why I do this?

speeches.speech: Gladly, my lord.
lines: Gladly, my lord.

speeches.speech: We have strict statutes and most biting laws. The needful bits and curbs to headstrong weeds, Which for this nineteen years we have let slip; Even like an o'ergrown lion in a cave, That goes not out to prey. Now, as fond fathers, Having bound up the threatening twigs of birch, Only to stick it in their children's sight For terror, not to use, in time the rod Becomes more mock'd than fear'd; so our decrees, Dead to infliction, to themselves are dead; And liberty plucks justice by the nose; The baby beats the nurse, and quite athwart Goes all decorum.
lines: We have strict statutes and most biting laws.
The needful bits and curbs to headstrong weeds,
Which for this nineteen years we have let slip;
Even like an o'ergrown lion in a cave,
That goes not out to prey. Now, as fond fathers,
Having bound up the threatening twigs of birch,
Only to stick it in their children's sight
For terror, not to use, in time the rod
Becomes more mock'd than fear'd; so our decrees,
Dead to infliction, to themselves are dead;
And liberty plucks justice by the nose;
The baby beats the nurse, and quite athwart
Goes all decorum.

speeches.speech: It rested in your grace To unloose this tied-up justice when you pleased: And it in you more dreadful would have seem'd Than in Lord Angelo.
lines: It rested in your grace
To unloose this tied-up justice when you pleased:
And it in you more dreadful would have seem'd
Than in Lord Angelo.

speeches.speech: I do fear, too dreadful: Sith 'twas my fault to give the people scope, 'Twould be my tyranny to strike and gall them For what I bid them do: for we bid this be done, When evil deeds have their permissive pass And not the punishment. Therefore indeed, my father, I have on Angelo imposed the office; Who may, in the ambush of my name, strike home, And yet my nature never in the fight To do in slander. And to behold his sway, I will, as 'twere a brother of your order, Visit both prince and people: therefore, I prithee, Supply me with the habit and instruct me How I may formally in person bear me Like a true friar. More reasons for this action At our more leisure shall I render you; Only, this one: Lord Angelo is precise; Stands at a guard with envy; scarce confesses That his blood flows, or that his appetite Is more to bread than stone: hence shall we see, If power change purpose, what our seemers be.
lines: I do fear, too dreadful:
Sith 'twas my fault to give the people scope,
'Twould be my tyranny to strike and gall them
For what I bid them do: for we bid this be done,
When evil deeds have their permissive pass
And not the punishment. Therefore indeed, my father,
I have on Angelo imposed the office;
Who may, in the ambush of my name, strike home,
And yet my nature never in the fight
To do in slander. And to behold his sway,
I will, as 'twere a brother of your order,
Visit both prince and people: therefore, I prithee,
Supply me with the habit and instruct me
How I may formally in person bear me
Like a true friar. More reasons for this action
At our more leisure shall I render you;
Only, this one: Lord Angelo is precise;
Stands at a guard with envy; scarce confesses
That his blood flows, or that his appetite
Is more to bread than stone: hence shall we see,
If power change purpose, what our seemers be.

speeches.speech: And have you nuns no farther privileges?
lines: And have you nuns no farther privileges?

speeches.speech: Are not these large enough?
lines: Are not these large enough?

speeches.speech: Yes, truly; I speak not as desiring more; But rather wishing a more strict restraint Upon the sisterhood, the votarists of Saint Clare.
lines: Yes, truly; I speak not as desiring more;
But rather wishing a more strict restraint
Upon the sisterhood, the votarists of Saint Clare.

speeches.speech: [Within] Ho! Peace be in this place!
lines: [Within] Ho! Peace be in this place!

speeches.speech: Who's that which calls?
lines: Who's that which calls?

speeches.speech: It is a man's voice. Gentle Isabella, Turn you the key, and know his business of him; You may, I may not; you are yet unsworn. When you have vow'd, you must not speak with men But in the presence of the prioress: Then, if you speak, you must not show your face, Or, if you show your face, you must not speak. He calls again; I pray you, answer him.
lines: It is a man's voice. Gentle Isabella,
Turn you the key, and know his business of him;
You may, I may not; you are yet unsworn.
When you have vow'd, you must not speak with men
But in the presence of the prioress:
Then, if you speak, you must not show your face,
Or, if you show your face, you must not speak.
He calls again; I pray you, answer him.

speeches.speech: Peace and prosperity! Who is't that calls
lines: Peace and prosperity! Who is't that calls

speeches.speech: Hail, virgin, if you be, as those cheek-roses Proclaim you are no less! Can you so stead me As bring me to the sight of Isabella, A novice of this place and the fair sister To her unhappy brother Claudio?
lines: Hail, virgin, if you be, as those cheek-roses
Proclaim you are no less! Can you so stead me
As bring me to the sight of Isabella,
A novice of this place and the fair sister
To her unhappy brother Claudio?

speeches.speech: Why 'her unhappy brother'? let me ask, The rather for I now must make you know I am that Isabella and his sister.
lines: Why 'her unhappy brother'? let me ask,
The rather for I now must make you know
I am that Isabella and his sister.

speeches.speech: Gentle and fair, your brother kindly greets you: Not to be weary with you, he's in prison.
lines: Gentle and fair, your brother kindly greets you:
Not to be weary with you, he's in prison.

speeches.speech: Woe me! for what?
lines: Woe me! for what?

speeches.speech: For that which, if myself might be his judge, He should receive his punishment in thanks: He hath got his friend with child.
lines: For that which, if myself might be his judge,
He should receive his punishment in thanks:
He hath got his friend with child.

speeches.speech: Sir, make me not your story.
lines: Sir, make me not your story.

speeches.speech: It is true. I would not--though 'tis my familiar sin With maids to seem the lapwing and to jest, Tongue far from heart--play with all virgins so: I hold you as a thing ensky'd and sainted. By your renouncement an immortal spirit, And to be talk'd with in sincerity, As with a saint.
lines: It is true.
I would not--though 'tis my familiar sin
With maids to seem the lapwing and to jest,
Tongue far from heart--play with all virgins so:
I hold you as a thing ensky'd and sainted.
By your renouncement an immortal spirit,
And to be talk'd with in sincerity,
As with a saint.

speeches.speech: You do blaspheme the good in mocking me.
lines: You do blaspheme the good in mocking me.

speeches.speech: Do not believe it. Fewness and truth, 'tis thus: Your brother and his lover have embraced: As those that feed grow full, as blossoming time That from the seedness the bare fallow brings To teeming foison, even so her plenteous womb Expresseth his full tilth and husbandry.
lines: Do not believe it. Fewness and truth, 'tis thus:
Your brother and his lover have embraced:
As those that feed grow full, as blossoming time
That from the seedness the bare fallow brings
To teeming foison, even so her plenteous womb
Expresseth his full tilth and husbandry.

speeches.speech: Some one with child by him? My cousin Juliet?
lines: Some one with child by him? My cousin Juliet?

speeches.speech: Is she your cousin?
lines: Is she your cousin?

speeches.speech: Adoptedly; as school-maids change their names By vain though apt affection.
lines: Adoptedly; as school-maids change their names
By vain though apt affection.

speeches.speech: She it is.
lines: She it is.

speeches.speech: O, let him marry her.
lines: O, let him marry her.

speeches.speech: This is the point. The duke is very strangely gone from hence; Bore many gentlemen, myself being one, In hand and hope of action: but we do learn By those that know the very nerves of state, His givings-out were of an infinite distance From his true-meant design. Upon his place, And with full line of his authority, Governs Lord Angelo; a man whose blood Is very snow-broth; one who never feels The wanton stings and motions of the sense, But doth rebate and blunt his natural edge With profits of the mind, study and fast. He--to give fear to use and liberty, Which have for long run by the hideous law, As mice by lions--hath pick'd out an act, Under whose heavy sense your brother's life Falls into forfeit: he arrests him on it; And follows close the rigour of the statute, To make him an example. All hope is gone, Unless you have the grace by your fair prayer To soften Angelo: and that's my pith of business 'Twixt you and your poor brother.
lines: This is the point.
The duke is very strangely gone from hence;
Bore many gentlemen, myself being one,
In hand and hope of action: but we do learn
By those that know the very nerves of state,
His givings-out were of an infinite distance
From his true-meant design. Upon his place,
And with full line of his authority,
Governs Lord Angelo; a man whose blood
Is very snow-broth; one who never feels
The wanton stings and motions of the sense,
But doth rebate and blunt his natural edge
With profits of the mind, study and fast.
He--to give fear to use and liberty,
Which have for long run by the hideous law,
As mice by lions--hath pick'd out an act,
Under whose heavy sense your brother's life
Falls into forfeit: he arrests him on it;
And follows close the rigour of the statute,
To make him an example. All hope is gone,
Unless you have the grace by your fair prayer
To soften Angelo: and that's my pith of business
'Twixt you and your poor brother.

speeches.speech: Doth he so seek his life?
lines: Doth he so seek his life?

speeches.speech: Has censured him Already; and, as I hear, the provost hath A warrant for his execution.
lines: Has censured him
Already; and, as I hear, the provost hath
A warrant for his execution.

speeches.speech: Alas! what poor ability's in me To do him good?
lines: Alas! what poor ability's in me
To do him good?

speeches.speech: Assay the power you have.
lines: Assay the power you have.

speeches.speech: My power? Alas, I doubt--
lines: My power? Alas, I doubt--

speeches.speech: Our doubts are traitors And make us lose the good we oft might win By fearing to attempt. Go to Lord Angelo, And let him learn to know, when maidens sue, Men give like gods; but when they weep and kneel, All their petitions are as freely theirs As they themselves would owe them.
lines: Our doubts are traitors
And make us lose the good we oft might win
By fearing to attempt. Go to Lord Angelo,
And let him learn to know, when maidens sue,
Men give like gods; but when they weep and kneel,
All their petitions are as freely theirs
As they themselves would owe them.

speeches.speech: I'll see what I can do.
lines: I'll see what I can do.

speeches.speech: But speedily.
lines: But speedily.

speeches.speech: I will about it straight; No longer staying but to give the mother Notice of my affair. I humbly thank you: Commend me to my brother: soon at night I'll send him certain word of my success.
lines: I will about it straight;
No longer staying but to give the mother
Notice of my affair. I humbly thank you:
Commend me to my brother: soon at night
I'll send him certain word of my success.

speeches.speech: I take my leave of you.
lines: I take my leave of you.

speeches.speech: Good sir, adieu.
lines: Good sir, adieu.

speeches.speech: We must not make a scarecrow of the law, Setting it up to fear the birds of prey, And let it keep one shape, till custom make it Their perch and not their terror.
lines: We must not make a scarecrow of the law,
Setting it up to fear the birds of prey,
And let it keep one shape, till custom make it
Their perch and not their terror.

speeches.speech: Ay, but yet Let us be keen, and rather cut a little, Than fall, and bruise to death. Alas, this gentleman Whom I would save, had a most noble father! Let but your honour know, Whom I believe to be most strait in virtue, That, in the working of your own affections, Had time cohered with place or place with wishing, Or that the resolute acting of your blood Could have attain'd the effect of your own purpose, Whether you had not sometime in your life Err'd in this point which now you censure him, And pull'd the law upon you.
lines: Ay, but yet
Let us be keen, and rather cut a little,
Than fall, and bruise to death. Alas, this gentleman
Whom I would save, had a most noble father!
Let but your honour know,
Whom I believe to be most strait in virtue,
That, in the working of your own affections,
Had time cohered with place or place with wishing,
Or that the resolute acting of your blood
Could have attain'd the effect of your own purpose,
Whether you had not sometime in your life
Err'd in this point which now you censure him,
And pull'd the law upon you.

speeches.speech: 'Tis one thing to be tempted, Escalus, Another thing to fall. I not deny, The jury, passing on the prisoner's life, May in the sworn twelve have a thief or two Guiltier than him they try. What's open made to justice, That justice seizes: what know the laws That thieves do pass on thieves? 'Tis very pregnant, The jewel that we find, we stoop and take't Because we see it; but what we do not see We tread upon, and never think of it. You may not so extenuate his offence For I have had such faults; but rather tell me, When I, that censure him, do so offend, Let mine own judgment pattern out my death, And nothing come in partial. Sir, he must die.
lines: 'Tis one thing to be tempted, Escalus,
Another thing to fall. I not deny,
The jury, passing on the prisoner's life,
May in the sworn twelve have a thief or two
Guiltier than him they try. What's open made to justice,
That justice seizes: what know the laws
That thieves do pass on thieves? 'Tis very pregnant,
The jewel that we find, we stoop and take't
Because we see it; but what we do not see
We tread upon, and never think of it.
You may not so extenuate his offence
For I have had such faults; but rather tell me,
When I, that censure him, do so offend,
Let mine own judgment pattern out my death,
And nothing come in partial. Sir, he must die.

speeches.speech: Be it as your wisdom will.
lines: Be it as your wisdom will.

speeches.speech: Where is the provost?
lines: Where is the provost?

speeches.speech: Here, if it like your honour.
lines: Here, if it like your honour.

speeches.speech: See that Claudio Be executed by nine to-morrow morning: Bring him his confessor, let him be prepared; For that's the utmost of his pilgrimage.
lines: See that Claudio
Be executed by nine to-morrow morning:
Bring him his confessor, let him be prepared;
For that's the utmost of his pilgrimage.

speeches.speech: [Aside] Well, heaven forgive him! and forgive us all! Some rise by sin, and some by virtue fall: Some run from brakes of ice, and answer none: And some condemned for a fault alone.
lines: [Aside] Well, heaven forgive him! and forgive us all!
Some rise by sin, and some by virtue fall:
Some run from brakes of ice, and answer none:
And some condemned for a fault alone.

speeches.speech: Come, bring them away: if these be good people in a commonweal that do nothing but use their abuses in common houses, I know no law: bring them away.
lines: Come, bring them away: if these be good people in
a commonweal that do nothing but use their abuses in
common houses, I know no law: bring them away.

speeches.speech: How now, sir! What's your name? and what's the matter?
lines: How now, sir! What's your name? and what's the matter?

speeches.speech: If it Please your honour, I am the poor duke's constable, and my name is Elbow: I do lean upon justice, sir, and do bring in here before your good honour two notorious benefactors.
lines: If it Please your honour, I am the poor duke's
constable, and my name is Elbow: I do lean upon
justice, sir, and do bring in here before your good
honour two notorious benefactors.

speeches.speech: Benefactors? Well; what benefactors are they? are they not malefactors?
lines: Benefactors? Well; what benefactors are they? are
they not malefactors?

speeches.speech: If it? please your honour, I know not well what they are: but precise villains they are, that I am sure of; and void of all profanation in the world that good Christians ought to have.
lines: If it? please your honour, I know not well what they
are: but precise villains they are, that I am sure
of; and void of all profanation in the world that
good Christians ought to have.

speeches.speech: This comes off well; here's a wise officer.
lines: This comes off well; here's a wise officer.

speeches.speech: Go to: what quality are they of? Elbow is your name? why dost thou not speak, Elbow?
lines: Go to: what quality are they of? Elbow is your
name? why dost thou not speak, Elbow?

speeches.speech: He cannot, sir; he's out at elbow.
lines: He cannot, sir; he's out at elbow.

speeches.speech: What are you, sir?
lines: What are you, sir?

speeches.speech: He, sir! a tapster, sir; parcel-bawd; one that serves a bad woman; whose house, sir, was, as they say, plucked down in the suburbs; and now she professes a hot-house, which, I think, is a very ill house too.
lines: He, sir! a tapster, sir; parcel-bawd; one that
serves a bad woman; whose house, sir, was, as they
say, plucked down in the suburbs; and now she
professes a hot-house, which, I think, is a very ill house too.

speeches.speech: How know you that?
lines: How know you that?

speeches.speech: My wife, sir, whom I detest before heaven and your honour,--
lines: My wife, sir, whom I detest before heaven and your honour,--

speeches.speech: How? thy wife?
lines: How? thy wife?

speeches.speech: Ay, sir; whom, I thank heaven, is an honest woman,--
lines: Ay, sir; whom, I thank heaven, is an honest woman,--

speeches.speech: Dost thou detest her therefore?
lines: Dost thou detest her therefore?

speeches.speech: I say, sir, I will detest myself also, as well as she, that this house, if it be not a bawd's house, it is pity of her life, for it is a naughty house.
lines: I say, sir, I will detest myself also, as well as
she, that this house, if it be not a bawd's house,
it is pity of her life, for it is a naughty house.

speeches.speech: How dost thou know that, constable?
lines: How dost thou know that, constable?

speeches.speech: Marry, sir, by my wife; who, if she had been a woman cardinally given, might have been accused in fornication, adultery, and all uncleanliness there.
lines: Marry, sir, by my wife; who, if she had been a woman
cardinally given, might have been accused in
fornication, adultery, and all uncleanliness there.

speeches.speech: By the woman's means?
lines: By the woman's means?

speeches.speech: Ay, sir, by Mistress Overdone's means: but as she spit in his face, so she defied him.
lines: Ay, sir, by Mistress Overdone's means: but as she
spit in his face, so she defied him.

speeches.speech: Sir, if it please your honour, this is not so.
lines: Sir, if it please your honour, this is not so.

speeches.speech: Prove it before these varlets here, thou honourable man; prove it.
lines: Prove it before these varlets here, thou honourable
man; prove it.

speeches.speech: Do you hear how he misplaces?
lines: Do you hear how he misplaces?

speeches.speech: Sir, she came in great with child; and longing, saving your honour's reverence, for stewed prunes; sir, we had but two in the house, which at that very distant time stood, as it were, in a fruit-dish, a dish of some three-pence; your honours have seen such dishes; they are not China dishes, but very good dishes,--
lines: Sir, she came in great with child; and longing,
saving your honour's reverence, for stewed prunes;
sir, we had but two in the house, which at that very
distant time stood, as it were, in a fruit-dish, a
dish of some three-pence; your honours have seen
such dishes; they are not China dishes, but very
good dishes,--

speeches.speech: Go to, go to: no matter for the dish, sir.
lines: Go to, go to: no matter for the dish, sir.

speeches.speech: No, indeed, sir, not of a pin; you are therein in the right: but to the point. As I say, this Mistress Elbow, being, as I say, with child, and being great-bellied, and longing, as I said, for prunes; and having but two in the dish, as I said, Master Froth here, this very man, having eaten the rest, as I said, and, as I say, paying for them very honestly; for, as you know, Master Froth, I could not give you three-pence again.
lines: No, indeed, sir, not of a pin; you are therein in
the right: but to the point. As I say, this
Mistress Elbow, being, as I say, with child, and
being great-bellied, and longing, as I said, for
prunes; and having but two in the dish, as I said,
Master Froth here, this very man, having eaten the
rest, as I said, and, as I say, paying for them very
honestly; for, as you know, Master Froth, I could
not give you three-pence again.

speeches.speech: No, indeed.
lines: No, indeed.

speeches.speech: Very well: you being then, if you be remembered, cracking the stones of the foresaid prunes,--
lines: Very well: you being then, if you be remembered,
cracking the stones of the foresaid prunes,--

speeches.speech: Ay, so I did indeed.
lines: Ay, so I did indeed.

speeches.speech: Why, very well; I telling you then, if you be remembered, that such a one and such a one were past cure of the thing you wot of, unless they kept very good diet, as I told you,--
lines: Why, very well; I telling you then, if you be
remembered, that such a one and such a one were past
cure of the thing you wot of, unless they kept very
good diet, as I told you,--

speeches.speech: All this is true.
lines: All this is true.

speeches.speech: Why, very well, then,--
lines: Why, very well, then,--

speeches.speech: Come, you are a tedious fool: to the purpose. What was done to Elbow's wife, that he hath cause to complain of? Come me to what was done to her.
lines: Come, you are a tedious fool: to the purpose. What
was done to Elbow's wife, that he hath cause to
complain of? Come me to what was done to her.

speeches.speech: Sir, your honour cannot come to that yet.
lines: Sir, your honour cannot come to that yet.

speeches.speech: No, sir, nor I mean it not.
lines: No, sir, nor I mean it not.

speeches.speech: Sir, but you shall come to it, by your honour's leave. And, I beseech you, look into Master Froth here, sir; a man of four-score pound a year; whose father died at Hallowmas: was't not at Hallowmas, Master Froth?
lines: Sir, but you shall come to it, by your honour's
leave. And, I beseech you, look into Master Froth
here, sir; a man of four-score pound a year; whose
father died at Hallowmas: was't not at Hallowmas,
Master Froth?

speeches.speech: All-hallond eve.
lines: All-hallond eve.

speeches.speech: Why, very well; I hope here be truths. He, sir, sitting, as I say, in a lower chair, sir; 'twas in the Bunch of Grapes, where indeed you have a delight to sit, have you not?
lines: Why, very well; I hope here be truths. He, sir,
sitting, as I say, in a lower chair, sir; 'twas in
the Bunch of Grapes, where indeed you have a delight
to sit, have you not?

speeches.speech: I have so; because it is an open room and good for winter.
lines: I have so; because it is an open room and good for winter.

speeches.speech: Why, very well, then; I hope here be truths.
lines: Why, very well, then; I hope here be truths.

speeches.speech: This will last out a night in Russia, When nights are longest there: I'll take my leave. And leave you to the hearing of the cause; Hoping you'll find good cause to whip them all.
lines: This will last out a night in Russia,
When nights are longest there: I'll take my leave.
And leave you to the hearing of the cause;
Hoping you'll find good cause to whip them all.

speeches.speech: I think no less. Good morrow to your lordship. [Exit ANGELO] Now, sir, come on: what was done to Elbow's wife, once more?
lines: I think no less. Good morrow to your lordship.
[Exit ANGELO]
Now, sir, come on: what was done to Elbow's wife, once more?

speeches.speech: Once, sir? there was nothing done to her once.
lines: Once, sir? there was nothing done to her once.

speeches.speech: I beseech you, sir, ask him what this man did to my wife.
lines: I beseech you, sir, ask him what this man did to my wife.

speeches.speech: I beseech your honour, ask me.
lines: I beseech your honour, ask me.

speeches.speech: Well, sir; what did this gentleman to her?
lines: Well, sir; what did this gentleman to her?

speeches.speech: I beseech you, sir, look in this gentleman's face. Good Master Froth, look upon his honour; 'tis for a good purpose. Doth your honour mark his face?
lines: I beseech you, sir, look in this gentleman's face.
Good Master Froth, look upon his honour; 'tis for a
good purpose. Doth your honour mark his face?

speeches.speech: Ay, sir, very well.
lines: Ay, sir, very well.

speeches.speech: Nay; I beseech you, mark it well.
lines: Nay; I beseech you, mark it well.

speeches.speech: Well, I do so.
lines: Well, I do so.

speeches.speech: Doth your honour see any harm in his face?
lines: Doth your honour see any harm in his face?

speeches.speech: Why, no.
lines: Why, no.

speeches.speech: I'll be supposed upon a book, his face is the worst thing about him. Good, then; if his face be the worst thing about him, how could Master Froth do the constable's wife any harm? I would know that of your honour.
lines: I'll be supposed upon a book, his face is the worst
thing about him. Good, then; if his face be the
worst thing about him, how could Master Froth do the
constable's wife any harm? I would know that of
your honour.

speeches.speech: He's in the right. Constable, what say you to it?
lines: He's in the right. Constable, what say you to it?

speeches.speech: First, an it like you, the house is a respected house; next, this is a respected fellow; and his mistress is a respected woman.
lines: First, an it like you, the house is a respected
house; next, this is a respected fellow; and his
mistress is a respected woman.

speeches.speech: By this hand, sir, his wife is a more respected person than any of us all.
lines: By this hand, sir, his wife is a more respected
person than any of us all.

speeches.speech: Varlet, thou liest; thou liest, wicked varlet! the time has yet to come that she was ever respected with man, woman, or child.
lines: Varlet, thou liest; thou liest, wicked varlet! the
time has yet to come that she was ever respected
with man, woman, or child.

speeches.speech: Sir, she was respected with him before he married with her.
lines: Sir, she was respected with him before he married with her.

speeches.speech: Which is the wiser here? Justice or Iniquity? Is this true?
lines: Which is the wiser here? Justice or Iniquity? Is
this true?

speeches.speech: O thou caitiff! O thou varlet! O thou wicked Hannibal! I respected with her before I was married to her! If ever I was respected with her, or she with me, let not your worship think me the poor duke's officer. Prove this, thou wicked Hannibal, or I'll have mine action of battery on thee.
lines: O thou caitiff! O thou varlet! O thou wicked
Hannibal! I respected with her before I was married
to her! If ever I was respected with her, or she
with me, let not your worship think me the poor
duke's officer. Prove this, thou wicked Hannibal, or
I'll have mine action of battery on thee.

speeches.speech: If he took you a box o' the ear, you might have your action of slander too.
lines: If he took you a box o' the ear, you might have your
action of slander too.

speeches.speech: Marry, I thank your good worship for it. What is't your worship's pleasure I shall do with this wicked caitiff?
lines: Marry, I thank your good worship for it. What is't
your worship's pleasure I shall do with this wicked caitiff?

speeches.speech: Truly, officer, because he hath some offences in him that thou wouldst discover if thou couldst, let him continue in his courses till thou knowest what they are.
lines: Truly, officer, because he hath some offences in him
that thou wouldst discover if thou couldst, let him
continue in his courses till thou knowest what they
are.

speeches.speech: Marry, I thank your worship for it. Thou seest, thou wicked varlet, now, what's come upon thee: thou art to continue now, thou varlet; thou art to continue.
lines: Marry, I thank your worship for it. Thou seest, thou
wicked varlet, now, what's come upon thee: thou art
to continue now, thou varlet; thou art to continue.

speeches.speech: Where were you born, friend?
lines: Where were you born, friend?

speeches.speech: Here in Vienna, sir.
lines: Here in Vienna, sir.

speeches.speech: Are you of fourscore pounds a year?
lines: Are you of fourscore pounds a year?

speeches.speech: Yes, an't please you, sir.
lines: Yes, an't please you, sir.

speeches.speech: So. What trade are you of, sir?
lines: So. What trade are you of, sir?

speeches.speech: Tapster; a poor widow's tapster.
lines: Tapster; a poor widow's tapster.

speeches.speech: Your mistress' name?
lines: Your mistress' name?

speeches.speech: Mistress Overdone.
lines: Mistress Overdone.

speeches.speech: Hath she had any more than one husband?
lines: Hath she had any more than one husband?

speeches.speech: Nine, sir; Overdone by the last.
lines: Nine, sir; Overdone by the last.

speeches.speech: Nine! Come hither to me, Master Froth. Master Froth, I would not have you acquainted with tapsters: they will draw you, Master Froth, and you will hang them. Get you gone, and let me hear no more of you.
lines: Nine! Come hither to me, Master Froth. Master
Froth, I would not have you acquainted with
tapsters: they will draw you, Master Froth, and you
will hang them. Get you gone, and let me hear no
more of you.

speeches.speech: I thank your worship. For mine own part, I never come into any room in a tap-house, but I am drawn in.
lines: I thank your worship. For mine own part, I never
come into any room in a tap-house, but I am drawn
in.

speeches.speech: Well, no more of it, Master Froth: farewell. [Exit FROTH] Come you hither to me, Master tapster. What's your name, Master tapster?
lines: Well, no more of it, Master Froth: farewell.
[Exit FROTH]
Come you hither to me, Master tapster. What's your
name, Master tapster?

speeches.speech: Pompey.
lines: Pompey.

speeches.speech: What else?
lines: What else?

speeches.speech: Bum, sir.
lines: Bum, sir.

speeches.speech: Troth, and your bum is the greatest thing about you; so that in the beastliest sense you are Pompey the Great. Pompey, you are partly a bawd, Pompey, howsoever you colour it in being a tapster, are you not? come, tell me true: it shall be the better for you.
lines: Troth, and your bum is the greatest thing about you;
so that in the beastliest sense you are Pompey the
Great. Pompey, you are partly a bawd, Pompey,
howsoever you colour it in being a tapster, are you
not? come, tell me true: it shall be the better for you.

speeches.speech: Truly, sir, I am a poor fellow that would live.
lines: Truly, sir, I am a poor fellow that would live.

speeches.speech: How would you live, Pompey? by being a bawd? What do you think of the trade, Pompey? is it a lawful trade?
lines: How would you live, Pompey? by being a bawd? What
do you think of the trade, Pompey? is it a lawful trade?

speeches.speech: If the law would allow it, sir.
lines: If the law would allow it, sir.

speeches.speech: But the law will not allow it, Pompey; nor it shall not be allowed in Vienna.
lines: But the law will not allow it, Pompey; nor it shall
not be allowed in Vienna.

speeches.speech: Does your worship mean to geld and splay all the youth of the city?
lines: Does your worship mean to geld and splay all the
youth of the city?

speeches.speech: No, Pompey.
lines: No, Pompey.

speeches.speech: Truly, sir, in my poor opinion, they will to't then. If your worship will take order for the drabs and the knaves, you need not to fear the bawds.
lines: Truly, sir, in my poor opinion, they will to't then.
If your worship will take order for the drabs and
the knaves, you need not to fear the bawds.

speeches.speech: There are pretty orders beginning, I can tell you: it is but heading and hanging.
lines: There are pretty orders beginning, I can tell you:
it is but heading and hanging.

speeches.speech: If you head and hang all that offend that way but for ten year together, you'll be glad to give out a commission for more heads: if this law hold in Vienna ten year, I'll rent the fairest house in it after three-pence a bay: if you live to see this come to pass, say Pompey told you so.
lines: If you head and hang all that offend that way but
for ten year together, you'll be glad to give out a
commission for more heads: if this law hold in
Vienna ten year, I'll rent the fairest house in it
after three-pence a bay: if you live to see this
come to pass, say Pompey told you so.

speeches.speech: Thank you, good Pompey; and, in requital of your prophecy, hark you: I advise you, let me not find you before me again upon any complaint whatsoever; no, not for dwelling where you do: if I do, Pompey, I shall beat you to your tent, and prove a shrewd Caesar to you; in plain dealing, Pompey, I shall have you whipt: so, for this time, Pompey, fare you well.
lines: Thank you, good Pompey; and, in requital of your
prophecy, hark you: I advise you, let me not find
you before me again upon any complaint whatsoever;
no, not for dwelling where you do: if I do, Pompey,
I shall beat you to your tent, and prove a shrewd
Caesar to you; in plain dealing, Pompey, I shall
have you whipt: so, for this time, Pompey, fare you well.

speeches.speech: I thank your worship for your good counsel: [Aside] but I shall follow it as the flesh and fortune shall better determine. Whip me? No, no; let carman whip his jade: The valiant heart is not whipt out of his trade.
lines: I thank your worship for your good counsel:
[Aside]
but I shall follow it as the flesh and fortune shall
better determine.
Whip me? No, no; let carman whip his jade:
The valiant heart is not whipt out of his trade.

speeches.speech: Come hither to me, Master Elbow; come hither, Master constable. How long have you been in this place of constable?
lines: Come hither to me, Master Elbow; come hither, Master
constable. How long have you been in this place of constable?

speeches.speech: Seven year and a half, sir.
lines: Seven year and a half, sir.

speeches.speech: I thought, by your readiness in the office, you had continued in it some time. You say, seven years together?
lines: I thought, by your readiness in the office, you had
continued in it some time. You say, seven years together?

speeches.speech: And a half, sir.
lines: And a half, sir.

speeches.speech: Alas, it hath been great pains to you. They do you wrong to put you so oft upon 't: are there not men in your ward sufficient to serve it?
lines: Alas, it hath been great pains to you. They do you
wrong to put you so oft upon 't: are there not men
in your ward sufficient to serve it?

speeches.speech: Faith, sir, few of any wit in such matters: as they are chosen, they are glad to choose me for them; I do it for some piece of money, and go through with all.
lines: Faith, sir, few of any wit in such matters: as they
are chosen, they are glad to choose me for them; I
do it for some piece of money, and go through with
all.

speeches.speech: Look you bring me in the names of some six or seven, the most sufficient of your parish.
lines: Look you bring me in the names of some six or seven,
the most sufficient of your parish.

speeches.speech: To your worship's house, sir?
lines: To your worship's house, sir?

speeches.speech: To my house. Fare you well. [Exit ELBOW] What's o'clock, think you?
lines: To my house. Fare you well.
[Exit ELBOW]
What's o'clock, think you?

speeches.speech: Eleven, sir.
lines: Eleven, sir.

speeches.speech: I pray you home to dinner with me.
lines: I pray you home to dinner with me.

speeches.speech: I humbly thank you.
lines: I humbly thank you.

speeches.speech: It grieves me for the death of Claudio; But there's no remedy.
lines: It grieves me for the death of Claudio;
But there's no remedy.

speeches.speech: Lord Angelo is severe.
lines: Lord Angelo is severe.

speeches.speech: It is but needful: Mercy is not itself, that oft looks so; Pardon is still the nurse of second woe: But yet,--poor Claudio! There is no remedy. Come, sir.
lines: It is but needful:
Mercy is not itself, that oft looks so;
Pardon is still the nurse of second woe:
But yet,--poor Claudio! There is no remedy.
Come, sir.

speeches.speech: He's hearing of a cause; he will come straight I'll tell him of you.
lines: He's hearing of a cause; he will come straight
I'll tell him of you.

speeches.speech: Pray you, do. [Exit Servant] I'll know His pleasure; may be he will relent. Alas, He hath but as offended in a dream! All sects, all ages smack of this vice; and he To die for't!
lines: Pray you, do.
[Exit Servant]
I'll know
His pleasure; may be he will relent. Alas,
He hath but as offended in a dream!
All sects, all ages smack of this vice; and he
To die for't!

speeches.speech: Now, what's the matter. Provost?
lines: Now, what's the matter. Provost?

speeches.speech: Is it your will Claudio shall die tomorrow?
lines: Is it your will Claudio shall die tomorrow?

speeches.speech: Did not I tell thee yea? hadst thou not order? Why dost thou ask again?
lines: Did not I tell thee yea? hadst thou not order?
Why dost thou ask again?

speeches.speech: Lest I might be too rash: Under your good correction, I have seen, When, after execution, judgment hath Repented o'er his doom.
lines: Lest I might be too rash:
Under your good correction, I have seen,
When, after execution, judgment hath
Repented o'er his doom.

speeches.speech: Go to; let that be mine: Do you your office, or give up your place, And you shall well be spared.
lines: Go to; let that be mine:
Do you your office, or give up your place,
And you shall well be spared.

speeches.speech: I crave your honour's pardon. What shall be done, sir, with the groaning Juliet? She's very near her hour.
lines: I crave your honour's pardon.
What shall be done, sir, with the groaning Juliet?
She's very near her hour.

speeches.speech: Dispose of her To some more fitter place, and that with speed.
lines: Dispose of her
To some more fitter place, and that with speed.

speeches.speech: Here is the sister of the man condemn'd Desires access to you.
lines: Here is the sister of the man condemn'd
Desires access to you.

speeches.speech: Hath he a sister?
lines: Hath he a sister?

speeches.speech: Ay, my good lord; a very virtuous maid, And to be shortly of a sisterhood, If not already.
lines: Ay, my good lord; a very virtuous maid,
And to be shortly of a sisterhood,
If not already.

speeches.speech: Well, let her be admitted. [Exit Servant] See you the fornicatress be removed: Let have needful, but not lavish, means; There shall be order for't.
lines: Well, let her be admitted.
[Exit Servant]
See you the fornicatress be removed:
Let have needful, but not lavish, means;
There shall be order for't.

speeches.speech: God save your honour!
lines: God save your honour!

speeches.speech: Stay a little while. [To ISABELLA] You're welcome: what's your will?
lines: Stay a little while.
[To ISABELLA]
You're welcome: what's your will?

speeches.speech: I am a woeful suitor to your honour, Please but your honour hear me.
lines: I am a woeful suitor to your honour,
Please but your honour hear me.

speeches.speech: Well; what's your suit?
lines: Well; what's your suit?

speeches.speech: There is a vice that most I do abhor, And most desire should meet the blow of justice; For which I would not plead, but that I must; For which I must not plead, but that I am At war 'twixt will and will not.
lines: There is a vice that most I do abhor,
And most desire should meet the blow of justice;
For which I would not plead, but that I must;
For which I must not plead, but that I am
At war 'twixt will and will not.

speeches.speech: Well; the matter?
lines: Well; the matter?

speeches.speech: I have a brother is condemn'd to die: I do beseech you, let it be his fault, And not my brother.
lines: I have a brother is condemn'd to die:
I do beseech you, let it be his fault,
And not my brother.

speeches.speech: [Aside] Heaven give thee moving graces!
lines: [Aside] Heaven give thee moving graces!

speeches.speech: Condemn the fault and not the actor of it? Why, every fault's condemn'd ere it be done: Mine were the very cipher of a function, To fine the faults whose fine stands in record, And let go by the actor.
lines: Condemn the fault and not the actor of it?
Why, every fault's condemn'd ere it be done:
Mine were the very cipher of a function,
To fine the faults whose fine stands in record,
And let go by the actor.

speeches.speech: O just but severe law! I had a brother, then. Heaven keep your honour!
lines: O just but severe law!
I had a brother, then. Heaven keep your honour!

speeches.speech: [Aside to ISABELLA] Give't not o'er so: to him again, entreat him; Kneel down before him, hang upon his gown: You are too cold; if you should need a pin, You could not with more tame a tongue desire it: To him, I say!
lines: [Aside to ISABELLA] Give't not o'er so: to him
again, entreat him;
Kneel down before him, hang upon his gown:
You are too cold; if you should need a pin,
You could not with more tame a tongue desire it:
To him, I say!

speeches.speech: Must he needs die?
lines: Must he needs die?

speeches.speech: Maiden, no remedy.
lines: Maiden, no remedy.

speeches.speech: Yes; I do think that you might pardon him, And neither heaven nor man grieve at the mercy.
lines: Yes; I do think that you might pardon him,
And neither heaven nor man grieve at the mercy.

speeches.speech: I will not do't.
lines: I will not do't.

speeches.speech: But can you, if you would?
lines: But can you, if you would?

speeches.speech: Look, what I will not, that I cannot do.
lines: Look, what I will not, that I cannot do.

speeches.speech: But might you do't, and do the world no wrong, If so your heart were touch'd with that remorse As mine is to him?
lines: But might you do't, and do the world no wrong,
If so your heart were touch'd with that remorse
As mine is to him?

speeches.speech: He's sentenced; 'tis too late.
lines: He's sentenced; 'tis too late.

speeches.speech: [Aside to ISABELLA] You are too cold.
lines: [Aside to ISABELLA] You are too cold.

speeches.speech: Too late? why, no; I, that do speak a word. May call it back again. Well, believe this, No ceremony that to great ones 'longs, Not the king's crown, nor the deputed sword, The marshal's truncheon, nor the judge's robe, Become them with one half so good a grace As mercy does. If he had been as you and you as he, You would have slipt like him; but he, like you, Would not have been so stern.
lines: Too late? why, no; I, that do speak a word.
May call it back again. Well, believe this,
No ceremony that to great ones 'longs,
Not the king's crown, nor the deputed sword,
The marshal's truncheon, nor the judge's robe,
Become them with one half so good a grace
As mercy does.
If he had been as you and you as he,
You would have slipt like him; but he, like you,
Would not have been so stern.

speeches.speech: Pray you, be gone.
lines: Pray you, be gone.

speeches.speech: I would to heaven I had your potency, And you were Isabel! should it then be thus? No; I would tell what 'twere to be a judge, And what a prisoner.
lines: I would to heaven I had your potency,
And you were Isabel! should it then be thus?
No; I would tell what 'twere to be a judge,
And what a prisoner.

speeches.speech: [Aside to ISABELLA] Ay, touch him; there's the vein.
lines: [Aside to ISABELLA]
Ay, touch him; there's the vein.

speeches.speech: Your brother is a forfeit of the law, And you but waste your words.
lines: Your brother is a forfeit of the law,
And you but waste your words.

speeches.speech: Alas, alas! Why, all the souls that were were forfeit once; And He that might the vantage best have took Found out the remedy. How would you be, If He, which is the top of judgment, should But judge you as you are? O, think on that; And mercy then will breathe within your lips, Like man new made.
lines: Alas, alas!
Why, all the souls that were were forfeit once;
And He that might the vantage best have took
Found out the remedy. How would you be,
If He, which is the top of judgment, should
But judge you as you are? O, think on that;
And mercy then will breathe within your lips,
Like man new made.

speeches.speech: Be you content, fair maid; It is the law, not I condemn your brother: Were he my kinsman, brother, or my son, It should be thus with him: he must die tomorrow.
lines: Be you content, fair maid;
It is the law, not I condemn your brother:
Were he my kinsman, brother, or my son,
It should be thus with him: he must die tomorrow.

speeches.speech: To-morrow! O, that's sudden! Spare him, spare him! He's not prepared for death. Even for our kitchens We kill the fowl of season: shall we serve heaven With less respect than we do minister To our gross selves? Good, good my lord, bethink you; Who is it that hath died for this offence? There's many have committed it.
lines: To-morrow! O, that's sudden! Spare him, spare him!
He's not prepared for death. Even for our kitchens
We kill the fowl of season: shall we serve heaven
With less respect than we do minister
To our gross selves? Good, good my lord, bethink you;
Who is it that hath died for this offence?
There's many have committed it.

speeches.speech: [Aside to ISABELLA] Ay, well said.
lines: [Aside to ISABELLA] Ay, well said.

speeches.speech: The law hath not been dead, though it hath slept: Those many had not dared to do that evil, If the first that did the edict infringe Had answer'd for his deed: now 'tis awake Takes note of what is done; and, like a prophet, Looks in a glass, that shows what future evils, Either new, or by remissness new-conceived, And so in progress to be hatch'd and born, Are now to have no successive degrees, But, ere they live, to end.
lines: The law hath not been dead, though it hath slept:
Those many had not dared to do that evil,
If the first that did the edict infringe
Had answer'd for his deed: now 'tis awake
Takes note of what is done; and, like a prophet,
Looks in a glass, that shows what future evils,
Either new, or by remissness new-conceived,
And so in progress to be hatch'd and born,
Are now to have no successive degrees,
But, ere they live, to end.

speeches.speech: Yet show some pity.
lines: Yet show some pity.

speeches.speech: I show it most of all when I show justice; For then I pity those I do not know, Which a dismiss'd offence would after gall; And do him right that, answering one foul wrong, Lives not to act another. Be satisfied; Your brother dies to-morrow; be content.
lines: I show it most of all when I show justice;
For then I pity those I do not know,
Which a dismiss'd offence would after gall;
And do him right that, answering one foul wrong,
Lives not to act another. Be satisfied;
Your brother dies to-morrow; be content.

speeches.speech: So you must be the first that gives this sentence, And he, that suffer's. O, it is excellent To have a giant's strength; but it is tyrannous To use it like a giant.
lines: So you must be the first that gives this sentence,
And he, that suffer's. O, it is excellent
To have a giant's strength; but it is tyrannous
To use it like a giant.

speeches.speech: [Aside to ISABELLA] That's well said.
lines: [Aside to ISABELLA] That's well said.

speeches.speech: Could great men thunder As Jove himself does, Jove would ne'er be quiet, For every pelting, petty officer Would use his heaven for thunder; Nothing but thunder! Merciful Heaven, Thou rather with thy sharp and sulphurous bolt Split'st the unwedgeable and gnarled oak Than the soft myrtle: but man, proud man, Drest in a little brief authority, Most ignorant of what he's most assured, His glassy essence, like an angry ape, Plays such fantastic tricks before high heaven As make the angels weep; who, with our spleens, Would all themselves laugh mortal.
lines: Could great men thunder
As Jove himself does, Jove would ne'er be quiet,
For every pelting, petty officer
Would use his heaven for thunder;
Nothing but thunder! Merciful Heaven,
Thou rather with thy sharp and sulphurous bolt
Split'st the unwedgeable and gnarled oak
Than the soft myrtle: but man, proud man,
Drest in a little brief authority,
Most ignorant of what he's most assured,
His glassy essence, like an angry ape,
Plays such fantastic tricks before high heaven
As make the angels weep; who, with our spleens,
Would all themselves laugh mortal.

speeches.speech: [Aside to ISABELLA] O, to him, to him, wench! he will relent; He's coming; I perceive 't.
lines: [Aside to ISABELLA] O, to him, to him, wench! he
will relent;
He's coming; I perceive 't.

speeches.speech: [Aside] Pray heaven she win him!
lines: [Aside] Pray heaven she win him!

speeches.speech: We cannot weigh our brother with ourself: Great men may jest with saints; 'tis wit in them, But in the less foul profanation.
lines: We cannot weigh our brother with ourself:
Great men may jest with saints; 'tis wit in them,
But in the less foul profanation.

speeches.speech: Thou'rt i' the right, girl; more o, that.
lines: Thou'rt i' the right, girl; more o, that.

speeches.speech: That in the captain's but a choleric word, Which in the soldier is flat blasphemy.
lines: That in the captain's but a choleric word,
Which in the soldier is flat blasphemy.

speeches.speech: [Aside to ISABELLA] Art avised o' that? more on 't.
lines: [Aside to ISABELLA] Art avised o' that? more on 't.

speeches.speech: Why do you put these sayings upon me?
lines: Why do you put these sayings upon me?

speeches.speech: Because authority, though it err like others, Hath yet a kind of medicine in itself, That skins the vice o' the top. Go to your bosom; Knock there, and ask your heart what it doth know That's like my brother's fault: if it confess A natural guiltiness such as is his, Let it not sound a thought upon your tongue Against my brother's life.
lines: Because authority, though it err like others,
Hath yet a kind of medicine in itself,
That skins the vice o' the top. Go to your bosom;
Knock there, and ask your heart what it doth know
That's like my brother's fault: if it confess
A natural guiltiness such as is his,
Let it not sound a thought upon your tongue
Against my brother's life.

speeches.speech: [Aside] She speaks, and 'tis Such sense, that my sense breeds with it. Fare you well.
lines: [Aside] She speaks, and 'tis
Such sense, that my sense breeds with it. Fare you well.

speeches.speech: Gentle my lord, turn back.
lines: Gentle my lord, turn back.

speeches.speech: I will bethink me: come again tomorrow.
lines: I will bethink me: come again tomorrow.

speeches.speech: Hark how I'll bribe you: good my lord, turn back.
lines: Hark how I'll bribe you: good my lord, turn back.

speeches.speech: How! bribe me?
lines: How! bribe me?

speeches.speech: Ay, with such gifts that heaven shall share with you.
lines: Ay, with such gifts that heaven shall share with you.

speeches.speech: [Aside to ISABELLA] You had marr'd all else.
lines: [Aside to ISABELLA] You had marr'd all else.

speeches.speech: Not with fond shekels of the tested gold, Or stones whose rates are either rich or poor As fancy values them; but with true prayers That shall be up at heaven and enter there Ere sun-rise, prayers from preserved souls, From fasting maids whose minds are dedicate To nothing temporal.
lines: Not with fond shekels of the tested gold,
Or stones whose rates are either rich or poor
As fancy values them; but with true prayers
That shall be up at heaven and enter there
Ere sun-rise, prayers from preserved souls,
From fasting maids whose minds are dedicate
To nothing temporal.

speeches.speech: Well; come to me to-morrow.
lines: Well; come to me to-morrow.

speeches.speech: [Aside to ISABELLA] Go to; 'tis well; away!
lines: [Aside to ISABELLA] Go to; 'tis well; away!

speeches.speech: Heaven keep your honour safe!
lines: Heaven keep your honour safe!

speeches.speech: [Aside]. Amen: For I am that way going to temptation, Where prayers cross.
lines: [Aside]. Amen:
For I am that way going to temptation,
Where prayers cross.

speeches.speech: At what hour to-morrow Shall I attend your lordship?
lines: At what hour to-morrow
Shall I attend your lordship?

speeches.speech: At any time 'fore noon.
lines: At any time 'fore noon.

speeches.speech: 'Save your honour!
lines: 'Save your honour!

speeches.speech: From thee, even from thy virtue! What's this, what's this? Is this her fault or mine? The tempter or the tempted, who sins most? Ha! Not she: nor doth she tempt: but it is I That, lying by the violet in the sun, Do as the carrion does, not as the flower, Corrupt with virtuous season. Can it be That modesty may more betray our sense Than woman's lightness? Having waste ground enough, Shall we desire to raze the sanctuary And pitch our evils there? O, fie, fie, fie! What dost thou, or what art thou, Angelo? Dost thou desire her foully for those things That make her good? O, let her brother live! Thieves for their robbery have authority When judges steal themselves. What, do I love her, That I desire to hear her speak again, And feast upon her eyes? What is't I dream on? O cunning enemy, that, to catch a saint, With saints dost bait thy hook! Most dangerous Is that temptation that doth goad us on To sin in loving virtue: never could the strumpet, With all her double vigour, art and nature, Once stir my temper; but this virtuous maid Subdues me quite. Even till now, When men were fond, I smiled and wonder'd how.
lines: From thee, even from thy virtue!
What's this, what's this? Is this her fault or mine?
The tempter or the tempted, who sins most?
Ha!
Not she: nor doth she tempt: but it is I
That, lying by the violet in the sun,
Do as the carrion does, not as the flower,
Corrupt with virtuous season. Can it be
That modesty may more betray our sense
Than woman's lightness? Having waste ground enough,
Shall we desire to raze the sanctuary
And pitch our evils there? O, fie, fie, fie!
What dost thou, or what art thou, Angelo?
Dost thou desire her foully for those things
That make her good? O, let her brother live!
Thieves for their robbery have authority
When judges steal themselves. What, do I love her,
That I desire to hear her speak again,
