And feast upon her eyes? What is't I dream on?
O cunning enemy, that, to catch a saint,
With saints dost bait thy hook! Most dangerous
Is that temptation that doth goad us on
To sin in loving virtue: never could the strumpet,
With all her double vigour, art and nature,
Once stir my temper; but this virtuous maid
Subdues me quite. Even till now,
When men were fond, I smiled and wonder'd how.

speeches.speech: Hail to you, provost! so I think you are.
lines: Hail to you, provost! so I think you are.

speeches.speech: I am the provost. What's your will, good friar?
lines: I am the provost. What's your will, good friar?

speeches.speech: Bound by my charity and my blest order, I come to visit the afflicted spirits Here in the prison. Do me the common right To let me see them and to make me know The nature of their crimes, that I may minister To them accordingly.
lines: Bound by my charity and my blest order,
I come to visit the afflicted spirits
Here in the prison. Do me the common right
To let me see them and to make me know
The nature of their crimes, that I may minister
To them accordingly.

speeches.speech: I would do more than that, if more were needful. [Enter JULIET] Look, here comes one: a gentlewoman of mine, Who, falling in the flaws of her own youth, Hath blister'd her report: she is with child; And he that got it, sentenced; a young man More fit to do another such offence Than die for this.
lines: I would do more than that, if more were needful.
[Enter JULIET]
Look, here comes one: a gentlewoman of mine,
Who, falling in the flaws of her own youth,
Hath blister'd her report: she is with child;
And he that got it, sentenced; a young man
More fit to do another such offence
Than die for this.

speeches.speech: When must he die?
lines: When must he die?

speeches.speech: As I do think, to-morrow. I have provided for you: stay awhile, [To JULIET] And you shall be conducted.
lines: As I do think, to-morrow.
I have provided for you: stay awhile,
[To JULIET]
And you shall be conducted.

speeches.speech: Repent you, fair one, of the sin you carry?
lines: Repent you, fair one, of the sin you carry?

speeches.speech: I do; and bear the shame most patiently.
lines: I do; and bear the shame most patiently.

speeches.speech: I'll teach you how you shall arraign your conscience, And try your penitence, if it be sound, Or hollowly put on.
lines: I'll teach you how you shall arraign your conscience,
And try your penitence, if it be sound,
Or hollowly put on.

speeches.speech: I'll gladly learn.
lines: I'll gladly learn.

speeches.speech: Love you the man that wrong'd you?
lines: Love you the man that wrong'd you?

speeches.speech: Yes, as I love the woman that wrong'd him.
lines: Yes, as I love the woman that wrong'd him.

speeches.speech: So then it seems your most offenceful act Was mutually committed?
lines: So then it seems your most offenceful act
Was mutually committed?

speeches.speech: Mutually.
lines: Mutually.

speeches.speech: Then was your sin of heavier kind than his.
lines: Then was your sin of heavier kind than his.

speeches.speech: I do confess it, and repent it, father.
lines: I do confess it, and repent it, father.

speeches.speech: 'Tis meet so, daughter: but lest you do repent, As that the sin hath brought you to this shame, Which sorrow is always towards ourselves, not heaven, Showing we would not spare heaven as we love it, But as we stand in fear,--
lines: 'Tis meet so, daughter: but lest you do repent,
As that the sin hath brought you to this shame,
Which sorrow is always towards ourselves, not heaven,
Showing we would not spare heaven as we love it,
But as we stand in fear,--

speeches.speech: I do repent me, as it is an evil, And take the shame with joy.
lines: I do repent me, as it is an evil,
And take the shame with joy.

speeches.speech: There rest. Your partner, as I hear, must die to-morrow, And I am going with instruction to him. Grace go with you, Benedicite!
lines: There rest.
Your partner, as I hear, must die to-morrow,
And I am going with instruction to him.
Grace go with you, Benedicite!

speeches.speech: Must die to-morrow! O injurious love, That respites me a life, whose very comfort Is still a dying horror!
lines: Must die to-morrow! O injurious love,
That respites me a life, whose very comfort
Is still a dying horror!

speeches.speech: 'Tis pity of him.
lines: 'Tis pity of him.

speeches.speech: When I would pray and think, I think and pray To several subjects. Heaven hath my empty words; Whilst my invention, hearing not my tongue, Anchors on Isabel: Heaven in my mouth, As if I did but only chew his name; And in my heart the strong and swelling evil Of my conception. The state, whereon I studied Is like a good thing, being often read, Grown fear'd and tedious; yea, my gravity, Wherein--let no man hear me--I take pride, Could I with boot change for an idle plume, Which the air beats for vain. O place, O form, How often dost thou with thy case, thy habit, Wrench awe from fools and tie the wiser souls To thy false seeming! Blood, thou art blood: Let's write good angel on the devil's horn: 'Tis not the devil's crest. [Enter a Servant] How now! who's there?
lines: When I would pray and think, I think and pray
To several subjects. Heaven hath my empty words;
Whilst my invention, hearing not my tongue,
Anchors on Isabel: Heaven in my mouth,
As if I did but only chew his name;
And in my heart the strong and swelling evil
Of my conception. The state, whereon I studied
Is like a good thing, being often read,
Grown fear'd and tedious; yea, my gravity,
Wherein--let no man hear me--I take pride,
Could I with boot change for an idle plume,
Which the air beats for vain. O place, O form,
How often dost thou with thy case, thy habit,
Wrench awe from fools and tie the wiser souls
To thy false seeming! Blood, thou art blood:
Let's write good angel on the devil's horn:
'Tis not the devil's crest.
[Enter a Servant]
How now! who's there?

speeches.speech: One Isabel, a sister, desires access to you.
lines: One Isabel, a sister, desires access to you.

speeches.speech: Teach her the way. [Exit Servant] O heavens! Why does my blood thus muster to my heart, Making both it unable for itself, And dispossessing all my other parts Of necessary fitness? So play the foolish throngs with one that swoons; Come all to help him, and so stop the air By which he should revive: and even so The general, subject to a well-wish'd king, Quit their own part, and in obsequious fondness Crowd to his presence, where their untaught love Must needs appear offence. [Enter ISABELLA] How now, fair maid?
lines: Teach her the way.
[Exit Servant]
O heavens!
Why does my blood thus muster to my heart,
Making both it unable for itself,
And dispossessing all my other parts
Of necessary fitness?
So play the foolish throngs with one that swoons;
Come all to help him, and so stop the air
By which he should revive: and even so
The general, subject to a well-wish'd king,
Quit their own part, and in obsequious fondness
Crowd to his presence, where their untaught love
Must needs appear offence.
[Enter ISABELLA]
How now, fair maid?

speeches.speech: I am come to know your pleasure.
lines: I am come to know your pleasure.

speeches.speech: That you might know it, would much better please me Than to demand what 'tis. Your brother cannot live.
lines: That you might know it, would much better please me
Than to demand what 'tis. Your brother cannot live.

speeches.speech: Even so. Heaven keep your honour!
lines: Even so. Heaven keep your honour!

speeches.speech: Yet may he live awhile; and, it may be, As long as you or I. yet he must die.
lines: Yet may he live awhile; and, it may be,
As long as you or I. yet he must die.

speeches.speech: Under your sentence?
lines: Under your sentence?

speeches.speech: Yea.
lines: Yea.

speeches.speech: When, I beseech you? that in his reprieve, Longer or shorter, he may be so fitted That his soul sicken not.
lines: When, I beseech you? that in his reprieve,
Longer or shorter, he may be so fitted
That his soul sicken not.

speeches.speech: Ha! fie, these filthy vices! It were as good To pardon him that hath from nature stolen A man already made, as to remit Their saucy sweetness that do coin heaven's image In stamps that are forbid: 'tis all as easy Falsely to take away a life true made As to put metal in restrained means To make a false one.
lines: Ha! fie, these filthy vices! It were as good
To pardon him that hath from nature stolen
A man already made, as to remit
Their saucy sweetness that do coin heaven's image
In stamps that are forbid: 'tis all as easy
Falsely to take away a life true made
As to put metal in restrained means
To make a false one.

speeches.speech: 'Tis set down so in heaven, but not in earth.
lines: 'Tis set down so in heaven, but not in earth.

speeches.speech: Say you so? then I shall pose you quickly. Which had you rather, that the most just law Now took your brother's life; or, to redeem him, Give up your body to such sweet uncleanness As she that he hath stain'd?
lines: Say you so? then I shall pose you quickly.
Which had you rather, that the most just law
Now took your brother's life; or, to redeem him,
Give up your body to such sweet uncleanness
As she that he hath stain'd?

speeches.speech: Sir, believe this, I had rather give my body than my soul.
lines: Sir, believe this,
I had rather give my body than my soul.

speeches.speech: I talk not of your soul: our compell'd sins Stand more for number than for accompt.
lines: I talk not of your soul: our compell'd sins
Stand more for number than for accompt.

speeches.speech: How say you?
lines: How say you?

speeches.speech: Nay, I'll not warrant that; for I can speak Against the thing I say. Answer to this: I, now the voice of the recorded law, Pronounce a sentence on your brother's life: Might there not be a charity in sin To save this brother's life?
lines: Nay, I'll not warrant that; for I can speak
Against the thing I say. Answer to this:
I, now the voice of the recorded law,
Pronounce a sentence on your brother's life:
Might there not be a charity in sin
To save this brother's life?

speeches.speech: Please you to do't, I'll take it as a peril to my soul, It is no sin at all, but charity.
lines: Please you to do't,
I'll take it as a peril to my soul,
It is no sin at all, but charity.

speeches.speech: Pleased you to do't at peril of your soul, Were equal poise of sin and charity.
lines: Pleased you to do't at peril of your soul,
Were equal poise of sin and charity.

speeches.speech: That I do beg his life, if it be sin, Heaven let me bear it! you granting of my suit, If that be sin, I'll make it my morn prayer To have it added to the faults of mine, And nothing of your answer.
lines: That I do beg his life, if it be sin,
Heaven let me bear it! you granting of my suit,
If that be sin, I'll make it my morn prayer
To have it added to the faults of mine,
And nothing of your answer.

speeches.speech: Nay, but hear me. Your sense pursues not mine: either you are ignorant, Or seem so craftily; and that's not good.
lines: Nay, but hear me.
Your sense pursues not mine: either you are ignorant,
Or seem so craftily; and that's not good.

speeches.speech: Let me be ignorant, and in nothing good, But graciously to know I am no better.
lines: Let me be ignorant, and in nothing good,
But graciously to know I am no better.

speeches.speech: Thus wisdom wishes to appear most bright When it doth tax itself; as these black masks Proclaim an enshield beauty ten times louder Than beauty could, display'd. But mark me; To be received plain, I'll speak more gross: Your brother is to die.
lines: Thus wisdom wishes to appear most bright
When it doth tax itself; as these black masks
Proclaim an enshield beauty ten times louder
Than beauty could, display'd. But mark me;
To be received plain, I'll speak more gross:
Your brother is to die.

speeches.speech: So.
lines: So.

speeches.speech: And his offence is so, as it appears, Accountant to the law upon that pain.
lines: And his offence is so, as it appears,
Accountant to the law upon that pain.

speeches.speech: True.
lines: True.

speeches.speech: Admit no other way to save his life,-- As I subscribe not that, nor any other, But in the loss of question,--that you, his sister, Finding yourself desired of such a person, Whose credit with the judge, or own great place, Could fetch your brother from the manacles Of the all-building law; and that there were No earthly mean to save him, but that either You must lay down the treasures of your body To this supposed, or else to let him suffer; What would you do?
lines: Admit no other way to save his life,--
As I subscribe not that, nor any other,
But in the loss of question,--that you, his sister,
Finding yourself desired of such a person,
Whose credit with the judge, or own great place,
Could fetch your brother from the manacles
Of the all-building law; and that there were
No earthly mean to save him, but that either
You must lay down the treasures of your body
To this supposed, or else to let him suffer;
What would you do?

speeches.speech: As much for my poor brother as myself: That is, were I under the terms of death, The impression of keen whips I'ld wear as rubies, And strip myself to death, as to a bed That longing have been sick for, ere I'ld yield My body up to shame.
lines: As much for my poor brother as myself:
That is, were I under the terms of death,
The impression of keen whips I'ld wear as rubies,
And strip myself to death, as to a bed
That longing have been sick for, ere I'ld yield
My body up to shame.

speeches.speech: Then must your brother die.
lines: Then must your brother die.

speeches.speech: And 'twere the cheaper way: Better it were a brother died at once, Than that a sister, by redeeming him, Should die for ever.
lines: And 'twere the cheaper way:
Better it were a brother died at once,
Than that a sister, by redeeming him,
Should die for ever.

speeches.speech: Were not you then as cruel as the sentence That you have slander'd so?
lines: Were not you then as cruel as the sentence
That you have slander'd so?

speeches.speech: Ignomy in ransom and free pardon Are of two houses: lawful mercy Is nothing kin to foul redemption.
lines: Ignomy in ransom and free pardon
Are of two houses: lawful mercy
Is nothing kin to foul redemption.

speeches.speech: You seem'd of late to make the law a tyrant; And rather proved the sliding of your brother A merriment than a vice.
lines: You seem'd of late to make the law a tyrant;
And rather proved the sliding of your brother
A merriment than a vice.

speeches.speech: O, pardon me, my lord; it oft falls out, To have what we would have, we speak not what we mean: I something do excuse the thing I hate, For his advantage that I dearly love.
lines: O, pardon me, my lord; it oft falls out,
To have what we would have, we speak not what we mean:
I something do excuse the thing I hate,
For his advantage that I dearly love.

speeches.speech: We are all frail.
lines: We are all frail.

speeches.speech: Else let my brother die, If not a feodary, but only he Owe and succeed thy weakness.
lines: Else let my brother die,
If not a feodary, but only he
Owe and succeed thy weakness.

speeches.speech: Nay, women are frail too.
lines: Nay, women are frail too.

speeches.speech: Ay, as the glasses where they view themselves; Which are as easy broke as they make forms. Women! Help Heaven! men their creation mar In profiting by them. Nay, call us ten times frail; For we are soft as our complexions are, And credulous to false prints.
lines: Ay, as the glasses where they view themselves;
Which are as easy broke as they make forms.
Women! Help Heaven! men their creation mar
In profiting by them. Nay, call us ten times frail;
For we are soft as our complexions are,
And credulous to false prints.

speeches.speech: I think it well: And from this testimony of your own sex,-- Since I suppose we are made to be no stronger Than faults may shake our frames,--let me be bold; I do arrest your words. Be that you are, That is, a woman; if you be more, you're none; If you be one, as you are well express'd By all external warrants, show it now, By putting on the destined livery.
lines: I think it well:
And from this testimony of your own sex,--
Since I suppose we are made to be no stronger
Than faults may shake our frames,--let me be bold;
I do arrest your words. Be that you are,
That is, a woman; if you be more, you're none;
If you be one, as you are well express'd
By all external warrants, show it now,
By putting on the destined livery.

speeches.speech: I have no tongue but one: gentle my lord, Let me entreat you speak the former language.
lines: I have no tongue but one: gentle my lord,
Let me entreat you speak the former language.

speeches.speech: Plainly conceive, I love you.
lines: Plainly conceive, I love you.

speeches.speech: My brother did love Juliet, And you tell me that he shall die for it.
lines: My brother did love Juliet,
And you tell me that he shall die for it.

speeches.speech: He shall not, Isabel, if you give me love.
lines: He shall not, Isabel, if you give me love.

speeches.speech: I know your virtue hath a licence in't, Which seems a little fouler than it is, To pluck on others.
lines: I know your virtue hath a licence in't,
Which seems a little fouler than it is,
To pluck on others.

speeches.speech: Believe me, on mine honour, My words express my purpose.
lines: Believe me, on mine honour,
My words express my purpose.

speeches.speech: Ha! little honour to be much believed, And most pernicious purpose! Seeming, seeming! I will proclaim thee, Angelo; look for't: Sign me a present pardon for my brother, Or with an outstretch'd throat I'll tell the world aloud What man thou art.
lines: Ha! little honour to be much believed,
And most pernicious purpose! Seeming, seeming!
I will proclaim thee, Angelo; look for't:
Sign me a present pardon for my brother,
Or with an outstretch'd throat I'll tell the world aloud
What man thou art.

speeches.speech: Who will believe thee, Isabel? My unsoil'd name, the austereness of my life, My vouch against you, and my place i' the state, Will so your accusation overweigh, That you shall stifle in your own report And smell of calumny. I have begun, And now I give my sensual race the rein: Fit thy consent to my sharp appetite; Lay by all nicety and prolixious blushes, That banish what they sue for; redeem thy brother By yielding up thy body to my will; Or else he must not only die the death, But thy unkindness shall his death draw out To lingering sufferance. Answer me to-morrow, Or, by the affection that now guides me most, I'll prove a tyrant to him. As for you, Say what you can, my false o'erweighs your true.
lines: Who will believe thee, Isabel?
My unsoil'd name, the austereness of my life,
My vouch against you, and my place i' the state,
Will so your accusation overweigh,
That you shall stifle in your own report
And smell of calumny. I have begun,
And now I give my sensual race the rein:
Fit thy consent to my sharp appetite;
Lay by all nicety and prolixious blushes,
That banish what they sue for; redeem thy brother
By yielding up thy body to my will;
Or else he must not only die the death,
But thy unkindness shall his death draw out
To lingering sufferance. Answer me to-morrow,
Or, by the affection that now guides me most,
I'll prove a tyrant to him. As for you,
Say what you can, my false o'erweighs your true.

speeches.speech: To whom should I complain? Did I tell this, Who would believe me? O perilous mouths, That bear in them one and the self-same tongue, Either of condemnation or approof; Bidding the law make court'sy to their will: Hooking both right and wrong to the appetite, To follow as it draws! I'll to my brother: Though he hath fallen by prompture of the blood, Yet hath he in him such a mind of honour. That, had he twenty heads to tender down On twenty bloody blocks, he'ld yield them up, Before his sister should her body stoop To such abhorr'd pollution. Then, Isabel, live chaste, and, brother, die: More than our brother is our chastity. I'll tell him yet of Angelo's request, And fit his mind to death, for his soul's rest.
lines: To whom should I complain? Did I tell this,
Who would believe me? O perilous mouths,
That bear in them one and the self-same tongue,
Either of condemnation or approof;
Bidding the law make court'sy to their will:
Hooking both right and wrong to the appetite,
To follow as it draws! I'll to my brother:
Though he hath fallen by prompture of the blood,
Yet hath he in him such a mind of honour.
That, had he twenty heads to tender down
On twenty bloody blocks, he'ld yield them up,
Before his sister should her body stoop
To such abhorr'd pollution.
Then, Isabel, live chaste, and, brother, die:
More than our brother is our chastity.
I'll tell him yet of Angelo's request,
And fit his mind to death, for his soul's rest.

speeches.speech: So then you hope of pardon from Lord Angelo?
lines: So then you hope of pardon from Lord Angelo?

speeches.speech: The miserable have no other medicine But only hope: I've hope to live, and am prepared to die.
lines: The miserable have no other medicine
But only hope:
I've hope to live, and am prepared to die.

speeches.speech: Be absolute for death; either death or life Shall thereby be the sweeter. Reason thus with life: If I do lose thee, I do lose a thing That none but fools would keep: a breath thou art, Servile to all the skyey influences, That dost this habitation, where thou keep'st, Hourly afflict: merely, thou art death's fool; For him thou labour'st by thy flight to shun And yet runn'st toward him still. Thou art not noble; For all the accommodations that thou bear'st Are nursed by baseness. Thou'rt by no means valiant; For thou dost fear the soft and tender fork Of a poor worm. Thy best of rest is sleep, And that thou oft provokest; yet grossly fear'st Thy death, which is no more. Thou art not thyself; For thou exist'st on many a thousand grains That issue out of dust. Happy thou art not; For what thou hast not, still thou strivest to get, And what thou hast, forget'st. Thou art not certain; For thy complexion shifts to strange effects, After the moon. If thou art rich, thou'rt poor; For, like an ass whose back with ingots bows, Thou bear's thy heavy riches but a journey, And death unloads thee. Friend hast thou none; For thine own bowels, which do call thee sire, The mere effusion of thy proper loins, Do curse the gout, serpigo, and the rheum, For ending thee no sooner. Thou hast nor youth nor age, But, as it were, an after-dinner's sleep, Dreaming on both; for all thy blessed youth Becomes as aged, and doth beg the alms Of palsied eld; and when thou art old and rich, Thou hast neither heat, affection, limb, nor beauty, To make thy riches pleasant. What's yet in this That bears the name of life? Yet in this life Lie hid moe thousand deaths: yet death we fear, That makes these odds all even.
lines: Be absolute for death; either death or life
Shall thereby be the sweeter. Reason thus with life:
If I do lose thee, I do lose a thing
That none but fools would keep: a breath thou art,
Servile to all the skyey influences,
That dost this habitation, where thou keep'st,
Hourly afflict: merely, thou art death's fool;
For him thou labour'st by thy flight to shun
And yet runn'st toward him still. Thou art not noble;
For all the accommodations that thou bear'st
Are nursed by baseness. Thou'rt by no means valiant;
For thou dost fear the soft and tender fork
Of a poor worm. Thy best of rest is sleep,
And that thou oft provokest; yet grossly fear'st
Thy death, which is no more. Thou art not thyself;
For thou exist'st on many a thousand grains
That issue out of dust. Happy thou art not;
For what thou hast not, still thou strivest to get,
And what thou hast, forget'st. Thou art not certain;
For thy complexion shifts to strange effects,
After the moon. If thou art rich, thou'rt poor;
For, like an ass whose back with ingots bows,
Thou bear's thy heavy riches but a journey,
And death unloads thee. Friend hast thou none;
For thine own bowels, which do call thee sire,
The mere effusion of thy proper loins,
Do curse the gout, serpigo, and the rheum,
For ending thee no sooner. Thou hast nor youth nor age,
But, as it were, an after-dinner's sleep,
Dreaming on both; for all thy blessed youth
Becomes as aged, and doth beg the alms
Of palsied eld; and when thou art old and rich,
Thou hast neither heat, affection, limb, nor beauty,
To make thy riches pleasant. What's yet in this
That bears the name of life? Yet in this life
Lie hid moe thousand deaths: yet death we fear,
That makes these odds all even.

speeches.speech: I humbly thank you. To sue to live, I find I seek to die; And, seeking death, find life: let it come on.
lines: I humbly thank you.
To sue to live, I find I seek to die;
And, seeking death, find life: let it come on.

speeches.speech: [Within] What, ho! Peace here; grace and good company!
lines: [Within] What, ho! Peace here; grace and good company!

speeches.speech: Who's there? come in: the wish deserves a welcome.
lines: Who's there? come in: the wish deserves a welcome.

speeches.speech: Dear sir, ere long I'll visit you again.
lines: Dear sir, ere long I'll visit you again.

speeches.speech: Most holy sir, I thank you.
lines: Most holy sir, I thank you.

speeches.speech: My business is a word or two with Claudio.
lines: My business is a word or two with Claudio.

speeches.speech: And very welcome. Look, signior, here's your sister.
lines: And very welcome. Look, signior, here's your sister.

speeches.speech: Provost, a word with you.
lines: Provost, a word with you.

speeches.speech: As many as you please.
lines: As many as you please.

speeches.speech: Bring me to hear them speak, where I may be concealed.
lines: Bring me to hear them speak, where I may be concealed.

speeches.speech: Now, sister, what's the comfort?
lines: Now, sister, what's the comfort?

speeches.speech: Why, As all comforts are; most good, most good indeed. Lord Angelo, having affairs to heaven, Intends you for his swift ambassador, Where you shall be an everlasting leiger: Therefore your best appointment make with speed; To-morrow you set on.
lines: Why,
As all comforts are; most good, most good indeed.
Lord Angelo, having affairs to heaven,
Intends you for his swift ambassador,
Where you shall be an everlasting leiger:
Therefore your best appointment make with speed;
To-morrow you set on.

speeches.speech: Is there no remedy?
lines: Is there no remedy?

speeches.speech: None, but such remedy as, to save a head, To cleave a heart in twain.
lines: None, but such remedy as, to save a head,
To cleave a heart in twain.

speeches.speech: But is there any?
lines: But is there any?

speeches.speech: Yes, brother, you may live: There is a devilish mercy in the judge, If you'll implore it, that will free your life, But fetter you till death.
lines: Yes, brother, you may live:
There is a devilish mercy in the judge,
If you'll implore it, that will free your life,
But fetter you till death.

speeches.speech: Perpetual durance?
lines: Perpetual durance?

speeches.speech: Ay, just; perpetual durance, a restraint, Though all the world's vastidity you had, To a determined scope.
lines: Ay, just; perpetual durance, a restraint,
Though all the world's vastidity you had,
To a determined scope.

speeches.speech: But in what nature?
lines: But in what nature?

speeches.speech: In such a one as, you consenting to't, Would bark your honour from that trunk you bear, And leave you naked.
lines: In such a one as, you consenting to't,
Would bark your honour from that trunk you bear,
And leave you naked.

speeches.speech: Let me know the point.
lines: Let me know the point.

speeches.speech: O, I do fear thee, Claudio; and I quake, Lest thou a feverous life shouldst entertain, And six or seven winters more respect Than a perpetual honour. Darest thou die? The sense of death is most in apprehension; And the poor beetle, that we tread upon, In corporal sufferance finds a pang as great As when a giant dies.
lines: O, I do fear thee, Claudio; and I quake,
Lest thou a feverous life shouldst entertain,
And six or seven winters more respect
Than a perpetual honour. Darest thou die?
The sense of death is most in apprehension;
And the poor beetle, that we tread upon,
In corporal sufferance finds a pang as great
As when a giant dies.

speeches.speech: Why give you me this shame? Think you I can a resolution fetch From flowery tenderness? If I must die, I will encounter darkness as a bride, And hug it in mine arms.
lines: Why give you me this shame?
Think you I can a resolution fetch
From flowery tenderness? If I must die,
I will encounter darkness as a bride,
And hug it in mine arms.

speeches.speech: There spake my brother; there my father's grave Did utter forth a voice. Yes, thou must die: Thou art too noble to conserve a life In base appliances. This outward-sainted deputy, Whose settled visage and deliberate word Nips youth i' the head and follies doth emmew As falcon doth the fowl, is yet a devil His filth within being cast, he would appear A pond as deep as hell.
lines: There spake my brother; there my father's grave
Did utter forth a voice. Yes, thou must die:
Thou art too noble to conserve a life
In base appliances. This outward-sainted deputy,
Whose settled visage and deliberate word
Nips youth i' the head and follies doth emmew
As falcon doth the fowl, is yet a devil
His filth within being cast, he would appear
A pond as deep as hell.

speeches.speech: The prenzie Angelo!
lines: The prenzie Angelo!

speeches.speech: O, 'tis the cunning livery of hell, The damned'st body to invest and cover In prenzie guards! Dost thou think, Claudio? If I would yield him my virginity, Thou mightst be freed.
lines: O, 'tis the cunning livery of hell,
The damned'st body to invest and cover
In prenzie guards! Dost thou think, Claudio?
If I would yield him my virginity,
Thou mightst be freed.

speeches.speech: O heavens! it cannot be.
lines: O heavens! it cannot be.

speeches.speech: Yes, he would give't thee, from this rank offence, So to offend him still. This night's the time That I should do what I abhor to name, Or else thou diest to-morrow.
lines: Yes, he would give't thee, from this rank offence,
So to offend him still. This night's the time
That I should do what I abhor to name,
Or else thou diest to-morrow.

speeches.speech: Thou shalt not do't.
lines: Thou shalt not do't.

speeches.speech: O, were it but my life, I'ld throw it down for your deliverance As frankly as a pin.
lines: O, were it but my life,
I'ld throw it down for your deliverance
As frankly as a pin.

speeches.speech: Thanks, dear Isabel.
lines: Thanks, dear Isabel.

speeches.speech: Be ready, Claudio, for your death tomorrow.
lines: Be ready, Claudio, for your death tomorrow.

speeches.speech: Yes. Has he affections in him, That thus can make him bite the law by the nose, When he would force it? Sure, it is no sin, Or of the deadly seven, it is the least.
lines: Yes. Has he affections in him,
That thus can make him bite the law by the nose,
When he would force it? Sure, it is no sin,
Or of the deadly seven, it is the least.

speeches.speech: Which is the least?
lines: Which is the least?

speeches.speech: If it were damnable, he being so wise, Why would he for the momentary trick Be perdurably fined? O Isabel!
lines: If it were damnable, he being so wise,
Why would he for the momentary trick
Be perdurably fined? O Isabel!

speeches.speech: What says my brother?
lines: What says my brother?

speeches.speech: Death is a fearful thing.
lines: Death is a fearful thing.

speeches.speech: And shamed life a hateful.
lines: And shamed life a hateful.

speeches.speech: Ay, but to die, and go we know not where; To lie in cold obstruction and to rot; This sensible warm motion to become A kneaded clod; and the delighted spirit To bathe in fiery floods, or to reside In thrilling region of thick-ribbed ice; To be imprison'd in the viewless winds, And blown with restless violence round about The pendent world; or to be worse than worst Of those that lawless and incertain thought Imagine howling: 'tis too horrible! The weariest and most loathed worldly life That age, ache, penury and imprisonment Can lay on nature is a paradise To what we fear of death.
lines: Ay, but to die, and go we know not where;
To lie in cold obstruction and to rot;
This sensible warm motion to become
A kneaded clod; and the delighted spirit
To bathe in fiery floods, or to reside
In thrilling region of thick-ribbed ice;
To be imprison'd in the viewless winds,
And blown with restless violence round about
The pendent world; or to be worse than worst
Of those that lawless and incertain thought
Imagine howling: 'tis too horrible!
The weariest and most loathed worldly life
That age, ache, penury and imprisonment
Can lay on nature is a paradise
To what we fear of death.

speeches.speech: Alas, alas!
lines: Alas, alas!

speeches.speech: Sweet sister, let me live: What sin you do to save a brother's life, Nature dispenses with the deed so far That it becomes a virtue.
lines: Sweet sister, let me live:
What sin you do to save a brother's life,
Nature dispenses with the deed so far
That it becomes a virtue.

speeches.speech: O you beast! O faithless coward! O dishonest wretch! Wilt thou be made a man out of my vice? Is't not a kind of incest, to take life From thine own sister's shame? What should I think? Heaven shield my mother play'd my father fair! For such a warped slip of wilderness Ne'er issued from his blood. Take my defiance! Die, perish! Might but my bending down Reprieve thee from thy fate, it should proceed: I'll pray a thousand prayers for thy death, No word to save thee.
lines: O you beast!
O faithless coward! O dishonest wretch!
Wilt thou be made a man out of my vice?
Is't not a kind of incest, to take life
From thine own sister's shame? What should I think?
Heaven shield my mother play'd my father fair!
For such a warped slip of wilderness
Ne'er issued from his blood. Take my defiance!
Die, perish! Might but my bending down
Reprieve thee from thy fate, it should proceed:
I'll pray a thousand prayers for thy death,
No word to save thee.

speeches.speech: Nay, hear me, Isabel.
lines: Nay, hear me, Isabel.

speeches.speech: O, fie, fie, fie! Thy sin's not accidental, but a trade. Mercy to thee would prove itself a bawd: 'Tis best thou diest quickly.
lines: O, fie, fie, fie!
Thy sin's not accidental, but a trade.
Mercy to thee would prove itself a bawd:
'Tis best thou diest quickly.

speeches.speech: O hear me, Isabella!
lines: O hear me, Isabella!

speeches.speech: Vouchsafe a word, young sister, but one word.
lines: Vouchsafe a word, young sister, but one word.

speeches.speech: What is your will?
lines: What is your will?

speeches.speech: Might you dispense with your leisure, I would by and by have some speech with you: the satisfaction I would require is likewise your own benefit.
lines: Might you dispense with your leisure, I would by and
by have some speech with you: the satisfaction I
would require is likewise your own benefit.

speeches.speech: I have no superfluous leisure; my stay must be stolen out of other affairs; but I will attend you awhile.
lines: I have no superfluous leisure; my stay must be
stolen out of other affairs; but I will attend you awhile.

speeches.speech: Son, I have overheard what hath passed between you and your sister. Angelo had never the purpose to corrupt her; only he hath made an essay of her virtue to practise his judgment with the disposition of natures: she, having the truth of honour in her, hath made him that gracious denial which he is most glad to receive. I am confessor to Angelo, and I know this to be true; therefore prepare yourself to death: do not satisfy your resolution with hopes that are fallible: tomorrow you must die; go to your knees and make ready.
lines: Son, I have overheard what hath passed between you
and your sister. Angelo had never the purpose to
corrupt her; only he hath made an essay of her
virtue to practise his judgment with the disposition
of natures: she, having the truth of honour in her,
hath made him that gracious denial which he is most
glad to receive. I am confessor to Angelo, and I
know this to be true; therefore prepare yourself to
death: do not satisfy your resolution with hopes
that are fallible: tomorrow you must die; go to
your knees and make ready.

speeches.speech: Let me ask my sister pardon. I am so out of love with life that I will sue to be rid of it.
lines: Let me ask my sister pardon. I am so out of love
with life that I will sue to be rid of it.

speeches.speech: Hold you there: farewell. [Exit CLAUDIO] Provost, a word with you!
lines: Hold you there: farewell.
[Exit CLAUDIO]
Provost, a word with you!

speeches.speech: What's your will, father
lines: What's your will, father

speeches.speech: That now you are come, you will be gone. Leave me awhile with the maid: my mind promises with my habit no loss shall touch her by my company.
lines: That now you are come, you will be gone. Leave me
awhile with the maid: my mind promises with my
habit no loss shall touch her by my company.

speeches.speech: In good time.
lines: In good time.

speeches.speech: The hand that hath made you fair hath made you good: the goodness that is cheap in beauty makes beauty brief in goodness; but grace, being the soul of your complexion, shall keep the body of it ever fair. The assault that Angelo hath made to you, fortune hath conveyed to my understanding; and, but that frailty hath examples for his falling, I should wonder at Angelo. How will you do to content this substitute, and to save your brother?
lines: The hand that hath made you fair hath made you good:
the goodness that is cheap in beauty makes beauty
brief in goodness; but grace, being the soul of
your complexion, shall keep the body of it ever
fair. The assault that Angelo hath made to you,
fortune hath conveyed to my understanding; and, but
that frailty hath examples for his falling, I should
wonder at Angelo. How will you do to content this
substitute, and to save your brother?

speeches.speech: I am now going to resolve him: I had rather my brother die by the law than my son should be unlawfully born. But, O, how much is the good duke deceived in Angelo! If ever he return and I can speak to him, I will open my lips in vain, or discover his government.
lines: I am now going to resolve him: I had rather my
brother die by the law than my son should be
unlawfully born. But, O, how much is the good duke
deceived in Angelo! If ever he return and I can
speak to him, I will open my lips in vain, or
discover his government.

speeches.speech: That shall not be much amiss: Yet, as the matter now stands, he will avoid your accusation; he made trial of you only. Therefore fasten your ear on my advisings: to the love I have in doing good a remedy presents itself. I do make myself believe that you may most uprighteously do a poor wronged lady a merited benefit; redeem your brother from the angry law; do no stain to your own gracious person; and much please the absent duke, if peradventure he shall ever return to have hearing of this business.
lines: That shall not be much amiss: Yet, as the matter
now stands, he will avoid your accusation; he made
trial of you only. Therefore fasten your ear on my
advisings: to the love I have in doing good a
remedy presents itself. I do make myself believe
that you may most uprighteously do a poor wronged
lady a merited benefit; redeem your brother from
the angry law; do no stain to your own gracious
person; and much please the absent duke, if
peradventure he shall ever return to have hearing of
this business.

speeches.speech: Let me hear you speak farther. I have spirit to do anything that appears not foul in the truth of my spirit.
lines: Let me hear you speak farther. I have spirit to do
anything that appears not foul in the truth of my spirit.

speeches.speech: Virtue is bold, and goodness never fearful. Have you not heard speak of Mariana, the sister of Frederick the great soldier who miscarried at sea?
lines: Virtue is bold, and goodness never fearful. Have
you not heard speak of Mariana, the sister of
Frederick the great soldier who miscarried at sea?

speeches.speech: I have heard of the lady, and good words went with her name.
lines: I have heard of the lady, and good words went with her name.

speeches.speech: She should this Angelo have married; was affianced to her by oath, and the nuptial appointed: between which time of the contract and limit of the solemnity, her brother Frederick was wrecked at sea, having in that perished vessel the dowry of his sister. But mark how heavily this befell to the poor gentlewoman: there she lost a noble and renowned brother, in his love toward her ever most kind and natural; with him, the portion and sinew of her fortune, her marriage-dowry; with both, her combinate husband, this well-seeming Angelo.
lines: She should this Angelo have married; was affianced
to her by oath, and the nuptial appointed: between
which time of the contract and limit of the
solemnity, her brother Frederick was wrecked at sea,
having in that perished vessel the dowry of his
sister. But mark how heavily this befell to the
poor gentlewoman: there she lost a noble and
renowned brother, in his love toward her ever most
kind and natural; with him, the portion and sinew of
her fortune, her marriage-dowry; with both, her
combinate husband, this well-seeming Angelo.

speeches.speech: Can this be so? did Angelo so leave her?
lines: Can this be so? did Angelo so leave her?

speeches.speech: Left her in her tears, and dried not one of them with his comfort; swallowed his vows whole, pretending in her discoveries of dishonour: in few, bestowed her on her own lamentation, which she yet wears for his sake; and he, a marble to her tears, is washed with them, but relents not.
lines: Left her in her tears, and dried not one of them
with his comfort; swallowed his vows whole,
pretending in her discoveries of dishonour: in few,
bestowed her on her own lamentation, which she yet
wears for his sake; and he, a marble to her tears,
is washed with them, but relents not.

speeches.speech: What a merit were it in death to take this poor maid from the world! What corruption in this life, that it will let this man live! But how out of this can she avail?
lines: What a merit were it in death to take this poor maid
from the world! What corruption in this life, that
it will let this man live! But how out of this can she avail?

speeches.speech: It is a rupture that you may easily heal: and the cure of it not only saves your brother, but keeps you from dishonour in doing it.
lines: It is a rupture that you may easily heal: and the
cure of it not only saves your brother, but keeps
you from dishonour in doing it.

speeches.speech: Show me how, good father.
lines: Show me how, good father.

speeches.speech: This forenamed maid hath yet in her the continuance of her first affection: his unjust unkindness, that in all reason should have quenched her love, hath, like an impediment in the current, made it more violent and unruly. Go you to Angelo; answer his requiring with a plausible obedience; agree with his demands to the point; only refer yourself to this advantage, first, that your stay with him may not be long; that the time may have all shadow and silence in it; and the place answer to convenience. This being granted in course,--and now follows all,--we shall advise this wronged maid to stead up your appointment, go in your place; if the encounter acknowledge itself hereafter, it may compel him to her recompense: and here, by this, is your brother saved, your honour untainted, the poor Mariana advantaged, and the corrupt deputy scaled. The maid will I frame and make fit for his attempt. If you think well to carry this as you may, the doubleness of the benefit defends the deceit from reproof. What think you of it?
lines: This forenamed maid hath yet in her the continuance
of her first affection: his unjust unkindness, that
in all reason should have quenched her love, hath,
like an impediment in the current, made it more
violent and unruly. Go you to Angelo; answer his
requiring with a plausible obedience; agree with
his demands to the point; only refer yourself to
this advantage, first, that your stay with him may
not be long; that the time may have all shadow and
silence in it; and the place answer to convenience.
This being granted in course,--and now follows
all,--we shall advise this wronged maid to stead up
your appointment, go in your place; if the encounter
acknowledge itself hereafter, it may compel him to
her recompense: and here, by this, is your brother
saved, your honour untainted, the poor Mariana
advantaged, and the corrupt deputy scaled. The maid
will I frame and make fit for his attempt. If you
think well to carry this as you may, the doubleness
of the benefit defends the deceit from reproof.
What think you of it?

speeches.speech: The image of it gives me content already; and I trust it will grow to a most prosperous perfection.
lines: The image of it gives me content already; and I
trust it will grow to a most prosperous perfection.

speeches.speech: It lies much in your holding up. Haste you speedily to Angelo: if for this night he entreat you to his bed, give him promise of satisfaction. I will presently to Saint Luke's: there, at the moated grange, resides this dejected Mariana. At that place call upon me; and dispatch with Angelo, that it may be quickly.
lines: It lies much in your holding up. Haste you speedily
to Angelo: if for this night he entreat you to his
bed, give him promise of satisfaction. I will
presently to Saint Luke's: there, at the moated
grange, resides this dejected Mariana. At that
place call upon me; and dispatch with Angelo, that
it may be quickly.

speeches.speech: I thank you for this comfort. Fare you well, good father.
lines: I thank you for this comfort. Fare you well, good father.

speeches.speech: Nay, if there be no remedy for it, but that you will needs buy and sell men and women like beasts, we shall have all the world drink brown and white bastard.
lines: Nay, if there be no remedy for it, but that you will
needs buy and sell men and women like beasts, we
shall have all the world drink brown and white bastard.

speeches.speech: O heavens! what stuff is here
lines: O heavens! what stuff is here

speeches.speech: 'Twas never merry world since, of two usuries, the merriest was put down, and the worser allowed by order of law a furred gown to keep him warm; and furred with fox and lamb-skins too, to signify, that craft, being richer than innocency, stands for the facing.
lines: 'Twas never merry world since, of two usuries, the
merriest was put down, and the worser allowed by
order of law a furred gown to keep him warm; and
furred with fox and lamb-skins too, to signify, that
craft, being richer than innocency, stands for the facing.

speeches.speech: Come your way, sir. 'Bless you, good father friar.
lines: Come your way, sir. 'Bless you, good father friar.

speeches.speech: And you, good brother father. What offence hath this man made you, sir?
lines: And you, good brother father. What offence hath
this man made you, sir?

speeches.speech: Marry, sir, he hath offended the law: and, sir, we take him to be a thief too, sir; for we have found upon him, sir, a strange picklock, which we have sent to the deputy.
lines: Marry, sir, he hath offended the law: and, sir, we
take him to be a thief too, sir; for we have found
upon him, sir, a strange picklock, which we have
sent to the deputy.

speeches.speech: Fie, sirrah! a bawd, a wicked bawd! The evil that thou causest to be done, That is thy means to live. Do thou but think What 'tis to cram a maw or clothe a back From such a filthy vice: say to thyself, From their abominable and beastly touches I drink, I eat, array myself, and live. Canst thou believe thy living is a life, So stinkingly depending? Go mend, go mend.
lines: Fie, sirrah! a bawd, a wicked bawd!
The evil that thou causest to be done,
That is thy means to live. Do thou but think
What 'tis to cram a maw or clothe a back
From such a filthy vice: say to thyself,
From their abominable and beastly touches
I drink, I eat, array myself, and live.
Canst thou believe thy living is a life,
So stinkingly depending? Go mend, go mend.

speeches.speech: Indeed, it does stink in some sort, sir; but yet, sir, I would prove--
lines: Indeed, it does stink in some sort, sir; but yet,
sir, I would prove--

speeches.speech: Nay, if the devil have given thee proofs for sin, Thou wilt prove his. Take him to prison, officer: Correction and instruction must both work Ere this rude beast will profit.
lines: Nay, if the devil have given thee proofs for sin,
Thou wilt prove his. Take him to prison, officer:
Correction and instruction must both work
Ere this rude beast will profit.

speeches.speech: He must before the deputy, sir; he has given him warning: the deputy cannot abide a whoremaster: if he be a whoremonger, and comes before him, he were as good go a mile on his errand.
lines: He must before the deputy, sir; he has given him
warning: the deputy cannot abide a whoremaster: if
he be a whoremonger, and comes before him, he were
as good go a mile on his errand.

speeches.speech: That we were all, as some would seem to be, From our faults, as faults from seeming, free!
lines: That we were all, as some would seem to be,
From our faults, as faults from seeming, free!

speeches.speech: His neck will come to your waist,--a cord, sir.
lines: His neck will come to your waist,--a cord, sir.

speeches.speech: I spy comfort; I cry bail. Here's a gentleman and a friend of mine.
lines: I spy comfort; I cry bail. Here's a gentleman and a
friend of mine.

speeches.speech: How now, noble Pompey! What, at the wheels of Caesar? art thou led in triumph? What, is there none of Pygmalion's images, newly made woman, to be had now, for putting the hand in the pocket and extracting it clutch'd? What reply, ha? What sayest thou to this tune, matter and method? Is't not drowned i' the last rain, ha? What sayest thou, Trot? Is the world as it was, man? Which is the way? Is it sad, and few words? or how? The trick of it?
lines: How now, noble Pompey! What, at the wheels of
Caesar? art thou led in triumph? What, is there
none of Pygmalion's images, newly made woman, to be
had now, for putting the hand in the pocket and
extracting it clutch'd? What reply, ha? What
sayest thou to this tune, matter and method? Is't
not drowned i' the last rain, ha? What sayest
thou, Trot? Is the world as it was, man? Which is
the way? Is it sad, and few words? or how? The
trick of it?

speeches.speech: Still thus, and thus; still worse!
lines: Still thus, and thus; still worse!

speeches.speech: How doth my dear morsel, thy mistress? Procures she still, ha?
lines: How doth my dear morsel, thy mistress? Procures she
still, ha?

speeches.speech: Troth, sir, she hath eaten up all her beef, and she is herself in the tub.
lines: Troth, sir, she hath eaten up all her beef, and she
is herself in the tub.

speeches.speech: Why, 'tis good; it is the right of it; it must be so: ever your fresh whore and your powdered bawd: an unshunned consequence; it must be so. Art going to prison, Pompey?
lines: Why, 'tis good; it is the right of it; it must be
so: ever your fresh whore and your powdered bawd:
an unshunned consequence; it must be so. Art going
to prison, Pompey?

speeches.speech: Yes, faith, sir.
lines: Yes, faith, sir.

speeches.speech: Why, 'tis not amiss, Pompey. Farewell: go, say I sent thee thither. For debt, Pompey? or how?
lines: Why, 'tis not amiss, Pompey. Farewell: go, say I
sent thee thither. For debt, Pompey? or how?

speeches.speech: For being a bawd, for being a bawd.
lines: For being a bawd, for being a bawd.

speeches.speech: Well, then, imprison him: if imprisonment be the due of a bawd, why, 'tis his right: bawd is he doubtless, and of antiquity too; bawd-born. Farewell, good Pompey. Commend me to the prison, Pompey: you will turn good husband now, Pompey; you will keep the house.
lines: Well, then, imprison him: if imprisonment be the
due of a bawd, why, 'tis his right: bawd is he
doubtless, and of antiquity too; bawd-born.
Farewell, good Pompey. Commend me to the prison,
Pompey: you will turn good husband now, Pompey; you
will keep the house.

speeches.speech: I hope, sir, your good worship will be my bail.
lines: I hope, sir, your good worship will be my bail.

speeches.speech: No, indeed, will I not, Pompey; it is not the wear. I will pray, Pompey, to increase your bondage: If you take it not patiently, why, your mettle is the more. Adieu, trusty Pompey. 'Bless you, friar.
lines: No, indeed, will I not, Pompey; it is not the wear.
I will pray, Pompey, to increase your bondage: If
you take it not patiently, why, your mettle is the
more. Adieu, trusty Pompey. 'Bless you, friar.

speeches.speech: And you.
lines: And you.

speeches.speech: Does Bridget paint still, Pompey, ha?
lines: Does Bridget paint still, Pompey, ha?

speeches.speech: Come your ways, sir; come.
lines: Come your ways, sir; come.

speeches.speech: You will not bail me, then, sir?
lines: You will not bail me, then, sir?

speeches.speech: Then, Pompey, nor now. What news abroad, friar? what news?
lines: Then, Pompey, nor now. What news abroad, friar?
what news?

speeches.speech: Come your ways, sir; come.
lines: Come your ways, sir; come.

speeches.speech: Go to kennel, Pompey; go. [Exeunt ELBOW, POMPEY and Officers] What news, friar, of the duke?
lines: Go to kennel, Pompey; go.
[Exeunt ELBOW, POMPEY and Officers]
What news, friar, of the duke?

speeches.speech: I know none. Can you tell me of any?
lines: I know none. Can you tell me of any?

speeches.speech: Some say he is with the Emperor of Russia; other some, he is in Rome: but where is he, think you?
lines: Some say he is with the Emperor of Russia; other
some, he is in Rome: but where is he, think you?

speeches.speech: I know not where; but wheresoever, I wish him well.
lines: I know not where; but wheresoever, I wish him well.

speeches.speech: It was a mad fantastical trick of him to steal from the state, and usurp the beggary he was never born to. Lord Angelo dukes it well in his absence; he puts transgression to 't.
lines: It was a mad fantastical trick of him to steal from
the state, and usurp the beggary he was never born
to. Lord Angelo dukes it well in his absence; he
puts transgression to 't.

speeches.speech: He does well in 't.
lines: He does well in 't.

speeches.speech: A little more lenity to lechery would do no harm in him: something too crabbed that way, friar.
lines: A little more lenity to lechery would do no harm in
him: something too crabbed that way, friar.

speeches.speech: It is too general a vice, and severity must cure it.
lines: It is too general a vice, and severity must cure it.

speeches.speech: Yes, in good sooth, the vice is of a great kindred; it is well allied: but it is impossible to extirp it quite, friar, till eating and drinking be put down. They say this Angelo was not made by man and woman after this downright way of creation: is it true, think you?
lines: Yes, in good sooth, the vice is of a great kindred;
it is well allied: but it is impossible to extirp
it quite, friar, till eating and drinking be put
down. They say this Angelo was not made by man and
woman after this downright way of creation: is it
true, think you?

speeches.speech: How should he be made, then?
lines: How should he be made, then?

speeches.speech: Some report a sea-maid spawned him; some, that he was begot between two stock-fishes. But it is certain that when he makes water his urine is congealed ice; that I know to be true: and he is a motion generative; that's infallible.
lines: Some report a sea-maid spawned him; some, that he
was begot between two stock-fishes. But it is
certain that when he makes water his urine is
congealed ice; that I know to be true: and he is a
motion generative; that's infallible.

speeches.speech: You are pleasant, sir, and speak apace.
lines: You are pleasant, sir, and speak apace.

speeches.speech: Why, what a ruthless thing is this in him, for the rebellion of a codpiece to take away the life of a man! Would the duke that is absent have done this? Ere he would have hanged a man for the getting a hundred bastards, he would have paid for the nursing a thousand: he had some feeling of the sport: he knew the service, and that instructed him to mercy.
lines: Why, what a ruthless thing is this in him, for the
rebellion of a codpiece to take away the life of a
man! Would the duke that is absent have done this?
Ere he would have hanged a man for the getting a
hundred bastards, he would have paid for the nursing
a thousand: he had some feeling of the sport: he
knew the service, and that instructed him to mercy.

speeches.speech: I never heard the absent duke much detected for women; he was not inclined that way.
lines: I never heard the absent duke much detected for
women; he was not inclined that way.

speeches.speech: O, sir, you are deceived.
lines: O, sir, you are deceived.

speeches.speech: 'Tis not possible.
lines: 'Tis not possible.

speeches.speech: Who, not the duke? yes, your beggar of fifty; and his use was to put a ducat in her clack-dish: the duke had crotchets in him. He would be drunk too; that let me inform you.
lines: Who, not the duke? yes, your beggar of fifty; and
his use was to put a ducat in her clack-dish: the
duke had crotchets in him. He would be drunk too;
that let me inform you.

speeches.speech: You do him wrong, surely.
lines: You do him wrong, surely.

speeches.speech: Sir, I was an inward of his. A shy fellow was the duke: and I believe I know the cause of his withdrawing.
lines: Sir, I was an inward of his. A shy fellow was the
duke: and I believe I know the cause of his
withdrawing.

speeches.speech: What, I prithee, might be the cause?
lines: What, I prithee, might be the cause?

speeches.speech: No, pardon; 'tis a secret must be locked within the teeth and the lips: but this I can let you understand, the greater file of the subject held the duke to be wise.
lines: No, pardon; 'tis a secret must be locked within the
teeth and the lips: but this I can let you
understand, the greater file of the subject held the
duke to be wise.

speeches.speech: Wise! why, no question but he was.
lines: Wise! why, no question but he was.

speeches.speech: A very superficial, ignorant, unweighing fellow.
lines: A very superficial, ignorant, unweighing fellow.

speeches.speech: Either this is the envy in you, folly, or mistaking: the very stream of his life and the business he hath helmed must upon a warranted need give him a better proclamation. Let him be but testimonied in his own bringings-forth, and he shall appear to the envious a scholar, a statesman and a soldier. Therefore you speak unskilfully: or if your knowledge be more it is much darkened in your malice.
lines: Either this is the envy in you, folly, or mistaking:
the very stream of his life and the business he hath
helmed must upon a warranted need give him a better
proclamation. Let him be but testimonied in his own
bringings-forth, and he shall appear to the
envious a scholar, a statesman and a soldier.
Therefore you speak unskilfully: or if your
knowledge be more it is much darkened in your malice.

speeches.speech: Sir, I know him, and I love him.
lines: Sir, I know him, and I love him.

speeches.speech: Love talks with better knowledge, and knowledge with dearer love.
lines: Love talks with better knowledge, and knowledge with
dearer love.

speeches.speech: Come, sir, I know what I know.
lines: Come, sir, I know what I know.

speeches.speech: I can hardly believe that, since you know not what you speak. But, if ever the duke return, as our prayers are he may, let me desire you to make your answer before him. If it be honest you have spoke, you have courage to maintain it: I am bound to call upon you; and, I pray you, your name?
lines: I can hardly believe that, since you know not what
you speak. But, if ever the duke return, as our
prayers are he may, let me desire you to make your
answer before him. If it be honest you have spoke,
you have courage to maintain it: I am bound to call
upon you; and, I pray you, your name?

speeches.speech: Sir, my name is Lucio; well known to the duke.
lines: Sir, my name is Lucio; well known to the duke.

speeches.speech: He shall know you better, sir, if I may live to report you.
lines: He shall know you better, sir, if I may live to
report you.

speeches.speech: I fear you not.
lines: I fear you not.

speeches.speech: O, you hope the duke will return no more; or you imagine me too unhurtful an opposite. But indeed I can do you little harm; you'll forswear this again.
lines: O, you hope the duke will return no more; or you
imagine me too unhurtful an opposite. But indeed I
can do you little harm; you'll forswear this again.

speeches.speech: I'll be hanged first: thou art deceived in me, friar. But no more of this. Canst thou tell if Claudio die to-morrow or no?
lines: I'll be hanged first: thou art deceived in me,
friar. But no more of this. Canst thou tell if
Claudio die to-morrow or no?

speeches.speech: Why should he die, sir?
lines: Why should he die, sir?

speeches.speech: Why? For filling a bottle with a tundish. I would the duke we talk of were returned again: the ungenitured agent will unpeople the province with continency; sparrows must not build in his house-eaves, because they are lecherous. The duke yet would have dark deeds darkly answered; he would never bring them to light: would he were returned! Marry, this Claudio is condemned for untrussing. Farewell, good friar: I prithee, pray for me. The duke, I say to thee again, would eat mutton on Fridays. He's not past it yet, and I say to thee, he would mouth with a beggar, though she smelt brown bread and garlic: say that I said so. Farewell.
lines: Why? For filling a bottle with a tundish. I would
the duke we talk of were returned again: the
ungenitured agent will unpeople the province with
continency; sparrows must not build in his
house-eaves, because they are lecherous. The duke
yet would have dark deeds darkly answered; he would
never bring them to light: would he were returned!
Marry, this Claudio is condemned for untrussing.
Farewell, good friar: I prithee, pray for me. The
duke, I say to thee again, would eat mutton on
Fridays. He's not past it yet, and I say to thee,
he would mouth with a beggar, though she smelt brown
bread and garlic: say that I said so. Farewell.

speeches.speech: No might nor greatness in mortality Can censure 'scape; back-wounding calumny The whitest virtue strikes. What king so strong Can tie the gall up in the slanderous tongue? But who comes here?
lines: No might nor greatness in mortality
Can censure 'scape; back-wounding calumny
The whitest virtue strikes. What king so strong
Can tie the gall up in the slanderous tongue?
But who comes here?

speeches.speech: Go; away with her to prison!
lines: Go; away with her to prison!

speeches.speech: Good my lord, be good to me; your honour is accounted a merciful man; good my lord.
lines: Good my lord, be good to me; your honour is accounted
a merciful man; good my lord.

speeches.speech: Double and treble admonition, and still forfeit in the same kind! This would make mercy swear and play the tyrant.
lines: Double and treble admonition, and still forfeit in
the same kind! This would make mercy swear and play
the tyrant.

speeches.speech: A bawd of eleven years' continuance, may it please your honour.
lines: A bawd of eleven years' continuance, may it please
your honour.

speeches.speech: My lord, this is one Lucio's information against me. Mistress Kate Keepdown was with child by him in the duke's time; he promised her marriage: his child is a year and a quarter old, come Philip and Jacob: I have kept it myself; and see how he goes about to abuse me!
lines: My lord, this is one Lucio's information against me.
Mistress Kate Keepdown was with child by him in the
duke's time; he promised her marriage: his child
is a year and a quarter old, come Philip and Jacob:
I have kept it myself; and see how he goes about to abuse me!

speeches.speech: That fellow is a fellow of much licence: let him be called before us. Away with her to prison! Go to; no more words. [Exeunt Officers with MISTRESS OVERDONE] Provost, my brother Angelo will not be altered; Claudio must die to-morrow: let him be furnished with divines, and have all charitable preparation. if my brother wrought by my pity, it should not be so with him.
lines: That fellow is a fellow of much licence: let him be
called before us. Away with her to prison! Go to;
no more words.
[Exeunt Officers with MISTRESS OVERDONE]
Provost, my brother Angelo will not be altered;
Claudio must die to-morrow: let him be furnished
with divines, and have all charitable preparation.
if my brother wrought by my pity, it should not be
so with him.

speeches.speech: So please you, this friar hath been with him, and advised him for the entertainment of death.
lines: So please you, this friar hath been with him, and
advised him for the entertainment of death.

speeches.speech: Good even, good father.
lines: Good even, good father.

speeches.speech: Bliss and goodness on you!
lines: Bliss and goodness on you!

speeches.speech: Of whence are you?
lines: Of whence are you?

speeches.speech: Not of this country, though my chance is now To use it for my time: I am a brother Of gracious order, late come from the See In special business from his holiness.
lines: Not of this country, though my chance is now
To use it for my time: I am a brother
Of gracious order, late come from the See
In special business from his holiness.

speeches.speech: What news abroad i' the world?
lines: What news abroad i' the world?

speeches.speech: None, but that there is so great a fever on goodness, that the dissolution of it must cure it: novelty is only in request; and it is as dangerous to be aged in any kind of course, as it is virtuous to be constant in any undertaking. There is scarce truth enough alive to make societies secure; but security enough to make fellowships accurst: much upon this riddle runs the wisdom of the world. This news is old enough, yet it is every day's news. I pray you, sir, of what disposition was the duke?
lines: None, but that there is so great a fever on
goodness, that the dissolution of it must cure it:
novelty is only in request; and it is as dangerous
to be aged in any kind of course, as it is virtuous
to be constant in any undertaking. There is scarce
truth enough alive to make societies secure; but
security enough to make fellowships accurst: much
upon this riddle runs the wisdom of the world. This
news is old enough, yet it is every day's news. I
pray you, sir, of what disposition was the duke?

speeches.speech: One that, above all other strifes, contended especially to know himself.
lines: One that, above all other strifes, contended
especially to know himself.

speeches.speech: What pleasure was he given to?
lines: What pleasure was he given to?

speeches.speech: Rather rejoicing to see another merry, than merry at any thing which professed to make him rejoice: a gentleman of all temperance. But leave we him to his events, with a prayer they may prove prosperous; and let me desire to know how you find Claudio prepared. I am made to understand that you have lent him visitation.
lines: Rather rejoicing to see another merry, than merry at
any thing which professed to make him rejoice: a
gentleman of all temperance. But leave we him to
his events, with a prayer they may prove prosperous;
and let me desire to know how you find Claudio
prepared. I am made to understand that you have
lent him visitation.

speeches.speech: He professes to have received no sinister measure from his judge, but most willingly humbles himself to the determination of justice: yet had he framed to himself, by the instruction of his frailty, many deceiving promises of life; which I by my good leisure have discredited to him, and now is he resolved to die.
lines: He professes to have received no sinister measure
from his judge, but most willingly humbles himself
to the determination of justice: yet had he framed
to himself, by the instruction of his frailty, many
deceiving promises of life; which I by my good
leisure have discredited to him, and now is he
resolved to die.

speeches.speech: You have paid the heavens your function, and the prisoner the very debt of your calling. I have laboured for the poor gentleman to the extremest shore of my modesty: but my brother justice have I found so severe, that he hath forced me to tell him he is indeed Justice.
lines: You have paid the heavens your function, and the
prisoner the very debt of your calling. I have
laboured for the poor gentleman to the extremest
shore of my modesty: but my brother justice have I
found so severe, that he hath forced me to tell him
he is indeed Justice.

speeches.speech: If his own life answer the straitness of his proceeding, it shall become him well; wherein if he chance to fail, he hath sentenced himself.
lines: If his own life answer the straitness of his
proceeding, it shall become him well; wherein if he
chance to fail, he hath sentenced himself.

speeches.speech: I am going to visit the prisoner. Fare you well.
lines: I am going to visit the prisoner. Fare you well.

speeches.speech: Peace be with you! [Exeunt ESCALUS and Provost] He who the sword of heaven will bear Should be as holy as severe; Pattern in himself to know, Grace to stand, and virtue go; More nor less to others paying Than by self-offences weighing. Shame to him whose cruel striking Kills for faults of his own liking! Twice treble shame on Angelo, To weed my vice and let his grow! O, what may man within him hide, Though angel on the outward side! How may likeness made in crimes, Making practise on the times, To draw with idle spiders' strings Most ponderous and substantial things! Craft against vice I must apply: With Angelo to-night shall lie His old betrothed but despised; So disguise shall, by the disguised, Pay with falsehood false exacting, And perform an old contracting.
lines: Peace be with you!
[Exeunt ESCALUS and Provost]
He who the sword of heaven will bear
Should be as holy as severe;
Pattern in himself to know,
Grace to stand, and virtue go;
More nor less to others paying
Than by self-offences weighing.
Shame to him whose cruel striking
Kills for faults of his own liking!
Twice treble shame on Angelo,
To weed my vice and let his grow!
O, what may man within him hide,
Though angel on the outward side!
How may likeness made in crimes,
Making practise on the times,
To draw with idle spiders' strings
Most ponderous and substantial things!
Craft against vice I must apply:
With Angelo to-night shall lie
His old betrothed but despised;
So disguise shall, by the disguised,
Pay with falsehood false exacting,
And perform an old contracting.

speeches.speech: Break off thy song, and haste thee quick away: Here comes a man of comfort, whose advice Hath often still'd my brawling discontent. [Exit Boy] [Enter DUKE VINCENTIO disguised as before] I cry you mercy, sir; and well could wish You had not found me here so musical: Let me excuse me, and believe me so, My mirth it much displeased, but pleased my woe.
lines: Break off thy song, and haste thee quick away:
Here comes a man of comfort, whose advice
Hath often still'd my brawling discontent.
[Exit Boy]
[Enter DUKE VINCENTIO disguised as before]
I cry you mercy, sir; and well could wish
You had not found me here so musical:
Let me excuse me, and believe me so,
My mirth it much displeased, but pleased my woe.

speeches.speech: 'Tis good; though music oft hath such a charm To make bad good, and good provoke to harm. I pray, you, tell me, hath any body inquired for me here to-day? much upon this time have I promised here to meet.
lines: 'Tis good; though music oft hath such a charm
To make bad good, and good provoke to harm.
I pray, you, tell me, hath any body inquired
for me here to-day? much upon this time have
I promised here to meet.

speeches.speech: You have not been inquired after: I have sat here all day.
lines: You have not been inquired after:
I have sat here all day.

speeches.speech: I do constantly believe you. The time is come even now. I shall crave your forbearance a little: may be I will call upon you anon, for some advantage to yourself.
lines: I do constantly believe you. The time is come even
now. I shall crave your forbearance a little: may
be I will call upon you anon, for some advantage to yourself.

speeches.speech: I am always bound to you.
lines: I am always bound to you.

speeches.speech: Very well met, and well come. What is the news from this good deputy?
lines: Very well met, and well come.
What is the news from this good deputy?

speeches.speech: He hath a garden circummured with brick, Whose western side is with a vineyard back'd; And to that vineyard is a planched gate, That makes his opening with this bigger key: This other doth command a little door Which from the vineyard to the garden leads; There have I made my promise Upon the heavy middle of the night To call upon him.
lines: He hath a garden circummured with brick,
Whose western side is with a vineyard back'd;
And to that vineyard is a planched gate,
That makes his opening with this bigger key:
This other doth command a little door
Which from the vineyard to the garden leads;
There have I made my promise
Upon the heavy middle of the night
To call upon him.

speeches.speech: But shall you on your knowledge find this way?
lines: But shall you on your knowledge find this way?

speeches.speech: I have ta'en a due and wary note upon't: With whispering and most guilty diligence, In action all of precept, he did show me The way twice o'er.
lines: I have ta'en a due and wary note upon't:
With whispering and most guilty diligence,
In action all of precept, he did show me
The way twice o'er.

speeches.speech: Are there no other tokens Between you 'greed concerning her observance?
lines: Are there no other tokens
Between you 'greed concerning her observance?

speeches.speech: No, none, but only a repair i' the dark; And that I have possess'd him my most stay Can be but brief; for I have made him know I have a servant comes with me along, That stays upon me, whose persuasion is I come about my brother.
lines: No, none, but only a repair i' the dark;
And that I have possess'd him my most stay
Can be but brief; for I have made him know
I have a servant comes with me along,
That stays upon me, whose persuasion is
I come about my brother.

speeches.speech: 'Tis well borne up. I have not yet made known to Mariana A word of this. What, ho! within! come forth! [Re-enter MARIANA] I pray you, be acquainted with this maid; She comes to do you good.
lines: 'Tis well borne up.
I have not yet made known to Mariana
A word of this. What, ho! within! come forth!
[Re-enter MARIANA]
I pray you, be acquainted with this maid;
She comes to do you good.

speeches.speech: I do desire the like.
lines: I do desire the like.

speeches.speech: Do you persuade yourself that I respect you?
lines: Do you persuade yourself that I respect you?

speeches.speech: Good friar, I know you do, and have found it.
lines: Good friar, I know you do, and have found it.

speeches.speech: Take, then, this your companion by the hand, Who hath a story ready for your ear. I shall attend your leisure: but make haste; The vaporous night approaches.
lines: Take, then, this your companion by the hand,
Who hath a story ready for your ear.
I shall attend your leisure: but make haste;
The vaporous night approaches.

speeches.speech: Will't please you walk aside?
lines: Will't please you walk aside?

speeches.speech: O place and greatness! millions of false eyes Are stuck upon thee: volumes of report Run with these false and most contrarious quests Upon thy doings: thousand escapes of wit Make thee the father of their idle dreams And rack thee in their fancies. [Re-enter MARIANA and ISABELLA] Welcome, how agreed?
lines: O place and greatness! millions of false eyes
Are stuck upon thee: volumes of report
Run with these false and most contrarious quests
Upon thy doings: thousand escapes of wit
Make thee the father of their idle dreams
And rack thee in their fancies.
[Re-enter MARIANA and ISABELLA]
Welcome, how agreed?

speeches.speech: She'll take the enterprise upon her, father, If you advise it.
lines: She'll take the enterprise upon her, father,
If you advise it.

speeches.speech: It is not my consent, But my entreaty too.
lines: It is not my consent,
But my entreaty too.

speeches.speech: Little have you to say When you depart from him, but, soft and low, 'Remember now my brother.'
lines: Little have you to say
When you depart from him, but, soft and low,
'Remember now my brother.'

speeches.speech: Fear me not.
lines: Fear me not.

speeches.speech: Nor, gentle daughter, fear you not at all. He is your husband on a pre-contract: To bring you thus together, 'tis no sin, Sith that the justice of your title to him Doth flourish the deceit. Come, let us go: Our corn's to reap, for yet our tithe's to sow.
lines: Nor, gentle daughter, fear you not at all.
He is your husband on a pre-contract:
To bring you thus together, 'tis no sin,
Sith that the justice of your title to him
Doth flourish the deceit. Come, let us go:
Our corn's to reap, for yet our tithe's to sow.

speeches.speech: Come hither, sirrah. Can you cut off a man's head?
lines: Come hither, sirrah. Can you cut off a man's head?

speeches.speech: If the man be a bachelor, sir, I can; but if he be a married man, he's his wife's head, and I can never cut off a woman's head.
lines: If the man be a bachelor, sir, I can; but if he be a
married man, he's his wife's head, and I can never
cut off a woman's head.

speeches.speech: Come, sir, leave me your snatches, and yield me a direct answer. To-morrow morning are to die Claudio and Barnardine. Here is in our prison a common executioner, who in his office lacks a helper: if you will take it on you to assist him, it shall redeem you from your gyves; if not, you shall have your full time of imprisonment and your deliverance with an unpitied whipping, for you have been a notorious bawd.
lines: Come, sir, leave me your snatches, and yield me a
direct answer. To-morrow morning are to die Claudio
and Barnardine. Here is in our prison a common
executioner, who in his office lacks a helper: if
you will take it on you to assist him, it shall
redeem you from your gyves; if not, you shall have
your full time of imprisonment and your deliverance
with an unpitied whipping, for you have been a
notorious bawd.

speeches.speech: Sir, I have been an unlawful bawd time out of mind; but yet I will be content to be a lawful hangman. I would be glad to receive some instruction from my fellow partner.
lines: Sir, I have been an unlawful bawd time out of mind;
but yet I will be content to be a lawful hangman. I
would be glad to receive some instruction from my
fellow partner.

speeches.speech: What, ho! Abhorson! Where's Abhorson, there?
lines: What, ho! Abhorson! Where's Abhorson, there?

speeches.speech: Do you call, sir?
lines: Do you call, sir?

speeches.speech: Sirrah, here's a fellow will help you to-morrow in your execution. If you think it meet, compound with him by the year, and let him abide here with you; if not, use him for the present and dismiss him. He cannot plead his estimation with you; he hath been a bawd.
