lines: Sirrah, here's a fellow will help you to-morrow in
your execution. If you think it meet, compound with
him by the year, and let him abide here with you; if
not, use him for the present and dismiss him. He
cannot plead his estimation with you; he hath been a bawd.

speeches.speech: A bawd, sir? fie upon him! he will discredit our mystery.
lines: A bawd, sir? fie upon him! he will discredit our mystery.

speeches.speech: Go to, sir; you weigh equally; a feather will turn the scale.
lines: Go to, sir; you weigh equally; a feather will turn
the scale.

speeches.speech: Pray, sir, by your good favour,--for surely, sir, a good favour you have, but that you have a hanging look,--do you call, sir, your occupation a mystery?
lines: Pray, sir, by your good favour,--for surely, sir, a
good favour you have, but that you have a hanging
look,--do you call, sir, your occupation a mystery?

speeches.speech: Ay, sir; a mystery
lines: Ay, sir; a mystery

speeches.speech: Painting, sir, I have heard say, is a mystery; and your whores, sir, being members of my occupation, using painting, do prove my occupation a mystery: but what mystery there should be in hanging, if I should be hanged, I cannot imagine.
lines: Painting, sir, I have heard say, is a mystery; and
your whores, sir, being members of my occupation,
using painting, do prove my occupation a mystery:
but what mystery there should be in hanging, if I
should be hanged, I cannot imagine.

speeches.speech: Sir, it is a mystery.
lines: Sir, it is a mystery.

speeches.speech: Proof?
lines: Proof?

speeches.speech: Every true man's apparel fits your thief: if it be too little for your thief, your true man thinks it big enough; if it be too big for your thief, your thief thinks it little enough: so every true man's apparel fits your thief.
lines: Every true man's apparel fits your thief: if it be
too little for your thief, your true man thinks it
big enough; if it be too big for your thief, your
thief thinks it little enough: so every true man's
apparel fits your thief.

speeches.speech: Are you agreed?
lines: Are you agreed?

speeches.speech: Sir, I will serve him; for I do find your hangman is a more penitent trade than your bawd; he doth oftener ask forgiveness.
lines: Sir, I will serve him; for I do find your hangman is
a more penitent trade than your bawd; he doth
oftener ask forgiveness.

speeches.speech: You, sirrah, provide your block and your axe to-morrow four o'clock.
lines: You, sirrah, provide your block and your axe
to-morrow four o'clock.

speeches.speech: Come on, bawd; I will instruct thee in my trade; follow.
lines: Come on, bawd; I will instruct thee in my trade; follow.

speeches.speech: I do desire to learn, sir: and I hope, if you have occasion to use me for your own turn, you shall find me yare; for truly, sir, for your kindness I owe you a good turn.
lines: I do desire to learn, sir: and I hope, if you have
occasion to use me for your own turn, you shall find
me yare; for truly, sir, for your kindness I owe you
a good turn.

speeches.speech: Call hither Barnardine and Claudio: [Exeunt POMPEY and ABHORSON] The one has my pity; not a jot the other, Being a murderer, though he were my brother. [Enter CLAUDIO] Look, here's the warrant, Claudio, for thy death: 'Tis now dead midnight, and by eight to-morrow Thou must be made immortal. Where's Barnardine?
lines: Call hither Barnardine and Claudio:
[Exeunt POMPEY and ABHORSON]
The one has my pity; not a jot the other,
Being a murderer, though he were my brother.
[Enter CLAUDIO]
Look, here's the warrant, Claudio, for thy death:
'Tis now dead midnight, and by eight to-morrow
Thou must be made immortal. Where's Barnardine?

speeches.speech: As fast lock'd up in sleep as guiltless labour When it lies starkly in the traveller's bones: He will not wake.
lines: As fast lock'd up in sleep as guiltless labour
When it lies starkly in the traveller's bones:
He will not wake.

speeches.speech: Who can do good on him? Well, go, prepare yourself. [Knocking within] But, hark, what noise? Heaven give your spirits comfort! [Exit CLAUDIO] By and by. I hope it is some pardon or reprieve For the most gentle Claudio. [Enter DUKE VINCENTIO disguised as before] Welcome father.
lines: Who can do good on him?
Well, go, prepare yourself.
[Knocking within]
But, hark, what noise?
Heaven give your spirits comfort!
[Exit CLAUDIO]
By and by.
I hope it is some pardon or reprieve
For the most gentle Claudio.
[Enter DUKE VINCENTIO disguised as before]
Welcome father.

speeches.speech: The best and wholesomest spirts of the night Envelope you, good Provost! Who call'd here of late?
lines: The best and wholesomest spirts of the night
Envelope you, good Provost! Who call'd here of late?

speeches.speech: None, since the curfew rung.
lines: None, since the curfew rung.

speeches.speech: Not Isabel?
lines: Not Isabel?

speeches.speech: No.
lines: No.

speeches.speech: They will, then, ere't be long.
lines: They will, then, ere't be long.

speeches.speech: What comfort is for Claudio?
lines: What comfort is for Claudio?

speeches.speech: There's some in hope.
lines: There's some in hope.

speeches.speech: It is a bitter deputy.
lines: It is a bitter deputy.

speeches.speech: Not so, not so; his life is parallel'd Even with the stroke and line of his great justice: He doth with holy abstinence subdue That in himself which he spurs on his power To qualify in others: were he meal'd with that Which he corrects, then were he tyrannous; But this being so, he's just. [Knocking within] Now are they come. [Exit Provost] This is a gentle provost: seldom when The steeled gaoler is the friend of men. [Knocking within] How now! what noise? That spirit's possessed with haste That wounds the unsisting postern with these strokes.
lines: Not so, not so; his life is parallel'd
Even with the stroke and line of his great justice:
He doth with holy abstinence subdue
That in himself which he spurs on his power
To qualify in others: were he meal'd with that
Which he corrects, then were he tyrannous;
But this being so, he's just.
[Knocking within]
Now are they come.
[Exit Provost]
This is a gentle provost: seldom when
The steeled gaoler is the friend of men.
[Knocking within]
How now! what noise? That spirit's possessed with haste
That wounds the unsisting postern with these strokes.

speeches.speech: There he must stay until the officer Arise to let him in: he is call'd up.
lines: There he must stay until the officer
Arise to let him in: he is call'd up.

speeches.speech: Have you no countermand for Claudio yet, But he must die to-morrow?
lines: Have you no countermand for Claudio yet,
But he must die to-morrow?

speeches.speech: None, sir, none.
lines: None, sir, none.

speeches.speech: As near the dawning, provost, as it is, You shall hear more ere morning.
lines: As near the dawning, provost, as it is,
You shall hear more ere morning.

speeches.speech: Happily You something know; yet I believe there comes No countermand; no such example have we: Besides, upon the very siege of justice Lord Angelo hath to the public ear Profess'd the contrary. [Enter a Messenger] This is his lordship's man.
lines: Happily
You something know; yet I believe there comes
No countermand; no such example have we:
Besides, upon the very siege of justice
Lord Angelo hath to the public ear
Profess'd the contrary.
[Enter a Messenger]
This is his lordship's man.

speeches.speech: And here comes Claudio's pardon.
lines: And here comes Claudio's pardon.

speeches.speech: [Giving a paper] My lord hath sent you this note; and by me this further charge, that you swerve not from the smallest article of it, neither in time, matter, or other circumstance. Good morrow; for, as I take it, it is almost day.
lines: [Giving a paper]
My lord hath sent you this note; and by me this
further charge, that you swerve not from the
smallest article of it, neither in time, matter, or
other circumstance. Good morrow; for, as I take it,
it is almost day.

speeches.speech: I shall obey him.
lines: I shall obey him.

speeches.speech: [Aside] This is his pardon, purchased by such sin For which the pardoner himself is in. Hence hath offence his quick celerity, When it is born in high authority: When vice makes mercy, mercy's so extended, That for the fault's love is the offender friended. Now, sir, what news?
lines: [Aside] This is his pardon, purchased by such sin
For which the pardoner himself is in.
Hence hath offence his quick celerity,
When it is born in high authority:
When vice makes mercy, mercy's so extended,
That for the fault's love is the offender friended.
Now, sir, what news?

speeches.speech: I told you. Lord Angelo, belike thinking me remiss in mine office, awakens me with this unwonted putting-on; methinks strangely, for he hath not used it before.
lines: I told you. Lord Angelo, belike thinking me remiss
in mine office, awakens me with this unwonted
putting-on; methinks strangely, for he hath not used it before.

speeches.speech: Pray you, let's hear.
lines: Pray you, let's hear.

speeches.speech: [Reads] 'Whatsoever you may hear to the contrary, let Claudio be executed by four of the clock; and in the afternoon Barnardine: for my better satisfaction, let me have Claudio's head sent me by five. Let this be duly performed; with a thought that more depends on it than we must yet deliver. Thus fail not to do your office, as you will answer it at your peril.' What say you to this, sir?
lines: [Reads]
'Whatsoever you may hear to the contrary, let
Claudio be executed by four of the clock; and in the
afternoon Barnardine: for my better satisfaction,
let me have Claudio's head sent me by five. Let
this be duly performed; with a thought that more
depends on it than we must yet deliver. Thus fail
not to do your office, as you will answer it at your peril.'
What say you to this, sir?

speeches.speech: What is that Barnardine who is to be executed in the afternoon?
lines: What is that Barnardine who is to be executed in the
afternoon?

speeches.speech: A Bohemian born, but here nursed un and bred; one that is a prisoner nine years old.
lines: A Bohemian born, but here nursed un and bred; one
that is a prisoner nine years old.

speeches.speech: How came it that the absent duke had not either delivered him to his liberty or executed him? I have heard it was ever his manner to do so.
lines: How came it that the absent duke had not either
delivered him to his liberty or executed him? I
have heard it was ever his manner to do so.

speeches.speech: His friends still wrought reprieves for him: and, indeed, his fact, till now in the government of Lord Angelo, came not to an undoubtful proof.
lines: His friends still wrought reprieves for him: and,
indeed, his fact, till now in the government of Lord
Angelo, came not to an undoubtful proof.

speeches.speech: It is now apparent?
lines: It is now apparent?

speeches.speech: Most manifest, and not denied by himself.
lines: Most manifest, and not denied by himself.

speeches.speech: Hath he born himself penitently in prison? how seems he to be touched?
lines: Hath he born himself penitently in prison? how
seems he to be touched?

speeches.speech: A man that apprehends death no more dreadfully but as a drunken sleep; careless, reckless, and fearless of what's past, present, or to come; insensible of mortality, and desperately mortal.
lines: A man that apprehends death no more dreadfully but
as a drunken sleep; careless, reckless, and fearless
of what's past, present, or to come; insensible of
mortality, and desperately mortal.

speeches.speech: He wants advice.
lines: He wants advice.

speeches.speech: He will hear none: he hath evermore had the liberty of the prison; give him leave to escape hence, he would not: drunk many times a day, if not many days entirely drunk. We have very oft awaked him, as if to carry him to execution, and showed him a seeming warrant for it: it hath not moved him at all.
lines: He will hear none: he hath evermore had the liberty
of the prison; give him leave to escape hence, he
would not: drunk many times a day, if not many days
entirely drunk. We have very oft awaked him, as if
to carry him to execution, and showed him a seeming
warrant for it: it hath not moved him at all.

speeches.speech: More of him anon. There is written in your brow, provost, honesty and constancy: if I read it not truly, my ancient skill beguiles me; but, in the boldness of my cunning, I will lay myself in hazard. Claudio, whom here you have warrant to execute, is no greater forfeit to the law than Angelo who hath sentenced him. To make you understand this in a manifested effect, I crave but four days' respite; for the which you are to do me both a present and a dangerous courtesy.
lines: More of him anon. There is written in your brow,
provost, honesty and constancy: if I read it not
truly, my ancient skill beguiles me; but, in the
boldness of my cunning, I will lay myself in hazard.
Claudio, whom here you have warrant to execute, is
no greater forfeit to the law than Angelo who hath
sentenced him. To make you understand this in a
manifested effect, I crave but four days' respite;
for the which you are to do me both a present and a
dangerous courtesy.

speeches.speech: Pray, sir, in what?
lines: Pray, sir, in what?

speeches.speech: In the delaying death.
lines: In the delaying death.

speeches.speech: A lack, how may I do it, having the hour limited, and an express command, under penalty, to deliver his head in the view of Angelo? I may make my case as Claudio's, to cross this in the smallest.
lines: A lack, how may I do it, having the hour limited,
and an express command, under penalty, to deliver
his head in the view of Angelo? I may make my case
as Claudio's, to cross this in the smallest.

speeches.speech: By the vow of mine order I warrant you, if my instructions may be your guide. Let this Barnardine be this morning executed, and his head born to Angelo.
lines: By the vow of mine order I warrant you, if my
instructions may be your guide. Let this Barnardine
be this morning executed, and his head born to Angelo.

speeches.speech: Angelo hath seen them both, and will discover the favour.
lines: Angelo hath seen them both, and will discover the favour.

speeches.speech: O, death's a great disguiser; and you may add to it. Shave the head, and tie the beard; and say it was the desire of the penitent to be so bared before his death: you know the course is common. If any thing fall to you upon this, more than thanks and good fortune, by the saint whom I profess, I will plead against it with my life.
lines: O, death's a great disguiser; and you may add to it.
Shave the head, and tie the beard; and say it was
the desire of the penitent to be so bared before his
death: you know the course is common. If any thing
fall to you upon this, more than thanks and good
fortune, by the saint whom I profess, I will plead
against it with my life.

speeches.speech: Pardon me, good father; it is against my oath.
lines: Pardon me, good father; it is against my oath.

speeches.speech: Were you sworn to the duke, or to the deputy?
lines: Were you sworn to the duke, or to the deputy?

speeches.speech: To him, and to his substitutes.
lines: To him, and to his substitutes.

speeches.speech: You will think you have made no offence, if the duke avouch the justice of your dealing?
lines: You will think you have made no offence, if the duke
avouch the justice of your dealing?

speeches.speech: But what likelihood is in that?
lines: But what likelihood is in that?

speeches.speech: Not a resemblance, but a certainty. Yet since I see you fearful, that neither my coat, integrity, nor persuasion can with ease attempt you, I will go further than I meant, to pluck all fears out of you. Look you, sir, here is the hand and seal of the duke: you know the character, I doubt not; and the signet is not strange to you.
lines: Not a resemblance, but a certainty. Yet since I see
you fearful, that neither my coat, integrity, nor
persuasion can with ease attempt you, I will go
further than I meant, to pluck all fears out of you.
Look you, sir, here is the hand and seal of the
duke: you know the character, I doubt not; and the
signet is not strange to you.

speeches.speech: I know them both.
lines: I know them both.

speeches.speech: The contents of this is the return of the duke: you shall anon over-read it at your pleasure; where you shall find, within these two days he will be here. This is a thing that Angelo knows not; for he this very day receives letters of strange tenor; perchance of the duke's death; perchance entering into some monastery; but, by chance, nothing of what is writ. Look, the unfolding star calls up the shepherd. Put not yourself into amazement how these things should be: all difficulties are but easy when they are known. Call your executioner, and off with Barnardine's head: I will give him a present shrift and advise him for a better place. Yet you are amazed; but this shall absolutely resolve you. Come away; it is almost clear dawn.
lines: The contents of this is the return of the duke: you
shall anon over-read it at your pleasure; where you
shall find, within these two days he will be here.
This is a thing that Angelo knows not; for he this
very day receives letters of strange tenor;
perchance of the duke's death; perchance entering
into some monastery; but, by chance, nothing of what
is writ. Look, the unfolding star calls up the
shepherd. Put not yourself into amazement how these
things should be: all difficulties are but easy
when they are known. Call your executioner, and off
with Barnardine's head: I will give him a present
shrift and advise him for a better place. Yet you
are amazed; but this shall absolutely resolve you.
Come away; it is almost clear dawn.

speeches.speech: I am as well acquainted here as I was in our house of profession: one would think it were Mistress Overdone's own house, for here be many of her old customers. First, here's young Master Rash; he's in for a commodity of brown paper and old ginger, ninescore and seventeen pounds; of which he made five marks, ready money: marry, then ginger was not much in request, for the old women were all dead. Then is there here one Master Caper, at the suit of Master Three-pile the mercer, for some four suits of peach-coloured satin, which now peaches him a beggar. Then have we here young Dizy, and young Master Deep-vow, and Master Copperspur, and Master Starve-lackey the rapier and dagger man, and young Drop-heir that killed lusty Pudding, and Master Forthlight the tilter, and brave Master Shooty the great traveller, and wild Half-can that stabbed Pots, and, I think, forty more; all great doers in our trade, and are now 'for the Lord's sake.'
lines: I am as well acquainted here as I was in our house
of profession: one would think it were Mistress
Overdone's own house, for here be many of her old
customers. First, here's young Master Rash; he's in
for a commodity of brown paper and old ginger,
ninescore and seventeen pounds; of which he made
five marks, ready money: marry, then ginger was not
much in request, for the old women were all dead.
Then is there here one Master Caper, at the suit of
Master Three-pile the mercer, for some four suits of
peach-coloured satin, which now peaches him a
beggar. Then have we here young Dizy, and young
Master Deep-vow, and Master Copperspur, and Master
Starve-lackey the rapier and dagger man, and young
Drop-heir that killed lusty Pudding, and Master
Forthlight the tilter, and brave Master Shooty the
great traveller, and wild Half-can that stabbed
Pots, and, I think, forty more; all great doers in
our trade, and are now 'for the Lord's sake.'

speeches.speech: Sirrah, bring Barnardine hither.
lines: Sirrah, bring Barnardine hither.

speeches.speech: Master Barnardine! you must rise and be hanged. Master Barnardine!
lines: Master Barnardine! you must rise and be hanged.
Master Barnardine!

speeches.speech: What, ho, Barnardine!
lines: What, ho, Barnardine!

speeches.speech: [Within] A pox o' your throats! Who makes that noise there? What are you?
lines: [Within] A pox o' your throats! Who makes that
noise there? What are you?

speeches.speech: Your friends, sir; the hangman. You must be so good, sir, to rise and be put to death.
lines: Your friends, sir; the hangman. You must be so
good, sir, to rise and be put to death.

speeches.speech: [Within] Away, you rogue, away! I am sleepy.
lines: [Within] Away, you rogue, away! I am sleepy.

speeches.speech: Tell him he must awake, and that quickly too.
lines: Tell him he must awake, and that quickly too.

speeches.speech: Pray, Master Barnardine, awake till you are executed, and sleep afterwards.
lines: Pray, Master Barnardine, awake till you are
executed, and sleep afterwards.

speeches.speech: Go in to him, and fetch him out.
lines: Go in to him, and fetch him out.

speeches.speech: He is coming, sir, he is coming; I hear his straw rustle.
lines: He is coming, sir, he is coming; I hear his straw rustle.

speeches.speech: Is the axe upon the block, sirrah?
lines: Is the axe upon the block, sirrah?

speeches.speech: Very ready, sir.
lines: Very ready, sir.

speeches.speech: How now, Abhorson? what's the news with you?
lines: How now, Abhorson? what's the news with you?

speeches.speech: Truly, sir, I would desire you to clap into your prayers; for, look you, the warrant's come.
lines: Truly, sir, I would desire you to clap into your
prayers; for, look you, the warrant's come.

speeches.speech: You rogue, I have been drinking all night; I am not fitted for 't.
lines: You rogue, I have been drinking all night; I am not
fitted for 't.

speeches.speech: O, the better, sir; for he that drinks all night, and is hanged betimes in the morning, may sleep the sounder all the next day.
lines: O, the better, sir; for he that drinks all night,
and is hanged betimes in the morning, may sleep the
sounder all the next day.

speeches.speech: Look you, sir; here comes your ghostly father: do we jest now, think you?
lines: Look you, sir; here comes your ghostly father: do
we jest now, think you?

speeches.speech: Sir, induced by my charity, and hearing how hastily you are to depart, I am come to advise you, comfort you and pray with you.
lines: Sir, induced by my charity, and hearing how hastily
you are to depart, I am come to advise you, comfort
you and pray with you.

speeches.speech: Friar, not I. I have been drinking hard all night, and I will have more time to prepare me, or they shall beat out my brains with billets: I will not consent to die this day, that's certain.
lines: Friar, not I. I have been drinking hard all night,
and I will have more time to prepare me, or they
shall beat out my brains with billets: I will not
consent to die this day, that's certain.

speeches.speech: O, sir, you must: and therefore I beseech you Look forward on the journey you shall go.
lines: O, sir, you must: and therefore I beseech you
Look forward on the journey you shall go.

speeches.speech: I swear I will not die to-day for any man's persuasion.
lines: I swear I will not die to-day for any man's
persuasion.

speeches.speech: But hear you.
lines: But hear you.

speeches.speech: Not a word: if you have any thing to say to me, come to my ward; for thence will not I to-day.
lines: Not a word: if you have any thing to say to me,
come to my ward; for thence will not I to-day.

speeches.speech: Unfit to live or die: O gravel heart! After him, fellows; bring him to the block.
lines: Unfit to live or die: O gravel heart!
After him, fellows; bring him to the block.

speeches.speech: Now, sir, how do you find the prisoner?
lines: Now, sir, how do you find the prisoner?

speeches.speech: A creature unprepared, unmeet for death; And to transport him in the mind he is Were damnable.
lines: A creature unprepared, unmeet for death;
And to transport him in the mind he is
Were damnable.

speeches.speech: Here in the prison, father, There died this morning of a cruel fever One Ragozine, a most notorious pirate, A man of Claudio's years; his beard and head Just of his colour. What if we do omit This reprobate till he were well inclined; And satisfy the deputy with the visage Of Ragozine, more like to Claudio?
lines: Here in the prison, father,
There died this morning of a cruel fever
One Ragozine, a most notorious pirate,
A man of Claudio's years; his beard and head
Just of his colour. What if we do omit
This reprobate till he were well inclined;
And satisfy the deputy with the visage
Of Ragozine, more like to Claudio?

speeches.speech: O, 'tis an accident that heaven provides! Dispatch it presently; the hour draws on Prefix'd by Angelo: see this be done, And sent according to command; whiles I Persuade this rude wretch willingly to die.
lines: O, 'tis an accident that heaven provides!
Dispatch it presently; the hour draws on
Prefix'd by Angelo: see this be done,
And sent according to command; whiles I
Persuade this rude wretch willingly to die.

speeches.speech: This shall be done, good father, presently. But Barnardine must die this afternoon: And how shall we continue Claudio, To save me from the danger that might come If he were known alive?
lines: This shall be done, good father, presently.
But Barnardine must die this afternoon:
And how shall we continue Claudio,
To save me from the danger that might come
If he were known alive?

speeches.speech: Let this be done. Put them in secret holds, both Barnardine and Claudio: Ere twice the sun hath made his journal greeting To the under generation, you shall find Your safety manifested.
lines: Let this be done.
Put them in secret holds, both Barnardine and Claudio:
Ere twice the sun hath made his journal greeting
To the under generation, you shall find
Your safety manifested.

speeches.speech: I am your free dependant.
lines: I am your free dependant.

speeches.speech: Quick, dispatch, and send the head to Angelo. [Exit Provost] Now will I write letters to Angelo,-- The provost, he shall bear them, whose contents Shall witness to him I am near at home, And that, by great injunctions, I am bound To enter publicly: him I'll desire To meet me at the consecrated fount A league below the city; and from thence, By cold gradation and well-balanced form, We shall proceed with Angelo.
lines: Quick, dispatch, and send the head to Angelo.
[Exit Provost]
Now will I write letters to Angelo,--
The provost, he shall bear them, whose contents
Shall witness to him I am near at home,
And that, by great injunctions, I am bound
To enter publicly: him I'll desire
To meet me at the consecrated fount
A league below the city; and from thence,
By cold gradation and well-balanced form,
We shall proceed with Angelo.

speeches.speech: Here is the head; I'll carry it myself.
lines: Here is the head; I'll carry it myself.

speeches.speech: Convenient is it. Make a swift return; For I would commune with you of such things That want no ear but yours.
lines: Convenient is it. Make a swift return;
For I would commune with you of such things
That want no ear but yours.

speeches.speech: I'll make all speed.
lines: I'll make all speed.

speeches.speech: [Within] Peace, ho, be here!
lines: [Within] Peace, ho, be here!

speeches.speech: The tongue of Isabel. She's come to know If yet her brother's pardon be come hither: But I will keep her ignorant of her good, To make her heavenly comforts of despair, When it is least expected.
lines: The tongue of Isabel. She's come to know
If yet her brother's pardon be come hither:
But I will keep her ignorant of her good,
To make her heavenly comforts of despair,
When it is least expected.

speeches.speech: Ho, by your leave!
lines: Ho, by your leave!

speeches.speech: Good morning to you, fair and gracious daughter.
lines: Good morning to you, fair and gracious daughter.

speeches.speech: The better, given me by so holy a man. Hath yet the deputy sent my brother's pardon?
lines: The better, given me by so holy a man.
Hath yet the deputy sent my brother's pardon?

speeches.speech: He hath released him, Isabel, from the world: His head is off and sent to Angelo.
lines: He hath released him, Isabel, from the world:
His head is off and sent to Angelo.

speeches.speech: Nay, but it is not so.
lines: Nay, but it is not so.

speeches.speech: It is no other: show your wisdom, daughter, In your close patience.
lines: It is no other: show your wisdom, daughter,
In your close patience.

speeches.speech: O, I will to him and pluck out his eyes!
lines: O, I will to him and pluck out his eyes!

speeches.speech: You shall not be admitted to his sight.
lines: You shall not be admitted to his sight.

speeches.speech: Unhappy Claudio! wretched Isabel! Injurious world! most damned Angelo!
lines: Unhappy Claudio! wretched Isabel!
Injurious world! most damned Angelo!

speeches.speech: This nor hurts him nor profits you a jot; Forbear it therefore; give your cause to heaven. Mark what I say, which you shall find By every syllable a faithful verity: The duke comes home to-morrow; nay, dry your eyes; One of our convent, and his confessor, Gives me this instance: already he hath carried Notice to Escalus and Angelo, Who do prepare to meet him at the gates, There to give up their power. If you can, pace your wisdom In that good path that I would wish it go, And you shall have your bosom on this wretch, Grace of the duke, revenges to your heart, And general honour.
lines: This nor hurts him nor profits you a jot;
Forbear it therefore; give your cause to heaven.
Mark what I say, which you shall find
By every syllable a faithful verity:
The duke comes home to-morrow; nay, dry your eyes;
One of our convent, and his confessor,
Gives me this instance: already he hath carried
Notice to Escalus and Angelo,
Who do prepare to meet him at the gates,
There to give up their power. If you can, pace your wisdom
In that good path that I would wish it go,
And you shall have your bosom on this wretch,
Grace of the duke, revenges to your heart,
And general honour.

speeches.speech: I am directed by you.
lines: I am directed by you.

speeches.speech: This letter, then, to Friar Peter give; 'Tis that he sent me of the duke's return: Say, by this token, I desire his company At Mariana's house to-night. Her cause and yours I'll perfect him withal, and he shall bring you Before the duke, and to the head of Angelo Accuse him home and home. For my poor self, I am combined by a sacred vow And shall be absent. Wend you with this letter: Command these fretting waters from your eyes With a light heart; trust not my holy order, If I pervert your course. Who's here?
lines: This letter, then, to Friar Peter give;
'Tis that he sent me of the duke's return:
Say, by this token, I desire his company
At Mariana's house to-night. Her cause and yours
I'll perfect him withal, and he shall bring you
Before the duke, and to the head of Angelo
Accuse him home and home. For my poor self,
I am combined by a sacred vow
And shall be absent. Wend you with this letter:
Command these fretting waters from your eyes
With a light heart; trust not my holy order,
If I pervert your course. Who's here?

speeches.speech: Good even. Friar, where's the provost?
lines: Good even. Friar, where's the provost?

speeches.speech: Not within, sir.
lines: Not within, sir.

speeches.speech: O pretty Isabella, I am pale at mine heart to see thine eyes so red: thou must be patient. I am fain to dine and sup with water and bran; I dare not for my head fill my belly; one fruitful meal would set me to 't. But they say the duke will be here to-morrow. By my troth, Isabel, I loved thy brother: if the old fantastical duke of dark corners had been at home, he had lived.
lines: O pretty Isabella, I am pale at mine heart to see
thine eyes so red: thou must be patient. I am fain
to dine and sup with water and bran; I dare not for
my head fill my belly; one fruitful meal would set
me to 't. But they say the duke will be here
to-morrow. By my troth, Isabel, I loved thy brother:
if the old fantastical duke of dark corners had been
at home, he had lived.

speeches.speech: Sir, the duke is marvellous little beholding to your reports; but the best is, he lives not in them.
lines: Sir, the duke is marvellous little beholding to your
reports; but the best is, he lives not in them.

speeches.speech: Friar, thou knowest not the duke so well as I do: he's a better woodman than thou takest him for.
lines: Friar, thou knowest not the duke so well as I do:
he's a better woodman than thou takest him for.

speeches.speech: Well, you'll answer this one day. Fare ye well.
lines: Well, you'll answer this one day. Fare ye well.

speeches.speech: Nay, tarry; I'll go along with thee I can tell thee pretty tales of the duke.
lines: Nay, tarry; I'll go along with thee
I can tell thee pretty tales of the duke.

speeches.speech: You have told me too many of him already, sir, if they be true; if not true, none were enough.
lines: You have told me too many of him already, sir, if
they be true; if not true, none were enough.

speeches.speech: I was once before him for getting a wench with child.
lines: I was once before him for getting a wench with child.

speeches.speech: Did you such a thing?
lines: Did you such a thing?

speeches.speech: Yes, marry, did I. but I was fain to forswear it; they would else have married me to the rotten medlar.
lines: Yes, marry, did I. but I was fain to forswear it;
they would else have married me to the rotten medlar.

speeches.speech: Sir, your company is fairer than honest. Rest you well.
lines: Sir, your company is fairer than honest. Rest you well.

speeches.speech: By my troth, I'll go with thee to the lane's end: if bawdy talk offend you, we'll have very little of it. Nay, friar, I am a kind of burr; I shall stick.
lines: By my troth, I'll go with thee to the lane's end:
if bawdy talk offend you, we'll have very little of
it. Nay, friar, I am a kind of burr; I shall stick.

speeches.speech: Every letter he hath writ hath disvouched other.
lines: Every letter he hath writ hath disvouched other.

speeches.speech: In most uneven and distracted manner. His actions show much like to madness: pray heaven his wisdom be not tainted! And why meet him at the gates, and redeliver our authorities there
lines: In most uneven and distracted manner. His actions
show much like to madness: pray heaven his wisdom be
not tainted! And why meet him at the gates, and
redeliver our authorities there

speeches.speech: I guess not.
lines: I guess not.

speeches.speech: And why should we proclaim it in an hour before his entering, that if any crave redress of injustice, they should exhibit their petitions in the street?
lines: And why should we proclaim it in an hour before his
entering, that if any crave redress of injustice,
they should exhibit their petitions in the street?

speeches.speech: He shows his reason for that: to have a dispatch of complaints, and to deliver us from devices hereafter, which shall then have no power to stand against us.
lines: He shows his reason for that: to have a dispatch of
complaints, and to deliver us from devices
hereafter, which shall then have no power to stand
against us.

speeches.speech: Well, I beseech you, let it be proclaimed betimes i' the morn; I'll call you at your house: give notice to such men of sort and suit as are to meet him.
lines: Well, I beseech you, let it be proclaimed betimes
i' the morn; I'll call you at your house: give
notice to such men of sort and suit as are to meet
him.

speeches.speech: I shall, sir. Fare you well.
lines: I shall, sir. Fare you well.

speeches.speech: Good night. [Exit ESCALUS] This deed unshapes me quite, makes me unpregnant And dull to all proceedings. A deflower'd maid! And by an eminent body that enforced The law against it! But that her tender shame Will not proclaim against her maiden loss, How might she tongue me! Yet reason dares her no; For my authority bears of a credent bulk, That no particular scandal once can touch But it confounds the breather. He should have lived, Save that riotous youth, with dangerous sense, Might in the times to come have ta'en revenge, By so receiving a dishonour'd life With ransom of such shame. Would yet he had lived! A lack, when once our grace we have forgot, Nothing goes right: we would, and we would not.
lines: Good night.
[Exit ESCALUS]
This deed unshapes me quite, makes me unpregnant
And dull to all proceedings. A deflower'd maid!
And by an eminent body that enforced
The law against it! But that her tender shame
Will not proclaim against her maiden loss,
How might she tongue me! Yet reason dares her no;
For my authority bears of a credent bulk,
That no particular scandal once can touch
But it confounds the breather. He should have lived,
Save that riotous youth, with dangerous sense,
Might in the times to come have ta'en revenge,
By so receiving a dishonour'd life
With ransom of such shame. Would yet he had lived!
A lack, when once our grace we have forgot,
Nothing goes right: we would, and we would not.

speeches.speech: These letters at fit time deliver me [Giving letters] The provost knows our purpose and our plot. The matter being afoot, keep your instruction, And hold you ever to our special drift; Though sometimes you do blench from this to that, As cause doth minister. Go call at Flavius' house, And tell him where I stay: give the like notice To Valentinus, Rowland, and to Crassus, And bid them bring the trumpets to the gate; But send me Flavius first.
lines: These letters at fit time deliver me
[Giving letters]
The provost knows our purpose and our plot.
The matter being afoot, keep your instruction,
And hold you ever to our special drift;
Though sometimes you do blench from this to that,
As cause doth minister. Go call at Flavius' house,
And tell him where I stay: give the like notice
To Valentinus, Rowland, and to Crassus,
And bid them bring the trumpets to the gate;
But send me Flavius first.

speeches.speech: It shall be speeded well.
lines: It shall be speeded well.

speeches.speech: I thank thee, Varrius; thou hast made good haste: Come, we will walk. There's other of our friends Will greet us here anon, my gentle Varrius.
lines: I thank thee, Varrius; thou hast made good haste:
Come, we will walk. There's other of our friends
Will greet us here anon, my gentle Varrius.

speeches.speech: To speak so indirectly I am loath: I would say the truth; but to accuse him so, That is your part: yet I am advised to do it; He says, to veil full purpose.
lines: To speak so indirectly I am loath:
I would say the truth; but to accuse him so,
That is your part: yet I am advised to do it;
He says, to veil full purpose.

speeches.speech: Be ruled by him.
lines: Be ruled by him.

speeches.speech: Besides, he tells me that, if peradventure He speak against me on the adverse side, I should not think it strange; for 'tis a physic That's bitter to sweet end.
lines: Besides, he tells me that, if peradventure
He speak against me on the adverse side,
I should not think it strange; for 'tis a physic
That's bitter to sweet end.

speeches.speech: I would Friar Peter--
lines: I would Friar Peter--

speeches.speech: O, peace! the friar is come.
lines: O, peace! the friar is come.

speeches.speech: Come, I have found you out a stand most fit, Where you may have such vantage on the duke, He shall not pass you. Twice have the trumpets sounded; The generous and gravest citizens Have hent the gates, and very near upon The duke is entering: therefore, hence, away!
lines: Come, I have found you out a stand most fit,
Where you may have such vantage on the duke,
He shall not pass you. Twice have the trumpets sounded;
The generous and gravest citizens
Have hent the gates, and very near upon
The duke is entering: therefore, hence, away!

speeches.speech: My very worthy cousin, fairly met! Our old and faithful friend, we are glad to see you.
lines: My very worthy cousin, fairly met!
Our old and faithful friend, we are glad to see you.

speeches.speech: [with Escalus] Happy return be to your royal grace!
lines: [with Escalus] Happy return be to your royal grace!

speeches.speech: Many and hearty thankings to you both. We have made inquiry of you; and we hear Such goodness of your justice, that our soul Cannot but yield you forth to public thanks, Forerunning more requital.
lines: Many and hearty thankings to you both.
We have made inquiry of you; and we hear
Such goodness of your justice, that our soul
Cannot but yield you forth to public thanks,
Forerunning more requital.

speeches.speech: You make my bonds still greater.
lines: You make my bonds still greater.

speeches.speech: O, your desert speaks loud; and I should wrong it, To lock it in the wards of covert bosom, When it deserves, with characters of brass, A forted residence 'gainst the tooth of time And razure of oblivion. Give me your hand, And let the subject see, to make them know That outward courtesies would fain proclaim Favours that keep within. Come, Escalus, You must walk by us on our other hand; And good supporters are you.
lines: O, your desert speaks loud; and I should wrong it,
To lock it in the wards of covert bosom,
When it deserves, with characters of brass,
A forted residence 'gainst the tooth of time
And razure of oblivion. Give me your hand,
And let the subject see, to make them know
That outward courtesies would fain proclaim
Favours that keep within. Come, Escalus,
You must walk by us on our other hand;
And good supporters are you.

speeches.speech: Now is your time: speak loud and kneel before him.
lines: Now is your time: speak loud and kneel before him.

speeches.speech: Justice, O royal duke! Vail your regard Upon a wrong'd, I would fain have said, a maid! O worthy prince, dishonour not your eye By throwing it on any other object Till you have heard me in my true complaint And given me justice, justice, justice, justice!
lines: Justice, O royal duke! Vail your regard
Upon a wrong'd, I would fain have said, a maid!
O worthy prince, dishonour not your eye
By throwing it on any other object
Till you have heard me in my true complaint
And given me justice, justice, justice, justice!

speeches.speech: Relate your wrongs; in what? by whom? be brief. Here is Lord Angelo shall give you justice: Reveal yourself to him.
lines: Relate your wrongs; in what? by whom? be brief.
Here is Lord Angelo shall give you justice:
Reveal yourself to him.

speeches.speech: O worthy duke, You bid me seek redemption of the devil: Hear me yourself; for that which I must speak Must either punish me, not being believed, Or wring redress from you. Hear me, O hear me, here!
lines: O worthy duke,
You bid me seek redemption of the devil:
Hear me yourself; for that which I must speak
Must either punish me, not being believed,
Or wring redress from you. Hear me, O hear me, here!

speeches.speech: My lord, her wits, I fear me, are not firm: She hath been a suitor to me for her brother Cut off by course of justice,--
lines: My lord, her wits, I fear me, are not firm:
She hath been a suitor to me for her brother
Cut off by course of justice,--

speeches.speech: By course of justice!
lines: By course of justice!

speeches.speech: And she will speak most bitterly and strange.
lines: And she will speak most bitterly and strange.

speeches.speech: Most strange, but yet most truly, will I speak: That Angelo's forsworn; is it not strange? That Angelo's a murderer; is 't not strange? That Angelo is an adulterous thief, An hypocrite, a virgin-violator; Is it not strange and strange?
lines: Most strange, but yet most truly, will I speak:
That Angelo's forsworn; is it not strange?
That Angelo's a murderer; is 't not strange?
That Angelo is an adulterous thief,
An hypocrite, a virgin-violator;
Is it not strange and strange?

speeches.speech: Nay, it is ten times strange.
lines: Nay, it is ten times strange.

speeches.speech: It is not truer he is Angelo Than this is all as true as it is strange: Nay, it is ten times true; for truth is truth To the end of reckoning.
lines: It is not truer he is Angelo
Than this is all as true as it is strange:
Nay, it is ten times true; for truth is truth
To the end of reckoning.

speeches.speech: Away with her! Poor soul, She speaks this in the infirmity of sense.
lines: Away with her! Poor soul,
She speaks this in the infirmity of sense.

speeches.speech: O prince, I conjure thee, as thou believest There is another comfort than this world, That thou neglect me not, with that opinion That I am touch'd with madness! Make not impossible That which but seems unlike: 'tis not impossible But one, the wicked'st caitiff on the ground, May seem as shy, as grave, as just, as absolute As Angelo; even so may Angelo, In all his dressings, characts, titles, forms, Be an arch-villain; believe it, royal prince: If he be less, he's nothing; but he's more, Had I more name for badness.
lines: O prince, I conjure thee, as thou believest
There is another comfort than this world,
That thou neglect me not, with that opinion
That I am touch'd with madness! Make not impossible
That which but seems unlike: 'tis not impossible
But one, the wicked'st caitiff on the ground,
May seem as shy, as grave, as just, as absolute
As Angelo; even so may Angelo,
In all his dressings, characts, titles, forms,
Be an arch-villain; believe it, royal prince:
If he be less, he's nothing; but he's more,
Had I more name for badness.

speeches.speech: By mine honesty, If she be mad,--as I believe no other,-- Her madness hath the oddest frame of sense, Such a dependency of thing on thing, As e'er I heard in madness.
lines: By mine honesty,
If she be mad,--as I believe no other,--
Her madness hath the oddest frame of sense,
Such a dependency of thing on thing,
As e'er I heard in madness.

speeches.speech: O gracious duke, Harp not on that, nor do not banish reason For inequality; but let your reason serve To make the truth appear where it seems hid, And hide the false seems true.
lines: O gracious duke,
Harp not on that, nor do not banish reason
For inequality; but let your reason serve
To make the truth appear where it seems hid,
And hide the false seems true.

speeches.speech: Many that are not mad Have, sure, more lack of reason. What would you say?
lines: Many that are not mad
Have, sure, more lack of reason. What would you say?

speeches.speech: I am the sister of one Claudio, Condemn'd upon the act of fornication To lose his head; condemn'd by Angelo: I, in probation of a sisterhood, Was sent to by my brother; one Lucio As then the messenger,--
lines: I am the sister of one Claudio,
Condemn'd upon the act of fornication
To lose his head; condemn'd by Angelo:
I, in probation of a sisterhood,
Was sent to by my brother; one Lucio
As then the messenger,--

speeches.speech: That's I, an't like your grace: I came to her from Claudio, and desired her To try her gracious fortune with Lord Angelo For her poor brother's pardon.
lines: That's I, an't like your grace:
I came to her from Claudio, and desired her
To try her gracious fortune with Lord Angelo
For her poor brother's pardon.

speeches.speech: That's he indeed.
lines: That's he indeed.

speeches.speech: You were not bid to speak.
lines: You were not bid to speak.

speeches.speech: No, my good lord; Nor wish'd to hold my peace.
lines: No, my good lord;
Nor wish'd to hold my peace.

speeches.speech: I wish you now, then; Pray you, take note of it: and when you have A business for yourself, pray heaven you then Be perfect.
lines: I wish you now, then;
Pray you, take note of it: and when you have
A business for yourself, pray heaven you then
Be perfect.

speeches.speech: I warrant your honour.
lines: I warrant your honour.

speeches.speech: The warrants for yourself; take heed to't.
lines: The warrants for yourself; take heed to't.

speeches.speech: This gentleman told somewhat of my tale,--
lines: This gentleman told somewhat of my tale,--

speeches.speech: Right.
lines: Right.

speeches.speech: It may be right; but you are i' the wrong To speak before your time. Proceed.
lines: It may be right; but you are i' the wrong
To speak before your time. Proceed.

speeches.speech: I went To this pernicious caitiff deputy,--
lines: I went
To this pernicious caitiff deputy,--

speeches.speech: That's somewhat madly spoken.
lines: That's somewhat madly spoken.

speeches.speech: Pardon it; The phrase is to the matter.
lines: Pardon it;
The phrase is to the matter.

speeches.speech: Mended again. The matter; proceed.
lines: Mended again. The matter; proceed.

speeches.speech: In brief, to set the needless process by, How I persuaded, how I pray'd, and kneel'd, How he refell'd me, and how I replied,-- For this was of much length,--the vile conclusion I now begin with grief and shame to utter: He would not, but by gift of my chaste body To his concupiscible intemperate lust, Release my brother; and, after much debatement, My sisterly remorse confutes mine honour, And I did yield to him: but the next morn betimes, His purpose surfeiting, he sends a warrant For my poor brother's head.
lines: In brief, to set the needless process by,
How I persuaded, how I pray'd, and kneel'd,
How he refell'd me, and how I replied,--
For this was of much length,--the vile conclusion
I now begin with grief and shame to utter:
He would not, but by gift of my chaste body
To his concupiscible intemperate lust,
Release my brother; and, after much debatement,
My sisterly remorse confutes mine honour,
And I did yield to him: but the next morn betimes,
His purpose surfeiting, he sends a warrant
For my poor brother's head.

speeches.speech: This is most likely!
lines: This is most likely!

speeches.speech: O, that it were as like as it is true!
lines: O, that it were as like as it is true!

speeches.speech: By heaven, fond wretch, thou knowist not what thou speak'st, Or else thou art suborn'd against his honour In hateful practise. First, his integrity Stands without blemish. Next, it imports no reason That with such vehemency he should pursue Faults proper to himself: if he had so offended, He would have weigh'd thy brother by himself And not have cut him off. Some one hath set you on: Confess the truth, and say by whose advice Thou camest here to complain.
lines: By heaven, fond wretch, thou knowist not what thou speak'st,
Or else thou art suborn'd against his honour
In hateful practise. First, his integrity
Stands without blemish. Next, it imports no reason
That with such vehemency he should pursue
Faults proper to himself: if he had so offended,
He would have weigh'd thy brother by himself
And not have cut him off. Some one hath set you on:
Confess the truth, and say by whose advice
Thou camest here to complain.

speeches.speech: And is this all? Then, O you blessed ministers above, Keep me in patience, and with ripen'd time Unfold the evil which is here wrapt up In countenance! Heaven shield your grace from woe, As I, thus wrong'd, hence unbelieved go!
lines: And is this all?
Then, O you blessed ministers above,
Keep me in patience, and with ripen'd time
Unfold the evil which is here wrapt up
In countenance! Heaven shield your grace from woe,
As I, thus wrong'd, hence unbelieved go!

speeches.speech: I know you'ld fain be gone. An officer! To prison with her! Shall we thus permit A blasting and a scandalous breath to fall On him so near us? This needs must be a practise. Who knew of Your intent and coming hither?
lines: I know you'ld fain be gone. An officer!
To prison with her! Shall we thus permit
A blasting and a scandalous breath to fall
On him so near us? This needs must be a practise.
Who knew of Your intent and coming hither?

speeches.speech: One that I would were here, Friar Lodowick.
lines: One that I would were here, Friar Lodowick.

speeches.speech: A ghostly father, belike. Who knows that Lodowick?
lines: A ghostly father, belike. Who knows that Lodowick?

speeches.speech: My lord, I know him; 'tis a meddling friar; I do not like the man: had he been lay, my lord For certain words he spake against your grace In your retirement, I had swinged him soundly.
lines: My lord, I know him; 'tis a meddling friar;
I do not like the man: had he been lay, my lord
For certain words he spake against your grace
In your retirement, I had swinged him soundly.

speeches.speech: Words against me? this is a good friar, belike! And to set on this wretched woman here Against our substitute! Let this friar be found.
lines: Words against me? this is a good friar, belike!
And to set on this wretched woman here
Against our substitute! Let this friar be found.

speeches.speech: But yesternight, my lord, she and that friar, I saw them at the prison: a saucy friar, A very scurvy fellow.
lines: But yesternight, my lord, she and that friar,
I saw them at the prison: a saucy friar,
A very scurvy fellow.

speeches.speech: Blessed be your royal grace! I have stood by, my lord, and I have heard Your royal ear abused. First, hath this woman Most wrongfully accused your substitute, Who is as free from touch or soil with her As she from one ungot.
lines: Blessed be your royal grace!
I have stood by, my lord, and I have heard
Your royal ear abused. First, hath this woman
Most wrongfully accused your substitute,
Who is as free from touch or soil with her
As she from one ungot.

speeches.speech: We did believe no less. Know you that Friar Lodowick that she speaks of?
lines: We did believe no less.
Know you that Friar Lodowick that she speaks of?

speeches.speech: I know him for a man divine and holy; Not scurvy, nor a temporary meddler, As he's reported by this gentleman; And, on my trust, a man that never yet Did, as he vouches, misreport your grace.
lines: I know him for a man divine and holy;
Not scurvy, nor a temporary meddler,
As he's reported by this gentleman;
And, on my trust, a man that never yet
Did, as he vouches, misreport your grace.

speeches.speech: My lord, most villanously; believe it.
lines: My lord, most villanously; believe it.

speeches.speech: Well, he in time may come to clear himself; But at this instant he is sick my lord, Of a strange fever. Upon his mere request, Being come to knowledge that there was complaint Intended 'gainst Lord Angelo, came I hither, To speak, as from his mouth, what he doth know Is true and false; and what he with his oath And all probation will make up full clear, Whensoever he's convented. First, for this woman. To justify this worthy nobleman, So vulgarly and personally accused, Her shall you hear disproved to her eyes, Till she herself confess it.
lines: Well, he in time may come to clear himself;
But at this instant he is sick my lord,
Of a strange fever. Upon his mere request,
Being come to knowledge that there was complaint
Intended 'gainst Lord Angelo, came I hither,
To speak, as from his mouth, what he doth know
Is true and false; and what he with his oath
And all probation will make up full clear,
Whensoever he's convented. First, for this woman.
To justify this worthy nobleman,
So vulgarly and personally accused,
Her shall you hear disproved to her eyes,
Till she herself confess it.

speeches.speech: Good friar, let's hear it. [ISABELLA is carried off guarded; and MARIANA comes forward] Do you not smile at this, Lord Angelo? O heaven, the vanity of wretched fools! Give us some seats. Come, cousin Angelo; In this I'll be impartial; be you judge Of your own cause. Is this the witness, friar? First, let her show her face, and after speak.
lines: Good friar, let's hear it.
[ISABELLA is carried off guarded; and MARIANA comes forward]
Do you not smile at this, Lord Angelo?
O heaven, the vanity of wretched fools!
Give us some seats. Come, cousin Angelo;
In this I'll be impartial; be you judge
Of your own cause. Is this the witness, friar?
First, let her show her face, and after speak.

speeches.speech: Pardon, my lord; I will not show my face Until my husband bid me.
lines: Pardon, my lord; I will not show my face
Until my husband bid me.

speeches.speech: What, are you married?
lines: What, are you married?

speeches.speech: No, my lord.
lines: No, my lord.

speeches.speech: Are you a maid?
lines: Are you a maid?

speeches.speech: No, my lord.
lines: No, my lord.

speeches.speech: A widow, then?
lines: A widow, then?

speeches.speech: Neither, my lord.
lines: Neither, my lord.

speeches.speech: Why, you are nothing then: neither maid, widow, nor wife?
lines: Why, you are nothing then: neither maid, widow, nor wife?

speeches.speech: My lord, she may be a punk; for many of them are neither maid, widow, nor wife.
lines: My lord, she may be a punk; for many of them are
neither maid, widow, nor wife.

speeches.speech: Silence that fellow: I would he had some cause To prattle for himself.
lines: Silence that fellow: I would he had some cause
To prattle for himself.

speeches.speech: Well, my lord.
lines: Well, my lord.

speeches.speech: My lord; I do confess I ne'er was married; And I confess besides I am no maid: I have known my husband; yet my husband Knows not that ever he knew me.
lines: My lord; I do confess I ne'er was married;
And I confess besides I am no maid:
I have known my husband; yet my husband
Knows not that ever he knew me.

speeches.speech: He was drunk then, my lord: it can be no better.
lines: He was drunk then, my lord: it can be no better.

speeches.speech: For the benefit of silence, would thou wert so too!
lines: For the benefit of silence, would thou wert so too!

speeches.speech: Well, my lord.
lines: Well, my lord.

speeches.speech: This is no witness for Lord Angelo.
lines: This is no witness for Lord Angelo.

speeches.speech: Now I come to't my lord She that accuses him of fornication, In self-same manner doth accuse my husband, And charges him my lord, with such a time When I'll depose I had him in mine arms With all the effect of love.
lines: Now I come to't my lord
She that accuses him of fornication,
In self-same manner doth accuse my husband,
And charges him my lord, with such a time
When I'll depose I had him in mine arms
With all the effect of love.

speeches.speech: Charges she more than me?
lines: Charges she more than me?

speeches.speech: Not that I know.
lines: Not that I know.

speeches.speech: No? you say your husband.
lines: No? you say your husband.

speeches.speech: Why, just, my lord, and that is Angelo, Who thinks he knows that he ne'er knew my body, But knows he thinks that he knows Isabel's.
lines: Why, just, my lord, and that is Angelo,
Who thinks he knows that he ne'er knew my body,
But knows he thinks that he knows Isabel's.

speeches.speech: This is a strange abuse. Let's see thy face.
lines: This is a strange abuse. Let's see thy face.

speeches.speech: My husband bids me; now I will unmask. [Unveiling] This is that face, thou cruel Angelo, Which once thou sworest was worth the looking on; This is the hand which, with a vow'd contract, Was fast belock'd in thine; this is the body That took away the match from Isabel, And did supply thee at thy garden-house In her imagined person.
lines: My husband bids me; now I will unmask.
[Unveiling]
This is that face, thou cruel Angelo,
Which once thou sworest was worth the looking on;
This is the hand which, with a vow'd contract,
Was fast belock'd in thine; this is the body
That took away the match from Isabel,
And did supply thee at thy garden-house
In her imagined person.

speeches.speech: Know you this woman?
lines: Know you this woman?

speeches.speech: Carnally, she says.
lines: Carnally, she says.

speeches.speech: Sirrah, no more!
lines: Sirrah, no more!

speeches.speech: Enough, my lord.
lines: Enough, my lord.

speeches.speech: My lord, I must confess I know this woman: And five years since there was some speech of marriage Betwixt myself and her; which was broke off, Partly for that her promised proportions Came short of composition, but in chief For that her reputation was disvalued In levity: since which time of five years I never spake with her, saw her, nor heard from her, Upon my faith and honour.
lines: My lord, I must confess I know this woman:
And five years since there was some speech of marriage
Betwixt myself and her; which was broke off,
Partly for that her promised proportions
Came short of composition, but in chief
For that her reputation was disvalued
In levity: since which time of five years
I never spake with her, saw her, nor heard from her,
Upon my faith and honour.

speeches.speech: Noble prince, As there comes light from heaven and words from breath, As there is sense in truth and truth in virtue, I am affianced this man's wife as strongly As words could make up vows: and, my good lord, But Tuesday night last gone in's garden-house He knew me as a wife. As this is true, Let me in safety raise me from my knees Or else for ever be confixed here, A marble monument!
lines: Noble prince,
As there comes light from heaven and words from breath,
As there is sense in truth and truth in virtue,
I am affianced this man's wife as strongly
As words could make up vows: and, my good lord,
But Tuesday night last gone in's garden-house
He knew me as a wife. As this is true,
Let me in safety raise me from my knees
Or else for ever be confixed here,
A marble monument!

speeches.speech: I did but smile till now: Now, good my lord, give me the scope of justice My patience here is touch'd. I do perceive These poor informal women are no more But instruments of some more mightier member That sets them on: let me have way, my lord, To find this practise out.
lines: I did but smile till now:
Now, good my lord, give me the scope of justice
My patience here is touch'd. I do perceive
These poor informal women are no more
But instruments of some more mightier member
That sets them on: let me have way, my lord,
To find this practise out.

speeches.speech: Ay, with my heart And punish them to your height of pleasure. Thou foolish friar, and thou pernicious woman, Compact with her that's gone, think'st thou thy oaths, Though they would swear down each particular saint, Were testimonies against his worth and credit That's seal'd in approbation? You, Lord Escalus, Sit with my cousin; lend him your kind pains To find out this abuse, whence 'tis derived. There is another friar that set them on; Let him be sent for.
lines: Ay, with my heart
And punish them to your height of pleasure.
Thou foolish friar, and thou pernicious woman,
Compact with her that's gone, think'st thou thy oaths,
Though they would swear down each particular saint,
Were testimonies against his worth and credit
That's seal'd in approbation? You, Lord Escalus,
Sit with my cousin; lend him your kind pains
To find out this abuse, whence 'tis derived.
There is another friar that set them on;
Let him be sent for.

speeches.speech: Would he were here, my lord! for he indeed Hath set the women on to this complaint: Your provost knows the place where he abides And he may fetch him.
lines: Would he were here, my lord! for he indeed
Hath set the women on to this complaint:
Your provost knows the place where he abides
And he may fetch him.

speeches.speech: Go do it instantly. [Exit Provost] And you, my noble and well-warranted cousin, Whom it concerns to hear this matter forth, Do with your injuries as seems you best, In any chastisement: I for a while will leave you; But stir not you till you have well determined Upon these slanderers.
lines: Go do it instantly.
[Exit Provost]
And you, my noble and well-warranted cousin,
Whom it concerns to hear this matter forth,
Do with your injuries as seems you best,
In any chastisement: I for a while will leave you;
But stir not you till you have well determined
Upon these slanderers.

speeches.speech: My lord, we'll do it throughly. [Exit DUKE] Signior Lucio, did not you say you knew that Friar Lodowick to be a dishonest person?
lines: My lord, we'll do it throughly.
[Exit DUKE]
Signior Lucio, did not you say you knew that
Friar Lodowick to be a dishonest person?

speeches.speech: 'Cucullus non facit monachum:' honest in nothing but in his clothes; and one that hath spoke most villanous speeches of the duke.
lines: 'Cucullus non facit monachum:' honest in nothing
but in his clothes; and one that hath spoke most
villanous speeches of the duke.

speeches.speech: We shall entreat you to abide here till he come and enforce them against him: we shall find this friar a notable fellow.
lines: We shall entreat you to abide here till he come and
enforce them against him: we shall find this friar a
notable fellow.

speeches.speech: As any in Vienna, on my word.
lines: As any in Vienna, on my word.

speeches.speech: Call that same Isabel here once again; I would speak with her. [Exit an Attendant] Pray you, my lord, give me leave to question; you shall see how I'll handle her.
lines: Call that same Isabel here once again; I would speak with her.
[Exit an Attendant]
Pray you, my lord, give me leave to question; you
shall see how I'll handle her.

speeches.speech: Not better than he, by her own report.
lines: Not better than he, by her own report.

speeches.speech: Say you?
lines: Say you?

speeches.speech: Marry, sir, I think, if you handled her privately, she would sooner confess: perchance, publicly, she'll be ashamed.
lines: Marry, sir, I think, if you handled her privately,
she would sooner confess: perchance, publicly,
she'll be ashamed.

speeches.speech: I will go darkly to work with her.
lines: I will go darkly to work with her.

speeches.speech: That's the way; for women are light at midnight. [Re-enter Officers with ISABELLA; and Provost with] the DUKE VINCENTIO in his friar's habit]
lines: That's the way; for women are light at midnight.
[Re-enter Officers with ISABELLA; and Provost with]
the DUKE VINCENTIO in his friar's habit]

speeches.speech: Come on, mistress: here's a gentlewoman denies all that you have said.
lines: Come on, mistress: here's a gentlewoman denies all
that you have said.

speeches.speech: My lord, here comes the rascal I spoke of; here with the provost.
lines: My lord, here comes the rascal I spoke of; here with
the provost.

speeches.speech: In very good time: speak not you to him till we call upon you.
lines: In very good time: speak not you to him till we
call upon you.

speeches.speech: Mum.
lines: Mum.

speeches.speech: Come, sir: did you set these women on to slander Lord Angelo? they have confessed you did.
lines: Come, sir: did you set these women on to slander
Lord Angelo? they have confessed you did.

speeches.speech: 'Tis false.
lines: 'Tis false.

speeches.speech: How! know you where you are?
lines: How! know you where you are?

speeches.speech: Respect to your great place! and let the devil Be sometime honour'd for his burning throne! Where is the duke? 'tis he should hear me speak.
lines: Respect to your great place! and let the devil
Be sometime honour'd for his burning throne!
Where is the duke? 'tis he should hear me speak.

speeches.speech: The duke's in us; and we will hear you speak: Look you speak justly.
lines: The duke's in us; and we will hear you speak:
Look you speak justly.

speeches.speech: Boldly, at least. But, O, poor souls, Come you to seek the lamb here of the fox? Good night to your redress! Is the duke gone? Then is your cause gone too. The duke's unjust, Thus to retort your manifest appeal, And put your trial in the villain's mouth Which here you come to accuse.
lines: Boldly, at least. But, O, poor souls,
Come you to seek the lamb here of the fox?
Good night to your redress! Is the duke gone?
Then is your cause gone too. The duke's unjust,
Thus to retort your manifest appeal,
And put your trial in the villain's mouth
Which here you come to accuse.

speeches.speech: This is the rascal; this is he I spoke of.
lines: This is the rascal; this is he I spoke of.

speeches.speech: Why, thou unreverend and unhallow'd friar, Is't not enough thou hast suborn'd these women To accuse this worthy man, but, in foul mouth And in the witness of his proper ear, To call him villain? and then to glance from him To the duke himself, to tax him with injustice? Take him hence; to the rack with him! We'll touse you Joint by joint, but we will know his purpose. What 'unjust'!
lines: Why, thou unreverend and unhallow'd friar,
Is't not enough thou hast suborn'd these women
To accuse this worthy man, but, in foul mouth
And in the witness of his proper ear,
To call him villain? and then to glance from him
To the duke himself, to tax him with injustice?
Take him hence; to the rack with him! We'll touse you
Joint by joint, but we will know his purpose.
What 'unjust'!

speeches.speech: Be not so hot; the duke Dare no more stretch this finger of mine than he Dare rack his own: his subject am I not, Nor here provincial. My business in this state Made me a looker on here in Vienna, Where I have seen corruption boil and bubble Till it o'er-run the stew; laws for all faults, But faults so countenanced, that the strong statutes Stand like the forfeits in a barber's shop, As much in mock as mark.
lines: Be not so hot; the duke
Dare no more stretch this finger of mine than he
Dare rack his own: his subject am I not,
Nor here provincial. My business in this state
Made me a looker on here in Vienna,
Where I have seen corruption boil and bubble
Till it o'er-run the stew; laws for all faults,
But faults so countenanced, that the strong statutes
Stand like the forfeits in a barber's shop,
As much in mock as mark.

speeches.speech: Slander to the state! Away with him to prison!
lines: Slander to the state! Away with him to prison!

speeches.speech: What can you vouch against him, Signior Lucio? Is this the man that you did tell us of?
lines: What can you vouch against him, Signior Lucio?
Is this the man that you did tell us of?

speeches.speech: 'Tis he, my lord. Come hither, goodman baldpate: do you know me?
lines: 'Tis he, my lord. Come hither, goodman baldpate:
do you know me?

speeches.speech: I remember you, sir, by the sound of your voice: I met you at the prison, in the absence of the duke.
lines: I remember you, sir, by the sound of your voice: I
met you at the prison, in the absence of the duke.

speeches.speech: O, did you so? And do you remember what you said of the duke?
lines: O, did you so? And do you remember what you said of the duke?

speeches.speech: Most notedly, sir.
lines: Most notedly, sir.

speeches.speech: Do you so, sir? And was the duke a fleshmonger, a fool, and a coward, as you then reported him to be?
lines: Do you so, sir? And was the duke a fleshmonger, a
fool, and a coward, as you then reported him to be?

speeches.speech: You must, sir, change persons with me, ere you make that my report: you, indeed, spoke so of him; and much more, much worse.
lines: You must, sir, change persons with me, ere you make
that my report: you, indeed, spoke so of him; and
much more, much worse.

speeches.speech: O thou damnable fellow! Did not I pluck thee by the nose for thy speeches?
lines: O thou damnable fellow! Did not I pluck thee by the
nose for thy speeches?

speeches.speech: I protest I love the duke as I love myself.
lines: I protest I love the duke as I love myself.

speeches.speech: Hark, how the villain would close now, after his treasonable abuses!
lines: Hark, how the villain would close now, after his
treasonable abuses!

speeches.speech: Such a fellow is not to be talked withal. Away with him to prison! Where is the provost? Away with him to prison! lay bolts enough upon him: let him speak no more. Away with those giglots too, and with the other confederate companion!
lines: Such a fellow is not to be talked withal. Away with
him to prison! Where is the provost? Away with him
to prison! lay bolts enough upon him: let him
speak no more. Away with those giglots too, and
with the other confederate companion!

speeches.speech: [To Provost] Stay, sir; stay awhile.
lines: [To Provost] Stay, sir; stay awhile.

speeches.speech: What, resists he? Help him, Lucio.
lines: What, resists he? Help him, Lucio.

speeches.speech: Come, sir; come, sir; come, sir; foh, sir! Why, you bald-pated, lying rascal, you must be hooded, must you? Show your knave's visage, with a pox to you! show your sheep-biting face, and be hanged an hour! Will't not off?
lines: Come, sir; come, sir; come, sir; foh, sir! Why, you
bald-pated, lying rascal, you must be hooded, must
you? Show your knave's visage, with a pox to you!
show your sheep-biting face, and be hanged an hour!
Will't not off?

speeches.speech: Thou art the first knave that e'er madest a duke. First, provost, let me bail these gentle three. [To LUCIO] Sneak not away, sir; for the friar and you Must have a word anon. Lay hold on him.
lines: Thou art the first knave that e'er madest a duke.
First, provost, let me bail these gentle three.
[To LUCIO]
Sneak not away, sir; for the friar and you
Must have a word anon. Lay hold on him.

speeches.speech: This may prove worse than hanging.
lines: This may prove worse than hanging.

speeches.speech: [To ESCALUS] What you have spoke I pardon: sit you down: We'll borrow place of him. [To ANGELO] Sir, by your leave. Hast thou or word, or wit, or impudence, That yet can do thee office? If thou hast, Rely upon it till my tale be heard, And hold no longer out.
lines: [To ESCALUS] What you have spoke I pardon: sit you down:
We'll borrow place of him.
[To ANGELO]
Sir, by your leave.
Hast thou or word, or wit, or impudence,
That yet can do thee office? If thou hast,
Rely upon it till my tale be heard,
And hold no longer out.

speeches.speech: O my dread lord, I should be guiltier than my guiltiness, To think I can be undiscernible, When I perceive your grace, like power divine,. Hath look'd upon my passes. Then, good prince, No longer session hold upon my shame, But let my trial be mine own confession: Immediate sentence then and sequent death Is all the grace I beg.
lines: O my dread lord,
I should be guiltier than my guiltiness,
To think I can be undiscernible,
When I perceive your grace, like power divine,. Hath look'd upon my passes. Then, good prince,
No longer session hold upon my shame,
But let my trial be mine own confession:
Immediate sentence then and sequent death
Is all the grace I beg.

speeches.speech: Come hither, Mariana. Say, wast thou e'er contracted to this woman?
lines: Come hither, Mariana.
Say, wast thou e'er contracted to this woman?

speeches.speech: I was, my lord.
lines: I was, my lord.

speeches.speech: Go take her hence, and marry her instantly. Do you the office, friar; which consummate, Return him here again. Go with him, provost.
lines: Go take her hence, and marry her instantly.
Do you the office, friar; which consummate,
Return him here again. Go with him, provost.

speeches.speech: My lord, I am more amazed at his dishonour Than at the strangeness of it.
lines: My lord, I am more amazed at his dishonour
Than at the strangeness of it.

speeches.speech: Come hither, Isabel. Your friar is now your prince: as I was then Advertising and holy to your business, Not changing heart with habit, I am still Attorney'd at your service.
lines: Come hither, Isabel.
Your friar is now your prince: as I was then
Advertising and holy to your business,
Not changing heart with habit, I am still
Attorney'd at your service.

speeches.speech: O, give me pardon, That I, your vassal, have employ'd and pain'd Your unknown sovereignty!
lines: O, give me pardon,
That I, your vassal, have employ'd and pain'd
Your unknown sovereignty!

speeches.speech: You are pardon'd, Isabel: And now, dear maid, be you as free to us. Your brother's death, I know, sits at your heart; And you may marvel why I obscured myself, Labouring to save his life, and would not rather Make rash remonstrance of my hidden power Than let him so be lost. O most kind maid, It was the swift celerity of his death, Which I did think with slower foot came on, That brain'd my purpose. But, peace be with him! That life is better life, past fearing death, Than that which lives to fear: make it your comfort, So happy is your brother.
lines: You are pardon'd, Isabel:
And now, dear maid, be you as free to us.
Your brother's death, I know, sits at your heart;
And you may marvel why I obscured myself,
Labouring to save his life, and would not rather
Make rash remonstrance of my hidden power
Than let him so be lost. O most kind maid,
It was the swift celerity of his death,
Which I did think with slower foot came on,
That brain'd my purpose. But, peace be with him!
That life is better life, past fearing death,
Than that which lives to fear: make it your comfort,
So happy is your brother.

speeches.speech: I do, my lord.
lines: I do, my lord.

speeches.speech: For this new-married man approaching here, Whose salt imagination yet hath wrong'd Your well defended honour, you must pardon For Mariana's sake: but as he adjudged your brother,-- Being criminal, in double violation Of sacred chastity and of promise-breach Thereon dependent, for your brother's life,-- The very mercy of the law cries out Most audible, even from his proper tongue, 'An Angelo for Claudio, death for death!' Haste still pays haste, and leisure answers leisure; Like doth quit like, and MEASURE still FOR MEASURE. Then, Angelo, thy fault's thus manifested; Which, though thou wouldst deny, denies thee vantage. We do condemn thee to the very block Where Claudio stoop'd to death, and with like haste. Away with him!
lines: For this new-married man approaching here,
Whose salt imagination yet hath wrong'd
Your well defended honour, you must pardon
For Mariana's sake: but as he adjudged your brother,--
Being criminal, in double violation
Of sacred chastity and of promise-breach
Thereon dependent, for your brother's life,--
The very mercy of the law cries out
Most audible, even from his proper tongue,
'An Angelo for Claudio, death for death!'
Haste still pays haste, and leisure answers leisure;
Like doth quit like, and MEASURE still FOR MEASURE.
Then, Angelo, thy fault's thus manifested;
Which, though thou wouldst deny, denies thee vantage.
We do condemn thee to the very block
Where Claudio stoop'd to death, and with like haste.
Away with him!

speeches.speech: O my most gracious lord, I hope you will not mock me with a husband.
lines: O my most gracious lord,
I hope you will not mock me with a husband.

speeches.speech: It is your husband mock'd you with a husband. Consenting to the safeguard of your honour, I thought your marriage fit; else imputation, For that he knew you, might reproach your life And choke your good to come; for his possessions, Although by confiscation they are ours, We do instate and widow you withal, To buy you a better husband.
lines: It is your husband mock'd you with a husband.
Consenting to the safeguard of your honour,
I thought your marriage fit; else imputation,
For that he knew you, might reproach your life
And choke your good to come; for his possessions,
Although by confiscation they are ours,
We do instate and widow you withal,
To buy you a better husband.

speeches.speech: O my dear lord, I crave no other, nor no better man.
lines: O my dear lord,
I crave no other, nor no better man.

speeches.speech: Never crave him; we are definitive.
lines: Never crave him; we are definitive.

speeches.speech: Gentle my liege,--
lines: Gentle my liege,--

speeches.speech: You do but lose your labour. Away with him to death! [To LUCIO] Now, sir, to you.
lines: You do but lose your labour.
Away with him to death!
[To LUCIO]
Now, sir, to you.

speeches.speech: O my good lord! Sweet Isabel, take my part; Lend me your knees, and all my life to come I'll lend you all my life to do you service.
lines: O my good lord! Sweet Isabel, take my part;
Lend me your knees, and all my life to come
I'll lend you all my life to do you service.

speeches.speech: Against all sense you do importune her: Should she kneel down in mercy of this fact, Her brother's ghost his paved bed would break, And take her hence in horror.
